essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Understand the structure and properties of atoms and molecules and their role in matter.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Bonds
Molecular formula
Tetrahedron
Lattice structures
Diamond
Graphite
Lubricant
Demonstrate they understand that atoms make up matter all around us.
Compare atomic particles in the nucleus and those that orbit the nucleus.
Show how atoms can be combined to make molecules.
Account for the difference that structure makes in properties of graphite and diamond.
Explain how the number of electrons and protons in an atom contributes to its charge.
Distinguish between 2d and 3d structures (water vs. methane)
Calculate charge on an atom.
Make models to reflect molecular formula
Classify atomic particles
Research natural product medicines originating from molecules
Communicate research findings
Investigate structures using online simulations and viewers
Foster a habit of inquiry into the atomic composition of simple molecules and extended structures.
Demonstrate resourcefulness as they use everyday materials to develop models.
Work collaboratively with group members and other members of the class to produce the models, make presentations, etc.
Develop a sense of stewardship towards the environment and respect for living organisms affected by energy choices.
Participate in a class discussion of their ideas and opinions related to atomic composition.
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning
Analyze structures to ensure faithfulness to the design.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Predicting Shape: 1. Using a model, predict what shape NCl3 will take. 2. Using a model, predict what shape CCl4 will take.

Teacher Download 3d Viewer: In order to reinforce with Learners, the 3 dimensional nature of molecules download the free Rasmol®/Raswin program here: http://www.openrasmol.org/ It is a molecular viewer that allows you to rotate molecules in real time using your mouse. In the >display< menu choose >ball & stick<. If you visit the following website you can download common molecular structures to view in Raswin by choosing >download RAW data < in the upper right. PDB files can be found here: https://github.com/wesbarnett/OPLS-molecules/tree/master/pdb

Writing About Molecules: Choose a natural medicine made from a local plant. Include in a two page essay: 1) A picture of the molecule (3 marks) 2) What is the medicine used for (3 marks) 3) What natural product does the medicine come from? (3 marks) 4) Where is the natural product grown in the world? (3 marks)
inclusive_learning_strategies: molecules. We know they are very small, so small, we can’t see them easily so …we are going to build models to represent them. First, let’s learn about the smallest building block, the atom. Learners listen “The Atom Song” https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oFLH_0B4ZLg (2:43 mins). The following question card will be distributed to each group. They are to watch the video carefully to determine the correct answers. When the song is over the teacher will review the answers by asking each team to report. The answers will be recorded on the board so everyone can learn the facts.

POSSIBLE QUESTION CARDS: 1. When were atoms discovered? 2. What do atoms make up? 3. Can you see an atom? 4. What are the three parts of an atom? 5. Which part of the atom has a positive charge and resides in the nucleus? 6. Which part of the atom has a negative charge and orbits the nucleus? Answers: 1. 1803 2. Atoms make up all things, 3. No because it is really small: 4. Protons, Neutrons and electrons: 5. Protons: 6. Electrons

Reinforcing the Vocabulary: View with Learners a second video to review the parts of the atom and to emphasize and explain the terminology: Retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=R1RMV5qhwyE (5:52 mins). Atom-small building block of everything! Molecule- we put atoms together to make molecules (like words make a sentence). Nucleus- in the centre of the atom and contains positive and neutral particles. Protons-positive particles in the nucleus. Neutrons- neutral particles in the nucleus. Electrons – negatively charged particles that circle the nucleus in different orbits. After the video, have Learners draw a simple picture of an atom.

Retrieved from: https://www.vectorstock.com/royalty-free-vector/simple-model-of-atom-structure-with-electrons-vector-21958597. Learners can build an atom using the PHET simulation here: https://phet.colorado.edu/sims/html/build-an-atom/latest/build-an-atom_all.html. As a guide they should answer the following questions: a. Where is the only place the simulation will allow you to add protons? (nucleus) b. Where is the only place the simulation will allow you to add neutrons? (nucleus) c. What is the charge on the atom when the number of electrons is one more than protons? (-1) d. What is the charge on the atom when the number of electrons is one less than the number of protons? (+1) e. How can the atom assume a neutral charge? (equal protons and electrons)

What is an Element? Tell Learners that when they hear the term element it refers to a substance that contains only one type of atom. For instance, if I said you had a bucket of the element gold, you would know it contained only gold atoms which have unique numbers of protons, neutrons and electrons.

Atoms Combine to Form Molecules! Explain to Learners that atoms can be combined in many different ways to make new substances called molecules. The atoms are joined together by something called bonds. Just like you have a bond or connection to your brother, sister, mother or father, atoms are bonded together to form molecules.

Building Models: Now Learners, we are going to use marshmallows (atoms) and toothpicks (bonds) to make some simple molecules.” The number of atoms in a molecule is called its molecular formula. E.g. the molecule hydrogen peroxide H₂O₂ has 2 hydrogen atoms and 2 oxygen atoms-note: we don’t usually write “1” in our formulae so CO is carbon monoxide and it has 1 carbon and 1 oxygen atom. Teacher Notes: a. You could also coffee sticks and plasticine balls if available. b. Choose marshmallows or plasticine of different colors for different types of atoms. The teacher will now distribute marshmallows and toothpicks so that children can build the molecules along with the teacher as the teacher demonstrates and poses questions.

The children will use a sheet of paper to create the table below and fill in drawings as they build their molecules. It is important that the teacher lead Learners in creating these molecules by: i. first telling them how many atoms of each they have to work with and ii. getting them to guess how they might be situated. Molecule: Hydrogen (H₂), Oxygen (O₂), Water (H₂O), Ammonia (NH₃), Methane (CH₄). Actual models will appear as below.

Teacher Note: It is important that you point out that molecules aren’t necessarily flat. E.g. The CH₄ molecule is not flat; bonds go out from the carbon atom in such a way that the 4 hydrogen atoms are as far apart as possible (a tetrahedron). This activity and photo were retrieved from: https://betterlesson.com/lesson/resource/3181798/marshmallow-molecules-directions-and-student-worksheet

Teacher note: If you have access to technology you may want to build models on screen or have Learners access the simulation at home for practice. See: https://phet.colorado.edu/sims/html/build-a-molecule/latest/build-a-molecule_all.html
Models of Extended Structures: Some molecules have different types of bonding that allows them to form what are called lattice structures. Two such examples are NaCl and diamond.

The teacher may divide the class up to make a model of each for the classroom using possible materials from the list below. Materials: skewers/sticks/straws, colored Styrofoam balls/marshmallows, hot glue gun (optional), NaCl Sodium chloride (table salt)
Diamond is a lattice of carbon atoms each in a tetrahedron shape
Because the carbon is bonded in so many directions, it makes diamond one of the hardest things on earth!

Graphite is another carbon molecule with an unusual structure. Based on this structure, why do you think it is in effective lubricant? The carbon layers are only weakly attracted and so the flat layers can easily roll over each other. This makes graphite a good choice for lubricating such things as locks. Its flexibility in one direction and strength in another makes it useful for making handles and shafts for sports items. (e.g. golf clubs and field hockey sticks)

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Gather and make sense of information to describe that synthetic materials come from natural resources and impact society

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Learners are expected to define the terms: Natural resources, Natural products, Synthetic materials, Chemical process, Impact on society
Identify a production process to produce synthetics, taking place in the home or industry.
Explain how polymers are formed.
Describe the different types of natural resources) and provide examples of how each could be used in the production of synthetic materials.
Classify synthetic materials into categories (e.g., plastics, synthetic fibres, medicines, alternative fuels) and discuss their specific applications in daily life.
Understand that human production processes make goods and products which may impact the environment.
Compare and contrast the properties of natural products and synthetic materials (e.g., durability, biodegradability, cost of production, environmental impact).

Discuss the role of chemistry in society, specifically how advancements in chemical processes have led to the development of new materials with significant societal benefits (e.g., medical advancements, diet alternatives, food safety)).
Explore the relationship between resource scarcity and the need for synthetic alternatives (e.g., the use of synthetic rubber during wartime shortages of natural rubber).
Understand that technological processes include manufacturing and constructing that may have an impact on their lives.
Understand the environmental implications of both the production and disposal of synthetic materials (e.g., pollution from plastic waste, energy use in manufacturing processes).
Identify careers related to the development and management of synthetic materials (e.g., chemical engineering, environmental science, materials science, waste management).
Investigate future trends in synthetic material development, including sustainable alternatives (e.g., bioplastics) and their potential to reduce environmental impact.
Differentiate between natural resources, natural products, and synthetic materials by analysing their origins and processes of transformation.
Identify examples of common synthetic materials and explain the natural resources from which they are derived.
Analyse the positive and negative impacts of synthetic materials on society, including economic, environmental, and health effects.
Research and present findings on a synthetic material’s impact on society, showing the connection between natural resources and synthetic products.
Undertake simple calculations to project impact of synthetic materials on cost of living, disposal, landfill and environment. (e.g. diapers)
Critically evaluate the societal implications of synthetic material production, such as plastic pollution or advancements in medicine.
Appreciate the importance of natural resources in the production of synthetic materials, recognizing the balance needed between resource use and environmental sustainability.
Appreciate that people use production processes to change materials to satisfy their needs.
Develop environmental awareness by reflecting on the environmental consequences of synthetic material use and promoting practices that minimize harm.
Respect scientific innovation by understanding how synthetic materials have contributed to societal advancements, while also considering ethical implications.
Value collaboration and open-mindedness when engaging in group projects and discussions, listening to and considering diverse perspectives.
Cultivate critical thinking about the use of synthetic materials in everyday life and the potential future challenges and opportunities they present.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Think-Pair-Share: Use a worksheet with a prompt asking learners to describe a natural material and a synthetic material they have used or encountered, with a word bank (Natural resource, fuel, petroleum, medicine, environment, bauxite, energy, plastic, material).

Why Have Synthetics Become Important? Have Learners research examples and write a response paper addressing: 1) Why was synthetic rubber necessary during world wars? 2) How was penicillin discovered/produced at the industrial level as a valuable antibiotic? 3) How can plastics be useful in the design and production of prosthetic devices? Rubric: Logical argument 2 marks, Flow of ideas 3, Grammar & punctuation 5 marks, Adequate sources of information 3 marks.
Exit Tickets: At the end of each lesson, ask Learners to write down one thing they learned and one question they still have about the topic.
Group Projects: Assign small groups to research a synthetic material and its impact on society.
Using the above graphic organizer, small student groups will create a poster, a short essay and a visual presentation (either poster or PowerPoint®) to communicate to their peers their findings.
Scoring Rubric

Natural Remedy versus Synthetic Drug Learners should create a chart to compare the pros and cons of natural medicines versus synthesized drugs. Based on the chart, in several paragraphs, they should make a case for whether one or the other is a better choice for them as a consumer nesting their choice in a real example of an ailment requiring treatment.

Do the Math on Diapers! A child in diapers for the first 2.5 years, will use, on average between 6,000 and 7,000 disposable diapers. Disposable diapers are the third largest contributors to landfills in the world. But they are also costly. Have Learners calculate what the diapers would cost in total above if they average $0.25 per diaper. (0.25 × 6500 = $1625.00)
Learners should calculate how many diapers will be used by 30 Learners in their first 2.5 years of life if all parents are using disposable diapers for their children. (30 × 6500 = 195,000).
inclusive_learning_strategies: Posing a Big Question: How many of you have ever made a product at home from raw materials or seen someone make an item? We know that some things occur naturally in nature while other things are man made from different starting materials. We often call the products made by humans, synthetic. A good example might be the clothes we wear. They can contain cotton which is a natural fibre which can be spun into thread to make clothes (see: https://sewport.com/fabrics-directory/cotton-fabric). If we check the tags on our clothes, we will often see a synthetic fibre called polyester which is made from hydrocarbon compounds. (see: https://sewport.com/fabrics-directory/polyester-fabric). I want you to work in groups and suggest at least two natural products at home and community and two synthetic products you are aware of. (grass for weaving, milk, eggs, nuts, tubers, silk, jute for rope/plastic rope, plastic containers, clothing, food products, foam insulation, synthetic cups). Let us get more practice at recognizing the natural versus the synthetic in our surroundings. Look at the following table. I think you will see that many natural products can sometimes be converted to synthetic products.

I bet you thought that polymer clay was made from real clay but that isn’t the case. It is actually manmade from compounds made from hydrogen and carbon (called hydrocarbons) but it has many properties similar to clay! (see: https://www.sculpey.com/blogs/blog/10-surprising-facts-about-polymer-clay).

Let us review some of the key definitions so we are able to better distinguish materials around us. •Natural resources are resources that exist in nature not created by humankind. •Natural products are substances available in nature that can be isolated and purified. •Synthetic materials are new substances made in a laboratory using readily available starting materials that may be natural in

Synthetic materials can be made by chemically changing the starting materials to create a material with different characteristics. (Paraphrased from: https://www.middleschoolchemistry.com/lessonplans/chapter6/lesson12)

Impacts: Making and using synthetic materials can have both positive and negative impacts on society. Let us think about how a synthetic material has impacted us. In groups I want you to discuss and arrive at two examples of the positive effects of synthetic plastics and two examples of negative impacts of synthetic plastics. (useful plastic products: containers, bags, protective coverings, waterproof clothing, toys, electronics) (negative impacts: disposal of plastic (landfill sites) water pollution, reliance on oil as a starting material, lack of recycling, dangerous fumes on burning)

What About Natural Resources? Learners, we have already referred to hydrocarbons as the starting materials for making many synthetic products, We will talk more about that later but you should know these hydrocarbons largely come from oil, a natural resource. For a very long time we have used the burning of wood, coal and oil for cooking and heating. If we also use oil for making useful synthetic materials, we may run out of this natural resource. This is an argument for developing more alternate energies like solar, tidal and wind power. For homework, I want you to write a one-page response paper that outlines the pros and cons of using oil for energy and synthetic materials.
Debating the Utility of Synthetic Materials: Learners engage in a debate on the benefits and drawbacks of synthetic materials in society.

One group can argue from the perspective of environmentalists, while another argues from the industry’s point of view. Have Learners watch the following video on Natural Resources to help them understand the definition. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qw6uXh9yM54 (5:16 mins)

Oil and Making Synthetics: The oil that we find in the ground largely arises from compressing plant life for 1000’s of years. This oil contains many molecules made up from carbon and hydrogen atoms as a backbone. These molecules are called hydrocarbons, and they can be refined to extract important building blocks for creating more complicated and useful synthetic molecules. One example of a useful hydrocarbon is ethylene purified from petroleum natural gas. It can be combined with many other ethylene molecules in a chemical reaction to make a larger synthetic molecule called polyethylene. Because ethylene is considered the building block or unit, we call it a monomer where “mono” means one. When we combine it with other ethylene molecules, we create polyethylene, which is referred to as a polymer where “poly” means many units.
Ethylene monomer (C2H4) Polyethylene is called a polymer. Polyethylene is a common chemical often associated with the plastics industry.
This pie chart shows the uses of polyethylene. You can see from the pie chart that low density polyethylene is used primarily for plastic bags and plastic coverings
Retrieved from: https://zpolymer.com/products/polyethylene/ldpc/

Video Resource: Making Plastics https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IwdUwffecsM (9:06 mins) As Learners watch the video they should consider answering the following questions: a) What are the raw materials for making plastics? b) What are the properties of plastics that make them useful? c) What are the many uses of plastics? d) What environmental concerns do you think plastics pose?
There are many more polymers made from natural hydrocarbon monomers. Have Learners for an overnight assignment look up the uses of “polyester” and “polypropylene” and “polyvinylchloride”.

Making a Polymer Plastic from Milk: Classroom Demonstration by the Teacher and/or an experiment for Learners if resources are available. See instructional video here for materials and process. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QulxqE4XY9M (5:06 mins). Materials needed: milk, vinegar, sieve, paper towel, hotplate or microwave. Instructions: 1. Heat 1 cup of 3% homogenised milk on a hot plate or in the microwave until it bubbles. 2. Add the hot milk to 1 tbsp (15 mL) of vinegar in a large bowl. 3. Pour the curdled mixture into a sieve lined with paper towels. 4. Allow the white solid to dry in the sieve for ~30 mins. Squeeze the solid in a ball between paper towels to further remove water. 5. Flatten out the dried solid and press it with a cookie cutter. 6. Place the pressed-out shape in the window for 24 hours to harden. You have demonstrated with children that the plastics can be bent and shaped. You have made a natural polymer plastic from a protein found in milk called Casein. The combination of heat and vinegar (acetic acid) helps to pull out or precipitate the protein casein from the milk. Plastic is now synthesized from petroleum products but before this way of making plastic, it was common to make natural plastics from plants and animal fat.

Making a Polymer from White Glue (teacher demonstration): In a small glass jar, combine 2 tablespoons of Borax cleaning powder and 10mL of water/stir. In another small jar, place 2 tablespoons of white carpenter’s glue. Pour the Borax slurry/solution into the jar of glue and stir with a wooden coffee stick. As you stir the glue will change consistency from a liquid to a rubbery polymer. Decant the liquid and rinse off the rubbery glob with water. Dry it off and pass around to children to see the new polymer.

Useful Polymers Have a Cost (teacher demonstration): Disposable diapers contain a synthetic polymer called sodium polyacrylate. It is a polymer that is able to trap up to 30 times its weight in water. Over a newspaper on the floor, a teacher can open the cotton in the middle of the diaper and, running it through their hands, they will see small sand-like pebbles of the polymer fall on the newspaper. Gathering the powder up in a jar, the teacher can add ml volumes of water and with stir note the powder forming a gel. The capacity of the powder/gel to absorb water will amaze the Learners. While this technology has been an enormous convenience for parents, this synthetic invention is said to take 500 years to biodegrade in a landfill site!

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Analyse and interpret data on the properties of substances before and after the substances interact to determine if a chemical reaction has occurred.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: Water vapor, Phase or state change, Physical change, Chemical change, Starting materials, Products, Reactants, Acrid smell, Closed system, Waft, Endothermic reaction, Law of Conservation of Mass, Collisions, Precipitate, Hard and soft water, Soap scum, Exothermic reaction, Combustion
Demonstrate they understand the difference between physical and chemical changes by giving examples of each.
Demonstrate they understand the range of indicators of chemical change by giving examples of each.
Explain how a burning splint can be used to assess which of the following gases are being released in a chemical reaction.
Demonstrate they understand that when a chemical reaction has occurred, new substances (products) are formed that have different properties than the original substances (reactants).
Demonstrate they understand that when two chemical compounds react, the atoms are rearranged to
form new products.
Differentiate between endothermic and exothermic chemical reaction.
Infer what will happen when certain substances react.
Hypothesize the changes that would take place after reactants are mixed.
Represent data collected from chemical reactions in the form of a graph or table.
Make careful observations of demonstrations and experiments.
Exercise the wafting technique.
Analyze and interpret observations to make conclusions about indicators of chemical change
Apply basic principles/indicators of chemical change to real life occurrences.
Classify endo and exothermic processes.
Measure temperature changes.
Assemble instrumentation to carry out an experiment.
Make careful volume and mass measurements of reactants
Communicate experimental/demonstration results by keeping good records in student notebooks.
Tabulate data
Construct graphs
Interpret graphs
Identify the products and reactants in a chemical reaction.
Write word equations to represent a chemical reaction
Appreciate the importance of physical and chemical changes in our everyday life.
Demonstrate persistence when conducting experiments on physical and chemical changes.
Show interest/curiosity in the activities and presentations on physical and chemical changes.
Use the inquiry approach when conducting scientific experiments on physical and chemical changes.
Show respect for evidence collected on different chemical and physical changes.
Work collaboratively with group members to conduct experiments on physical and chemical changes.
Show respect for living things and the physical environment by disposing of chemicals properly as this could harm organisms.
Participate actively in classroom discussions on physical and chemical changes.
Wear safety goggles at all times when conducting experiments.
Respect and undertake wafting of odors in observations
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and help peers who may have physical or learning challenges.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Changes in Water: Learners should explain why state changes of water from solid > liquid > gas are physical changes and not chemical. Have them explain how this relates to the water cycle and our weather. (Evaporation, condensation, precipitation are all physical changes of water.)

Practicing Terminology (true or false)? 1. In a chemical reaction the starting materials are called reactants. (T) 2. In a reaction of vinegar with steel wool we would say that iron oxide (rust) is a product of the reaction. (T) 3. Dissolving solids in water is a physical change. (T) 4. A chemical reaction that overall causes cooling is called an exothermic reaction (F) 5. The reaction of iron and oxygen is an exothermic reaction because it creates heat. (T) 6. Combustion is the process of burning (T)

Experimental Findings: Learners should be asked to list all the indicators of chemical change they observed in experiments and demonstrations held in class. (gas evolved/ temperature change/ precipitates/ color change/ odour change/ starting materials consumed/ light produced)

Graphical Analysis: Learners should explain why the graph of temperature versus time for the steel wool/vinegar experiment eventually flattened out? (After the reactants get used up to make iron oxide, there is no more heat produced and the temperature of the room will begin to moderate the measured temperature of the system.)

Which Gas is Formed?: Have Learners explain how you would determine if the gas given off during a chemical reaction was Oxygen, Hydrogen or Carbon dioxide, using a lighted splint. (oxygen- burn brighter/ hydrogen flammable explode/ carbon dioxide quenches flame)

Applications
1. Your grandmother is making a loaf bread in the kitchen. What evidence can you provide her that this represents a chemical change and not a physical change? (gas evolved to rise bread, starting materials consumed)
2. The power goes out and you light a wax candle with a match. Through the evening the candle continues to run until the candle ha almost disappeared. What evidence can you provide that burning a candle is a chemical change? (heat is produced, a gas is given off, wax as starting material is consumed)
3. You mistakenly leave a litre of milk out on the counter on a hot day. When you return you notice big changes. What evidence can you provide that a chemical change has occurred? (spoiled odor, curdling- precipitate)
4. Your parent’s car will not start. You suspect the battery is the problem. You open the hood and observe the battery cells and terminals. What evidence is likely present that would suggest a chemical change has been occurring that causes the battery to malfunction? (corrosion-terminals may have white or green solid on copper wires, water in battery cells has diminished)
5. You visited a carnival celebration and observed elaborate fireworks explosions. What evidence can you suggest that fireworks involves chemical reactions? (burning occurs of the reactants, light is produced, gas is produced)

Explain the Findings: A One Page Response Paper
A wax candle is lit. 30 mL of vinegar is added to a beaker. To the same beaker is added a heaping tablespoon of baking soda. Instantly, many bubbles are produced. The beaker is moved towards the candle and the evolving gas is poured out over the candle wick. The flame is extinguished. In a one-page response paper, respond to the following:
- Draw a picture that depicts the experiment (2 marks)
- Explain the chemical changes you are observing. (5 marks)
- Explain how you can predict what gas is being produced in the beaker. (3 marks)
- Bonus question (+5) Research the two chemical reactions that are happening in this experiment and write down the word equation for each

Teacher note: Vinegar (acetic acid) plus baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) generates carbon dioxide which puts out the flame. The other products are water and sodium acetate. The reaction in the flame is carbon plus heat and oxygen (in the air) to generate water and carbon dioxide.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Remind Learners that in grade 5, they would have done several experiments where they cooled substances, melted substances, mixed substances, etc. We learned the difference between a physical change and a chemical change. Let us review, with this picture. If we place a watch glass on a beaker of boiling water.
Retrieved from: https://www.fishersci.ca/shop/products/pyrex-ribbed-watch-glasses-3/p-207592

What is happening to the volume of water in the beaker? (it is decreasing-disappearing) Where does the liquid water go? (it turns to water vapor; water as a gas) What do we see on the bottom of the watch glass? (water droplets; water as a liquid) What has happened to the water vapor that was escaping? (it cooled on the glass and converted back to liquid water) So if I were to ask you what type of change this was chemical or physical, what would you say? (physical change) What other examples might be physical change of water? (melting an ice cube from solid to liquid water) On a piece of paper, I want you to write down why each of the pictures below represents a physical change.
Melting chocolate (change from solid chocolate to liquid chocolate upon cooling will become solid again)
Retrieved from: https://pixabay.com/photos/chocolate-cream-

food-dessert-sweet-89034/
Ice Treat
(change from liquid juice to frozen desert)
Retrieved from: https://www.freepik.com
So to review our definition:
Physical changes are those changes that do not result in new substances being formed. In most cases, these changes can also be easily reversed.
The other type of change we have observed in the world around us is called a chemical change. Unlike physical changes, these changes cannot be reversed because a new substance is formed. Think about cookies and bread. These all started with ingredients like flour, water, baking soda and maybe yeast; we end up with something that has very different characteristics; something we can eat! We can’t go back to the starting materials because they have been transformed into a different product.
Today we are going to investigate the evidence we can gather that a chemical change has actually occurred.
Experiment 1: Done as a teacher demonstration
Materials needed for this reaction are: Tablespoon of Baking soda, 20mL vinegar, a bottle, a balloon, a measuring cylinder, safety goggles, a scale and a spatula/spoon, a funnel, thermometer.

NOTE: This experiment can also be done with an Alka seltzer® tablet and water if this is more accessible to teachers
Retrieved from: https://pixabay.com/photos/baking-soda-box-white-powder-768950/
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:TBBSC_Digital_Scale.jpg
Demonstration
Add 20 mL of vinegar to a plastic bottle. Weigh the total mass of the following: a balloon, 1 tablespoon of baking soda and the bottle containing vinegar. Record the total mass on the board for children to see and record in their notebook. This is the before data. Explain to Learners we call these starting materials reactants.

Retrieved from: https://www.shutterstock.com/search/bottle-balloon
Ask them to observe carefully the characteristics of the starting materials and write those down. Pass the bottle around for children to observe and ask these questions.
- What does the bottle of liquid look like (clear liquid in the bottom of bottle)?
- What does the liquid smell like? (sharp or acrid smell)
- What does the baking soda look like? (fine white powder, no smell)
- Using a thermometer, measure the temperature of the liquid vinegar. Have Learners record this temperature in their notebook.
Stretch the neck of a balloon several times. With a funnel (make with paper if necessary), add the baking soda into the bottle and quickly place the neck of the balloon over the opening in the top of the bottle.
- Ask the Learners what they observe happening? (there are bubbles in the liquid/ the balloon is beginning to inflate)
- What has happened to the solid go that was added to the bottle? (it is no longer there, it may bay have reacted)
- While the balloon is still on the bottle, weigh the entire closed system again and have Learners record the mass. (teacher note: the mass should not change- we will speak to this later)
- Remove the balloon and pass the bottle around for Learners to carefully smell. Teacher note: teach Learners to use their hand to waft the smell towards their nose- they should not ever put their nose directly to the opening of the reaction vessel (see:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m0xoin9snHQ (0.54 mins) How does the smell compare to the beginning before we added the baking soda? (not as strong smelling)

Measure the temperature of the liquid in the bottle and have Learners record it. (The temperature should have decreased- explanation: It took energy to break the baking soda and vinegar apart and energy was released when the products were formed. Since more energy was needed to break the baking soda and vinegar apart, the temperature went down (decreased). A reaction that overall causes cooling is called an endothermic reaction.)

Learners, what are the changes we have observed? 1) A gas is produced 2) The solid baking soda has disappeared and only a liquid remains (irreversible reaction) 3) The temperature has changed in the liquid 4) The odor of the gas above the liquid has become less acrid
These are all indicators or signs that a chemical change has occurred.

Repeat the experiment for Learners but this time leave the balloon off of the bottle. Learners, you have already identified that this chemical change produces a gas. There is a simple test we can do to narrow down what that gas might be. What are some of your guesses? It has to be somehow part of the starting materials. Vinegar is also called acetic acid and the formula is: CH3COOH Baking soda is called sodium bicarbonate and the formula is: NaHCO3
If you look at the starting materials, what gasses are possible if we mix these up? (oxygen, carbon dioxide, hydrogen)

After adding the baking soda to the vinegar, prepare to hold a lit wooden splint at the mouth of the bottle but first ask Learners these questions: Now Learners, we know that to burn something we need oxygen so if this chemical reaction is producing oxygen, what will happen? (the splint will continue to burn or burn brighter) We also know that carbon dioxide that we breathe out not only blows out birthday candles but is also used in fire extinguishers. If this reaction is producing carbon dioxide, what will likely happen? (It should put the flame out.) Lastly, hydrogen gas is very flammable. You may have heard that hot air balloons of the past that were filled with hydrogen gas created some big explosions. (see: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fURATK5Yt30 (5:09 mins) What is likely to happen if the gas produced is hydrogen? (The splint will burn but maybe explode!)
Proceed to hold the burning splint to the mouth of the bottle- the splint should go out. Which gas is it Learners? (carbon dioxide)

Explain to Learners that the outcome of this reaction is new materials we call products of the reaction. Because the balloon sealed off the bottle, we say that this was a closed system; nothing could escape.

What did you notice about the masses that you wrote down before and after the chemical reaction? (they are almost the same) Why would that be? Let me explain. Everything in and on Earth is made of matter. The amount of matter on Earth does not change. Matter is only changed from one form to another. Matter changes physically and chemically all the time; however, the total amount of matter always remains the same. This is known as the Law of Conservation of Matter.

Matter can neither be created nor destroyed. It can only change from one substance to another. In our experiment, there was a chemical change but even though we have new products, the overall mass of the system did not change.
Experiment 2: Done as a teacher demonstration
Materials required Two clear plastic cups, Sodium carbonate (Washing soda), Magnesium sulphate (Epsom salt), Water, Measuring cylinder, Measuring spoon and Popsicle sticks for stirring
Retrieved from: https://pixabay.com/photos/popsicle-sticks-sticks-wood-350084/ https://www.flickr.com/photos/creative_tools/4297933700

Demonstration Place two teaspoons of Epsom salts in a cup and 2 teaspoons of washing soda in a separate cup. Explain to Learners that these should not be touched, tasted or smelled as you pass them around to make observations. They should record their observations in their notebooks.

What do you observe? (a crystalline solid and a powdery solid) Ask Learners what they think will happen if you add one solid to another? (a chemical reaction) Add the Epsom salts to the washing soda and of course nothing happens. “Learners, I bet you were expecting an explosion!” In order for a chemical reaction to happen to happen, the reactants must somehow have collisions, some way of interacting. In this case the solids can’t interact at the molecular level because they both exist as solids. They act like marbles added to sand! Nothing happens. We are going to repeat the combination but this time, I am going to add 100mL of water to each solid before I combine them. In this way, the molecules can collide within the medium of water once they are dissolved. Show Learners separate containers and the addition of water and stirring. It looks different now because the solids are now dissolved in water. The water and the independent solids have not reacted- We could prove this by evaporating the water off. We would see that we got the washing powder and Epsom salts right back again- that would demonstrate only a physical change. What do you notice about the two solutions? Did the solids dissolve? (the solutions are both clear; you can’t see any solids) But what do you predict will happen when we combine the two solutions? (a chemical reaction) Slowly add the washing soda solution to the Epsom salts solution. Learners, what do you see happening? (a solid is forming in the water and falling to the bottom) Scientists call that solid a precipitate. What do you suppose that solid is? It must be some combination of the starting materials! What did we start with?

Washing soda has a chemical name sodium carbonate. Epsom salt has a chemical name magnesium sulfate. These two chemicals get together and swap partners! sodium carbonate + magnesium sulfate > magnesium carbonate + sodium sulfate As it happens, one product, sodium sulfate readily dissolves in water but we say that magnesium carbonate precipitates out of the water as a solid. So Learners, we see that a precipitate is another indicator of chemical change. Compounds of magnesium and calcium are common in our environment, but they can impact our water supply. Hard and soft water Water can be classified as hard water and soft water As water passes over rocks, some of the minerals (e.g. Calcium and Magnesium), in these rocks dissolve in the water. It becomes hard water. Hard water has minerals containing magnesium and calcium salts dissolved in it. Hard water does not lather with soap, making it difficult to wash clothes with soap. Soft water on the other hand lathers soap. Rainwater is a very good example of soft water, as it contains very little dissolved substance which is why rainwater is suitable for household purposes. As hard water does not mix well with soap. We have to ‘soften’ hard water before we can use it

What happens when soap is mixed with “hard” water? Soap “scum” is a common example of a precipitate that forms when certain minerals in water (e.g. calcium and magnesium) react with soap. In this experiment, you will be making your own hard water and mixing it with soap to see what happens. You will then compare the lathering ability of soap and soap scum to find out whether soap scum is a new substance or it is the same as soap. Student Experiment Water, Epsom salt (magnesium sulfate) , liquid soap, paper towels, 2 Popsicle sticks, 2 Empty disposable water bottles (8-oz) with caps, clear plastic cups, 1 small cup, 1 tablespoon, 1 teaspoon Procedure 1. Label three plastic cups Soap, Water and Hard Water. 2. Prepare the soap solution (a few drops of liquid soap in a glass of water and stirring the mixture). 3. Prepare the hard water (2 teaspoons of Epsom salt (magnesium sulfate) to a glass of water. Stir the solution until no more solid will dissolve). 4. Place ¼ cup of water in the cup labelled Water. 5. Add 1 tablespoon of the soap solution to both the Water and Hard Water cups. 6. Look at both cups from the top and the side. Ask Learners: What differences, if any, do you observe in the way soap combines with water compared with what happened when it was added to hard water?

Expected results: The soap solution will mix with water and cause the water to look cloudy. When the soap solution is added to hard water, a white curd-like substance will form. This is the precipitate, “soap scum” which is the result of magnesium sulfate in the hard water undergoing a chemical reaction with a soap molecule.
Experiment 3: More Indicators of Chemical Change

Teacher note: This can be done as a teacher demonstration or a student experiment. The chemical reaction of steel wool (iron) and vinegar (acetic acid) creates a foul smell so it should be carried out in a well-ventilated area inside the classroom or outside.
Materials needed: Vinegar, steel wool, thermometer, Ziplock® bag, graduated cylinder, glass reaction container (bowl or beaker)

Procedure: Place the steel wool in a Ziplock® plastic bag and pass it around the classroom for Learners to observe and take note of the characteristics of the steel wool. Explain to them that this is iron wool (symbol Fe). Place 20 mL of vinegar in a bowl or beaker for each student group and ask them to observe and take note of the characteristics of the vinegar (e.g. clear liquid with sharp acid smell) Teacher note: Again, remind Learners of the practice of wafting a smell towards their nose rather than putting their nose in close proximity to the vinegar. (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m0xoin9snHQ) Within the bag, have Learners break off a piece of the steel wool, remove it from the bag and place it in the reaction bowl containing the 20mL of vinegar. Be sure to cover the steel wool with vinegar and let it set for one minute.

Remove the steel wool from the vinegar and gently squeeze out any excess vinegar. Wrap the steel wool around the base of the thermometer and place the thermometer into the clean glass beaker. Cover the beaker with a paper towel or a lid to trap the heat inside. Record the starting temperature and then record the temperature every 30 seconds for 5 minutes. Ask them to record their observations in their notebooks.

Sample notebook: Observations of starting materials Steel wool: Vinegar: Temperature (degrees C) | Time (secs) 0 | 30 | 60 > 5 mins Observations of reaction and products: In their notebooks, Learners should plot a graph from their data table where temperature is on the y axis (ordinate) and time is on the x axis (abscissa). Have Learners comment on the shape of the graph (increase in temperature and then leveling of temperature as reaction subsides) What signs did they see that a chemical reaction maybe happening? Disappearance of steel wool starting material
Color change (brownish orange)
Temperature change (increases and then levels off)
New odour given off
Learners what do you think the products may have been? (Perhaps, something with iron in it?)
What was the color change did you see? (orange brown)
Did it remind you of anything you may have seen in the community that was made of iron? (rusty nails, rusty bicycle, rusty car, rusty railing)
Rust is iron reacted with oxygen in the air. (Fe2O3) Let me explain this reaction you have just seen. Iron + Oxygen → Iron oxide

Explanation: Steel wool contains iron. It has a protective coating that keeps oxygen from coming into contact with the iron. When the steel wool is soaked in the vinegar, the vinegar which contains acetic acid, removes this coating. When the protective coating is removed, the iron is exposed and oxygen in the atmosphere can reach the iron in the steel wool and reacts with it to form rust, also called iron oxide. The iron and the oxygen are referred to as reactants while the rust or oxide, is the product of the reaction. The reaction of iron and oxygen creates heat and therefore we call it an exothermic reaction
Real world applications: 1. Why do you think some items made from steel will
sometimes rust? (scratching the paint off a car or bicycle)

Persons living near to the sea have observed that metal objects tend to rust more easily than those much further away from the sea. Why do you think this is so? (the salt in the air may assist the rust forming reaction)
Ask Learners to make a list of items in the community that rust easily. (steel items)
What do you think can be done to help to prevent rusting? (Coating item with a layer of paint of grease)
Why do you think certain items are made from stainless steel instead of iron? (Stainless steel less likely to rust)
Find the cost of an item that is made from stainless steel and one made from iron? (stainless steel is more costly)
One Last Indicator of Chemical Change: Light

Burning of a candle or wood is the heating of the reactant to a high enough temperature in the presence of oxygen. This causes what we call burning and burning gives off light. Besides carbon dioxide and water, combustion (burning) produces light!
Other things can burn in the presence of oxygen and produce large bright amounts of light. A good example is the burning of magnesium wire. See here: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/1HCtPCHFA5Q
In fact, burning magnesium wire in a closed system was often used for flash lighting in old cameras. (see: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/5eUIepuZjKc

Light is also given off in complex biochemical reactions within the bodies of animals. This is called bioluminescence. (see: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fYsAK24le6U (3:29 mins) Examples include fireflies and Deep sea animals

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Develop and use a model to describe how the total number of atoms does not change in a chemical reaction and thus mass is conserved.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: Conservation of matter (mass), Effervescent tablets, Methane, Combustion reaction, Reactants, Products, Open system, Oxidation reaction, Atoms vs molecules, Coefficients, Subscripts

Compare an open and closed system using an example (balloon experiments).

Explain how a factory model of car assembly can be used as an analogy of atoms get redistributed in a chemical reaction.
Distinguish atoms and molecules
Distinguish coefficients and subscripts in a balanced chemical equation.
Observe reactions for indicators of chemical change
Classify open and closed systems
Measure masses accurately using a scale
Hypothesize why reactants generate certain products
Count and tabulate atoms in a balanced chemical reaction.
Predict products based on reactant identity
Predict mass of products based on conservation of mass principle.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Conservation of Mass in Closed Systems: In the Alka Seltzer® experiment and also the steel wool experiment, in the reactions, we are able to accurately measure the mass of reactants (before) and products (after) because these are closed systems. What does that mean? (The reactants before, they are consumed, can easily be measured independently. One of the products however is a gas. We can measure the mass of the flask and it contents with ease but we need the balloon to contain the gas produced otherwise it would be lost to the atmosphere. Because the balloon keeps the gases inside the flask system, we say that we have a closed system.)

Predicting Mass of Products: Assuming a closed system, if 32 g of Sulfur reacts with 32g of Oxygen to form a substance called Sulfur dioxide. Based on the principle of conservation of mass, what is the mass of the product formed in this reaction? (64 grams of sulfur dioxide)
Counting Atoms: Many of you would have seen the experiment where you combine vinegar with baking soda.

The following is the balanced equation that describes that chemical reaction. CH3COOH + NaHCO3 →CH3COONa + CO2 + H2O. Learners should confirm the conservation of matter by counting atoms on the reactant and product sides of the equation and filling in the table below. Teacher note: You may have to teach Learners how to count atoms given the subscripts on some. You needn’t discuss moles at this point, just explain that the balancing of equations will come later. (Table: Atoms | # Reactant side | # Product side: H (5) | (5); C (3) | (3); O (5) | (5); Na (1) | (1))
inclusive_learning_strategies: Introduction: Learners, I want to pose to you a simple problem to start today. I want you to imagine you are operating a car assembly factory. In that factory each car frame must have 4 wheels attached to it. So my assembly line instructions would be: 1 frame + 4 wheels generates >>> 1 car. a. So, if I had 2 frames and 8 wheels, how many cars would I turn out? (two cars) b. What if I had 2 frames and 6 wheels? (only 1 car with 2 wheels & 1 frame left over) c. What if I had 1 frames and 9 wheels? (1 cars with 5 wheels left over). In cases b & c above we are limited in the cars we can turn out by one of the starting materials. What is limiting us in (b) not enough wheels (c) not enough frames. Chemical reactions are exactly the same. They can only make products if the proper amount of reactants are available. Another interesting thing I want you to think about with regard to our factory equation.

The number of frames and wheels remains the same on both sides of the equation. They are just distributed in a different way. Chemical equations are exactly the same. Atoms are neither created nor destroyed. Both sides of an equation will have the same number of atoms, they will just be distributed in different ways. Since this is true, this in turn means that the mass of the reactants will be the same as the mass of the products This is called conservation of matter (mass) and it is centrally important when it comes to planning chemical reactions in the laboratory. Let us leave our factory now and think more about actual chemical reactions.

Conservation of Mass Experiment: Alka Seltzer® Example can be done in student groups or as a teacher demonstration. Either way, it most important to engage discussion. Alka-Seltzer® is a common antacid used to relieve an upset stomach. Alka-Seltzer® and other fizzing effervescent tablets produce carbon dioxide when placed in water. Ask Learners if they have ever seen what happens when an antacid tablet such as Alka-Seltzer® is dropped in water. Allow Learners to share their experiences. (Learners’ responses will vary). Explain to Learners that you will be demonstrating the reaction between an effervescent tablet and water. Procedure: 1. Using a measuring cylinder / a measuring cup / a beaker, measure 100 mL of water. 2. Add the 100 mL of water to a bottle and place the bottle on a weighing scale. 3. Place an effervescent tablet in a balloon after breaking it into two or three pieces. 4. Place the balloon with the tablet on the scale with the bottle. 5. Learners should note the total mass of the water, the bottle, balloon and effervescent tablet in their notebooks. 6. Fit the neck of the balloon tightly around the mouth of the bottle (containing the water) being careful not to drop the antacid pieces into the water at the bottom of the bottle.

Ask Learners what they think might happen when you raise the balloon above the water bottle and allow the tablet pieces to drop in the bottle? (dissolving of the tablet in water and then a reaction with gas produced)
Raise the balloon and allow the tablet pieces to drop into the water. The Learners will see bubbles begin to form and the balloon should partially inflate.
Ask Learners what evidence they have that there has been a chemical reaction? (reactant consumed, gas bubbles produced and trapped in balloon)

When the tablets have fully dissolved and the carbon dioxide bubbles subside, place the whole system on the weighing scale again. Have Learners record in their notebooks the mass of the system after the chemical reaction (i.e. the products).

Learners should compare the total mass of the reactants (before reaction) and the total mass of the products (after reaction). These numbers should very nearly be the same. Ask Learners what could lead to a difference in masses? (leaky balloon, poor mass measurement)
The teacher should then reinforce the principle of Conservation of Mass.

Reviewing a Past Reaction: You may remember that we can rinse steel wool with vinegar and then expose it to oxygen in the air and it will form iron oxide (rust) in a chemical reaction. If we place the rinsed steel wool in a flask and cover the flask with a balloon two interesting things happen:
The balloon will be pushed into the flask as the iron consumes the oxygen and makes iron oxide (rust),
The mass of the system, start to finish, does not change.

Because this is a closed system, this experiment once again demonstrates that the mass of the reactants and products does not change. We know this is because atoms are redistributed but neither lost or created within the chemical reaction

Considering Conservation at the Atomic Level: Methane (CH4) is a gas made up of carbon and hydrogen. Methane is emitted during the production and transport of coal, natural gas, and oil. Methane emissions also result from livestock and other agricultural practices and by the decay of organic waste in municipal solid waste landfills.
Teacher note; If you have molecular models or coffee sticks/plasticine, you should demonstrate that methane forms a tetrahedron structure as per below.
Methane is very useful because it can be burned in a combustion reaction.
Ask Learners what is needed to burn something? (a fuel, a kindling temperature and oxygen)
Ask Learners what is produced from burning? (carbon dioxide and water)

The Learners should then be shown the balanced equation below that describes methane burning. Teacher note: It is not important at this stage to explain “how” we balance an equation. Just explain that we must have a balanced equation to adequately describe the mass balance in a reaction.
Retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VvbX8PitSpg
Reiterate the components that they have already mentioned: Methane (CH₄) is a molecule containing carbon and hydrogen
To burn methane, you need oxygen (O₂) which is added to the CH₄
These two are called the reactants
The products of the chemical reaction are carbon dioxide (CO₂) and water (H₂O)
This is called a balanced combustion reaction because it describes exactly how the burning of the fuel methane occurs.
Ask Learners to look carefully at the chemical structures of each molecule in the reaction.
Ask them to count the black colored balls for carbon (C) on each side of the reaction (1 black ball reactant side, 1 black ball product side)
Ask them to count the white colored balls for hydrogen (H) on each side of the reaction (4 white balls reactant side, 4 white balls product side)
Ask them to count the red colored balls for oxygen (O) on each side of the reaction (4 red balls reactant side, 4 red balls product side)
Ask them “did we lose any atoms in converting methane to products through a chemical reaction (No, the atoms are reorganized but they still count up the same)
Explain to Learners that this is an example of conservation of matter that involves a chemical reaction
Introduce a Review Example- Copper Pipe
Give Learners the scenario of a copper pipe that has been weighed.
Ask them what happens to the pipe if left in the open air? (it will rust)
When metals like iron and copper combine with oxygen in the open air, we say they become oxidized to form rust.

When the copper grabs oxygen from the air, matter has not increased overall because the copper (Cu) simply reacted with the oxygen in the atmosphere that itself has mass. This may be harder to understand because this is an open system but overall the mass in the entire system has not changed.
Retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VvbX8PitSpg (5:52 mins)

Point out to Learners that in this oxidation reaction, the number of atoms of each component on the reactant and product side is the same. The air gave up some oxygen to oxidized (rust) the copper pipe but overall, the amount of matter in the world has not changed, it has just been reorganized.
Representing a Chemical Reaction

In much of our discussions to date, we have written word equations to represent chemical reactions. However, these word equations do not tell us what atoms or molecules are actually involved in the reaction. To describe chemical reactions, scientists use balanced chemical equations, which make use of symbols and formulas for elements, molecules and compounds. In a chemical equation as with word equations, the reactants are shown on the left-hand side of the equation and the products are shown on the right-hand side of the equation.
For example, the balanced chemical equation for the reaction of sodium and water is the following:

From this equation, we can see that on the left-hand side we have sodium and water as reactants. The 2 in front of sodium tells us there are two sodium atoms reacting and the 2 in front of water tell us there are 2 molecules of water reacting. These numbers are referred to as coefficients in the balanced equation.
Teacher note: reiterate that atoms come together to make molecules.

Learners, How many molecules of sodium hydroxide are produced? (2) How many molecules of hydrogen gas are produced (1) *explain to Learners that we sometimes don’t add a 1 coefficient because 1 molecule is implied *

If you look again at the equation, you will see the number 2 after the H in water on the reactant side and 2 after the H on the product side. These numbers called subscripts are simply the number of atoms that make up the molecule. Example: There are 2 atoms of H in every water molecule and 2 atoms of H in every hydrogen gas molecule (H2)
Let us do an atom count on each side of the reaction to confirm conservation of matter. Atoms # Reactant side # Product side H (4) (4) O (2) (2) Na (2) (2)

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Develop models to describe that organisms have unique and diverse life cycles but all have in common birth, growth, reproduction, and death

things grow and change over time.

Using live organisms or pictures, observe the changes that occur during a plant’s life cycle.

Activity: Drawing and describing the different stages of a plant's life cycle
Homework Assignment: Look around your yard, select five plants and state what stage of their lifecycle they are at.
Activity: Creating an art piece of a stage of the life cycle of a flowering plant
Simulation: Being a pollinator

Set up an imaginary garden with flowers that are tagged with certain information about each plant and its flower. Each group will select a flower that the pollinator they were assigned, would be attracted to. Once they have selected their flower, they will take it back to their table.
Have a member from each group present their flower to the class and explain why it would be attractive to their pollinator.
Before Learners do the next activity, demonstrate the process of pollination between 2 flowers: take the pollen from the anther of one flower and put it on the stigma of the other flower.
For this assignment, each student will design a flower using construction paper, pipe cleaners and other craft materials, or using a computer software
Have them describe how it would be pollinated (by an animal, wind, or humans) and how the features of the flower help in the pollination process.

Learners will present their flower to the rest of the class. Presenters will name their flower, describe it and state how it is pollinated. At random points, ask the presenter to identify the part of the flower.
Bees play a very important role in nature. One such role is the role of a pollinator.
What is a pollinator? (A pollinator is an organism that helps to carry pollen from the male part of the flower (stamen) to the female part of the same or another flower.)
List four pollinators other than bees (Birds, Moths, Ants and Humans)

State two other ways in which bees are important in nature. (Bees produce honey to feed their colonies, man and other organisms. Bee hives provide shelter for many other insects. Bees also serve as food for other organisms.)

State two ways that we can protect bees and other pollinators. (Plant plants that produce flowers in home gardens and in the community. Avoid using pesticides and herbicides in gardens. Support local beekeepers. Create areas that bees would be able to build their nests undisturbed.)
Pollination is the process whereby pollen is transferred from the anther to the stigma.
What is self-pollination? (Self-pollination is defined as the deposition of pollen grains from a flower to the stigma of the same flower.)
What is cross-pollination? (Cross-pollination is defined as the deposition of pollen grains from a flower to the stigma of another flower.)
What is wind-pollination? (Wind pollination describes the process of the transfer of pollen from one individual plant to another, whereby the pollen is carried by air currents.)
Plants that are pollinated by the wind (wind-pollinated) have different characteristics from those pollinated by insects (insect-pollinated).
Give two characteristics of plants that are wind-pollination. (Flowers are usually small and inconspicuous, and do not possess a scent or produce nectar.)
Give two characteristics of plants that are insect-pollination.
State which of the following flowers is a wind-pollinated flower. (No. 2)
What is fertilization in plants?
Following the processes of pollination and fertilization, the next stage is the formation of fruits.
State two ways in which fertilization and pollination are important.
Explain how fruits are formed.
Give two examples of a fruit with one seed.
Give two examples of a fruit with a few seeds.
Give two examples of a fruit with many seeds.
What is germination?
In the diagram below, explain what is happening at the stages labelled A and B.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Each living thing undergoes a series of changes as it goes through the different life stages. These changes follow a particular pattern that is very specific to its species. These series of changes are referred to as its life cycle. It is called a cycle because the stages are continuous. Some life cycles are as short as a few hours or a few days, while others last for a few hundred years. Some species produce offspring that are very similar to the adult while others produce offspring that undergo several stages, before emerging as an adult form that can reproduce and start the cycle again. In this unit, we will be looking at the life cycles of several different organisms. We will start with the butterfly.

Life cycle of a butterfly: Have you ever noticed that butterflies don’t seem to maintain their beautiful wings throughout the season? So you are telling me that the winged butterfly doesn’t appear that way all season? What else do they look like? (Caterpillars, Cocoons). Teacher note: Before the lesson starts, collect butterflies, eggs, larvae and pupae. Start the lesson by asking Learners a series of questions about the butterfly to pique their interest. If you have a butterfly in a jar that Learners can see, that would be very helpful. Retrieved from: https://pixabay.com/photos/butterfly-insects-love-spring-763578/ Ask the Learners what they know about butterflies. 1. Have you ever touched a butterfly? (Yes/No) 2. What did it look like? (colors, wing shapes, sizes)) 3. Was it colourful? (Yes/No) Many of you have seen parts of the life cycle of the butterfly but in order to get a complete picture, we should actually make our own observations. Activity: Observing the different stages of the butterfly Put caterpillars in a box and feed them daily on a fresh supply of the type of leaves that they feed on. This allows Learners to observe the stages of the life cycle (from caterpillar to adult) and to hypothesise how they think their caterpillar will turn into a butterfly and what it may look like.

You can introduce the term metamorphosis as a passage between stages of the life cycle. After setting up the box, ask Learners to do the following: 1. Observe and record the development of the caterpillars each day. 2. Record all changes from caterpillar to pupa, to the fully developed butterfly phase. Learners should draw labelled pictures of the stages in their notebooks making note of the timeline of each stage. 3. Learners are expected to use the following materials to create a model of the stages of the butterfly development. Teacher note: Materials which can be used to make models of the life cycle of the butterfly may include paper plates, and coloured pipe cleaners (cut in half)/pieces of wool, small beans or peas, cotton balls, green leaves cut from construction paper, food colouring, glue/tape, crayons/markers. A variety of pasta can also be used to complete the task: Spiral pasta - caterpillar, shell pasta - chrysalis, bow-tie pasta - butterfly, small pasta - eggs) After the life cycle is completed, release the fully developed butterflies. In a closure session, ask the Learners to contribute one stage of the cycle and add this to the chalk board. Asks Learners to elaborate on their findings and correct any misconceptions as you proceed. Use their diagram to explain what happens at each stage of the life cycle. As you move through the different stages of the life cycle, have live or preserved specimens to show to Learners.

Review: Butterflies pass through four stages during their life cycles: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis and adult butterfly. We will now look closer at the diagram of the life cycle to see what happens at each stage
Teacher note: After going through the life cycle diagram, reiterate why it is called a life cycle and how the diagram depicts this.

First stage of life cycle of a butterfly: A butterfly starts its life as an egg, laid by an adult, female butterfly after mating. The adult, female butterfly lays her eggs on plants. Butterfly eggs vary in size, shape and colour but most are surrounded by a protective hard shell.

Second stage of life cycle of a butterfly: A caterpillar also called a butterfly larva, develops from the egg and then eats its way out of the shell. A caterpillar spends all its time eating. The plant supplies food and water for the caterpillar. As it grows, the caterpillar becomes too large for its skin and moults (sheds its skin) to make new skin.

Third stage of the butterfly life cycle: When the caterpillar reaches its final size, it stops feeding. It wriggles and twists, to gradually remove its old skin, revealing a new protective skin called a cocoon. Inside the cocoon, the caterpillar changes into a wormlike creature called a pupa or chrysalis (shown below). You may have seen chrysalis hanging from trees.

Fourth stage of the butterfly life cycle: When the pupa reaches adulthood, it changes into a butterfly and leaves the cocoon. It pumps blood into its wrinkled wings and expands them to their full size before flying away. When the butterfly comes out of the chrysalis, it is ready to find a mate and lay eggs. Then the cycle can start all over again

Mosquito Life Cycle
Observing the stages:
1. Collect the different stages of the mosquito life cycle: Show the children examples of eggs, pupa, larva and adult.
2. Keep the different stages in a large screw top jar or an aquarium with a glass top. Put water weeds in the jar or aquarium.
3. Teach the children to identify the different stages in the life cycle of the mosquito and draw labelled pictures of the stages in their notebooks.

4. Do not allow adult mosquitoes to fly out and bite the Learners. This can cause Learners to be infected with diseases carried by the mosquito.
Teacher Note:
It is important that Learners are able to recognize stages in the life cycle of the mosquito, as these insects can transmit diseases such as Dengue fever, Zika, Yellow fever and Chikungunya and it is important that Learners are part of any monitoring or control activities taking place in their home, as the majority of mosquito breeding sites, are found on private properties. Mosquitoes go through a four-stage life cycle like the butterfly. These stages include the egg, larva, pupa and adult.

Recognizing mosquito life cycle at home/ Controlling Mosquitos
1. Show the children diagrams of the Aedes aegypti mosquito that carries dengue fever. Point out to them that this is the same mosquito that spreads Zika and Chikungunya viruses. Allow them time to observe the diagrams.
2. Encourage the children to collect stages of the life cycle of the mosquito found near their homes.
3. Discuss areas in and around the home and in the community that may be good breeding sites for mosquitoes.
4. Discuss what can be done to control mosquitoes near homes and in the community.

Control
- Mosquitoes carry diseases such as malaria fever, dengue fever, encephalitis, and the Ross River virus.
- The larva is the stage when you can most easily control the mosquito, because it has to live in water for a long time.
- Remove water containers, e.g. buckets, discarded containers, roof gutters, pot plant trays.
- Cover entry spaces to rainwater tanks with fine wire mesh.
- Put fish that eat mosquito larvae in ponds.
- Remove water containers, e.g. buckets, discarded containers, roof gutters, pot plant trays. Draining the water or adding insecticide to the water kills the larvae and controls mosquitoes.
- If you must go outside, wear a long-sleeved shirt, long pants and use an insect repellent. Products such as burning citronella candles have been found to repel insects in the outdoors.

would have noticed that as the organism went through each stage of its life cycle, it experienced certain changes. These set of changes is referred to as metamorphosis. There are two types of metamorphosis: one is referred to as complete metamorphosis and the other, incomplete metamorphosis. Complete metamorphosis Animals that undergo complete metamorphosis go through four distinct stages: egg, larva, pupa and adult. The butterfly and the mosquito are two examples of organisms that undergo complete metamorphosis. Incomplete metamorphosis Incomplete metamorphosis is a type of metamorphosis in which an insect hatches from an egg and then goes through several nymphal stages. Each nymphal stage looks like a small version of the adult but getting slightly bigger with age. At the final nymphal stage, the insect then molts into the adult form. Examples of animals that undergo incomplete metamorphosis include Cockroach, grasshopper, termites, cricket, and praying mantis. We will look at an example of an animal that undergoes incomplete metamorphosis a little later.

At which stage does it look like its half frog and half fish? (Young frog or froglet) 3. At which stage does it look like a frog? (It looks like a frog when it gets to the final stage or an adult frog)

Comparing Student Findings: A Review of the Life Cycle We will now look at the life cycle of the frog. Here are the four stages.
Retrieved from: https://www.sciencewithme.com/learn-about-the-life-cycle-of-the-frog/

Stage 1: Egg Female frogs lay eggs. They are laid in still waters where there are no strong currents to threaten their survival. Why do you think fast water currents will affect frog eggs? (They could wash them down the river or out to sea or they may get bashed about in the water.). Tadpoles hatch from the eggs after about one to three weeks.

Stage 2: Tadpoles. A tadpole hatches from an egg. A tadpole is born with a tail, poorly formed gills but no legs. The tadpole lives in water. The tadpole breathes underwater through gills and swims like a fish. The tadpole does not stay this way: Firstly, the tadpole’s body changes shape. Secondly, it grows back legs and its jaws are formed.

Froglet A tadpole eventually grows into a froglet. The froglet is different from the tadpole because it has legs for hopping and it breathes with lungs. After about four weeks, the gills it had as a tadpole, are covered over with skin. When this happens, breathing will begin to take place through the lungs.

Frog: A froglet grows into an adult frog. As this happens, it undergoes tow changes: Change No. 1: a head will be easier to discern and the body will get longer. Change No. 2: By about 12 weeks, the next step in the frog’s development will be almost complete. At this point, it will stop eating plants for nutrition, it reabsorbs its tail (which is why the tail disappears and the adult frog has no tail). When the final stage of development is complete, the froglet is now an adult frog. A new life cycle begins when a female frog lays eggs.

Life cycle of the cockroach: An Example of Incomplete Metamorphosis: The cockroach is a difficult life cycle to observe, so I am simply going to review what we know about the cycle, and I want you to think about where you have seen some of the stages. The cockroach has three life stages: the egg, a nymphal and the adult stage. The life cycle from egg to adult averages about 600 days.

Cockroach Eggs: The life cycle of a cockroach life begins with a cockroach egg. The cockroach lays egg in an egg case also called an ootheca, each egg case contains 10 to 50 eggs. The deposited ootheca contains water sufficient for the eggs to develop without receiving additional water

Nymph: Next in the cockroach life cycle is the nymphal stage. Nymphs hatch and undergo several molts, growing a little each time they shed their skins. They will reach full adult size and coloration with their last molt.
Adult: After their final molt, nymphs enter the adult stage and reproduce to start the cycle over again.

Life cycles that involve live births instead of hatching from eggs: The third animal life cycle involves live births. Nearly all mammals including humans, dogs, bears, horses, and many others go through a live birth life cycle. These animals do not lay eggs, the animal grows in a special way inside the female’s body. They are then born alive. When the young are born, they resemble the adult form of the species. Sometimes brothers and sisters (offspring) will display traits of one parent or the other or even perhaps a trait passed down through the generations. In this way offspring can actually look quite different from each other, even though they have the same parents. They usually need a lot of care. In almost all cases, the mother produces milk to feed them. The young then grow through several stages into adulthood. Although the young doesn’t change much in appearance, it gets larger over time, as it goes through the different stages of the life cycle.

The Human life cycle: Every human being goes through the same stages of life in the same order. There are six main stages in the human life cycle: 1. Foetus -At this time, a baby is growing inside its mum's womb. During this stage, the foetus is entirely dependent on the female for everything. 2. Baby - A baby is born after spending nine months inside the womb. 3. Childhood -At this stage, the child learns to walk, talk and eat independently. 4. Adolescence - The stage between childhood and adulthood when children become teenagers. Here, bodies develop during adolescence. 5. Adulthood - At this stage, the human body is fully developed and this is usually the stage where-reproduction usually takes place.
This is the last stage in the lifecycle of a human. This period starts from 65 years of age, until the end of their lives.
