It does however extend ideas to allow for comparison with life cycles of living things. Life cycle stages and processes of a flowering plant Like all living things, plants grow, reproduce and go through a life cycle. While the life cycle of a plant is different from that of an animal, it still goes through the same basic stages as other living things. Different types of plants have different life cycles. In this section, we will look at the life cycle of a flowering plant (plants that produce flowers) only. The life cycle of a flowering plant is shown in the diagram below. It begins with a seed and ends with a mature plant. Let us go through the different stages together.

The seed A seed is a structure that contains a small embryonic plant covered by a thin outer covering called the seed coat. The embryo is what will grow to become a fully-grown plant. Seeds come in all different shapes and sizes, from the tiny orchid seed to the palm tree seed, to the large seed of a coconut. Teacher note: Review the different parts of the seed with Learners and their functions. Experiments on germination can also be set up for Learners’ observation, if necessary.

Germination Once a seed is provided with the necessary resources and receives the right signals for germination, the seed will begin to absorb water through its seed coat, which will expand and burst open. The embryo found inside the seed, is made up of a shoot and a root. The root emerges first from inside the seed and begins to grow down into the soil. Next, the shoot emerges and grows upwards towards the sun. The shoot develops into a small seedling.

Retrieved from: https://www.britannica.com/science/seed-plant-reproductive-part/Germination See also time lapse bean growth: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w77zPAtVTuI (3:09 mins) Seedling The shoot develops into a stem with either one or two ‘leaves’. These first leaves are called cotyledons and are not considered ‘true leaves’. Cotyledons provide the developing seedling with nutrients (that are stored in the leaf-like structures) until it grows true leaves that can produce food through photosynthesis. Young Plant As time (days, weeks, months or years) passes, the leaves and stem will get taller, wider and thicker, as the seedling matures into a young plant. Its roots, stems and branches will also grow thicker and stronger, allowing it to support the growing plant and structures such as leaves and flowers. Mature plant The young plant develops into a mature plant. Depending on the species of plant, this can take anything from days to weeks to even years. A mature plant grows flowers, fruits and leaves.

Retrieved from: https://pixabay.com/photos/tree-palm-coconuts-coconut-tree-1281680/ https://pixabay.com/photos/banana-blossom-bloom-banana-flower-64420/ https://pxhere.com/en/photo/703093 Mature plants make their own food through photosynthesis, using the green pigment chlorophyll in their leaves to combine energy from the sun, carbon dioxide and water to make sugar, while releasing oxygen as a by-product. Reproducing stage Plants, like animals, need to reproduce for their species to survive. One way that plants produce offspring is by making seeds. Flowering plants produce seeds through the use of their flowers. The Flowering stage When a flowering plant is ready to reproduce it produces flowers. Once the plant has reached maturity, the flower buds will develop into flowers. These flowers are important in a plant’s life cycle as this is where the new seeds are made. In other words, the flower is where reproduction happens. There are two key events involved in this stage: pollination and fertilisation.

Retrieved from: https://pixabay.com/photos/nature-wildflowers-reed-grass-4737410/ https://pixabay.com/photos/rose-flower-buds-pink-rose-3475452/ Parts of a flower Within the flower, you will find a stamen, which is the male part of the flower, and a pistil the female part. A stamen is composed of the filament and anther. The male part of the flower produces small grains of pollen. The pistil consists of the stigma, style (pollen tube), ovary and ovules (female gametes). Some flowers have only male parts, some have only female parts and some have both male and female parts together. The basic parts of the flower are shown in the diagram below.

Vocabulary - Parts of a Flower: pollen: a fine powder produced by certain plants that plays a role in reproduction; Stamen: the (male) part of a flower where pollen is produced; Stigma: the tip of the pistil (the female part of the flower), which receives the male pollen grains; pistil: the (female) part of the flower that receives the pollen; Ovule: a minute structure that, after fertilization, becomes a plant seed
Activity - labelling a diagram of a flower: Show Learners a diagram of a flower. Which parts do you think are male? Which parts do you think are female? Have Learners label their own diagrams.

What is pollination: To produce seeds that grow into new plants, flowers must be pollinated. To be pollinated, pollen must be moved from the stamen to the stigma. This movement of the pollen from the male anther to the female stigma is called pollination. Pollination can be of two types: self-pollination or cross-pollination.
Self-pollination occurs when the pollen from the anther is deposited on the stigma of the same flower, or another flower on the same plant.
Cross-pollination is the transfer of pollen from the anther of one flower to the stigma of another flower on a different plant of the same species.

Pollinators: Since plants cannot move from place to place, most of them need help with pollination. Animals that pollinate flowers are called pollinators. Pollinators include bees, butterflies, moths, wasps, flies, bats, birds and even hummingbirds but the most common pollinators are insects.

How pollinators move pollen?: When creatures come to feed or collect sweet nectar (made at the base of the petals), from the flowers, or shelter and nest-building materials, they become covered in pollen. As they fly from flower to flower in order to collect nectar, they accidentally rub against the stamens and get pollen stuck all over themselves. When they move to another flower to feed, some of the pollen can rub off onto this new plant's stigma.

People can accidentally transfer pollen from one flower to another by going from flower to flower and handling them. Humans can also manually pollinate some flowers. Discuss why farmers sometimes have to transfer pollen for themselves.
Similarly, when animals pass by a flower, pollen can be carried on their bodies and fur and transferred to different flowers.
On a breezy day, pollen can be transported by air currents. Plants that are wind-pollinated include wheat, rice, corn and rye. Pollen can also be transferred from plant to plant by water.

Features of Insect pollinated plants: Flowers are brightly coloured, scented and secrete nectar. Pollen grains are larger, sticky and spiny. Pollen grains are fewer in number. The stigma of flowers is small and deep within the flower. The stamens are within the flower tube
Some may have a shape that fits with the pollinator (i.e. hibiscus and hummingbird).

Features of Wind pollinated plants: Flowers have no bright colours, special odours, or nectar. Flowers are small and most have no petals. Stamens and stigmas are exposed to air currents. Large amounts of pollen are produced. Pollen grains are smooth, light and easily airborne. Stigma are feathery to catch pollen from the wind. Examples of wind-pollinated plants include: wheat, rice, corn, rye, barley, several grasses and oats.

Observing pollinators: For the first part of this activity, take Learners around the neighbourhood for a casual observation walk. If a flower garden is nearby, it can also be used. Before Learners are taken on the visit/walk, visit the area to see the plants with brightly coloured flowers, where they are located and the pollinators that are found in the area. Before you leave for the visit/walk, remind Learners that they should not remove or damage any of the plants. When they get to the area, ask Learners to do the following: 1. Make a list of the plants with brightly coloured flowers. Take as many pictures of the pictures of the flowers as possible. Make sketches of these flowers if you do not have a camera. Note their colour and size. 2. Make a note of the insects or birds that visit these flowers. Find out the names of these organisms with your teacher’s help. 3. Try to catch a few of the insect pollinators, using a net, not your hands. See if a yellow powdery substance is sticking to their legs and bodies. This substance is pollen (pollen grains). 4. Take as many pictures as possible of the organisms visiting the flowers. 5. When Learners return to the classroom, discuss their findings with them. Make a list of the pollinators that were seen and the flowers they visited. After the class discussion: 1. Assign each group a pollinator. Provide the group with a Profile card that corresponds to the pollinator that they were assigned. 2. Pass out the Pollinator Observation Data Sheet. 3. Learners will then return to the area they first visited and use the data sheet on their pollinator to find out the following: 4. What number of flowers did your pollinator visit the most? (Answers will vary.) 5. What are three flower traits that you think attracted your pollinator? (Answers will vary.)

Other questions: 1. Why do pollinators visit flowers? (Pollinators visit flowers in search of nectar and pollen, to use as food). 2. How do pollinators know which flowers to go to? (There are many different ways pollinators decide which flowers to visit, these include the colour, taste, patterns and popularity of the flower.) 3. What are two strategies that flowers have to attract the most pollinators? (Many flowers use colours to attract insects, sometimes helped by coloured marks to guide them. Flowers are often shaped to provide a landing platform for visiting insects to land on or to force them to brush against anthers and stigmas.)
What is fertilization: After a flower is pollinated, each pollen grain which is on the stigma grows a tiny tube down towards the ovules in the ovary.
Retrieved from: https://k8schoollessons.com/pollination-and-fertilization/

Then the male gametes in pollen grains can reach the ovules, the pollen tube breaks into the ovule and join female gametes or eggs produced in ovules. The female gametes or eggs then join with the male gametes. This joining or the combination of male gametes and female gametes is called fertilization. Fertilization cannot take place without pollination.

After fertilization, a seed forms. The fertilized egg grows to become the embryo found in a seed that will later grow into a new plant. There is also a protective coat around the seed called the seed coat.
The petals, the stamen and the stigma then fall off and the ovary develops into a fruit to protect the seed; fruits take on a variety of different shapes and sizes and colour.

Fruits contain one, a few or many seeds. Some flowers, such as avocados, have only one ovule in their ovary, so their fruit will have only one seed. Many flowers, such as kiwifruit, have lots of ovules in their ovaries, so their fruit contains many seeds. The fruit also helps in dispersing the seeds from the parent plant to continue the cycle.

Questions: 1. Why do you think the petals, the stamen and the stigma fall off? (They fall off because they are no longer needed.) 2. Why is it important to protect the embryo? (It is necessary to protect the embryo from drying out and from any injuries. If the embryo is damaged, it will not be able to germinate and produce a new plant.) 3. Give 2 examples of fruits that contain 1 seed, a few seed and many seeds • (Examples of fruits that contain one seed are: Avocado pear, Coconut, Mango, Plum. • Examples of fruits that contain a few seeds are: Sapodilla, Grape, Apple, Grapefruit • Examples of fruits that contain many seeds are: Watermelon, Papaya, Guava, Passion Fruit Dragon fruit, Pomegranate)

Activity: Fertilization Dissect the flower and show Learners the path the pollen takes. 1. Have Learners trace the path of the pollen and label where fertilization happens on their diagrams. 2. Observe the stamen (with anthers and filament) and the small, thin, pistil. Do you see pollen? (Yes/No) 3. There is an anther cap; pollen is not released until the flower is ready. How is this pistil different from the diagrams you have seen? (Answers will depend on the type of flower used). 4. How many stamens are there? (Answers will depend on the type of flower used). 5. Draw the stamens and pistils in your notebook and label them.
6. Gently pull out each of the stamens. Only a pistil and ovary (the bottom bulb-like structure) will remain.

Dissect the ovary. 1. With your toothpick, make a vertical slice down the middle of the ovary and open it like a book. 2. Place the two halves of the ovary on your plate. What do you see inside the ovary? What do you notice about the way ovules are arranged? What needs to happen so ovules grow into seeds? Draw your dissected ovary with ovules in your notebook and label them. Use your hand lens to see the arrangement of ovules. Select a few on the end of your toothpick to observe. 3. Indicate the location of pollen grains and ovules on a diagram of a flower and trace the path from the stigma to the ovary of a flower.

Seed dispersal The final stage in a flowering plant life cycle is seed dispersal. These seeds, when ready, are dispersed into the environment, so that they can themselves germinate and grow into new plants. The seeds are dispersed by wind, animals, and water.
Teacher note Review with Learners the agents of dispersal and discuss the role that dispersal plays in the life cycle of plants.

Plant death Eventually the plant will age and die. This is the end of the life cycle for that individual, not the end of the species. This may not happen until the plant has reproduced several times. If the plant had successfully produced seeds, its species would survive into the future as the cycle begins again.
Discuss with Learners why plant death is an important part of the plant life cycle.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: ELO 1: Use mathematical representations to describe a simple model for waves that includes how the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy in a wave.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description:
Learners are expected to: Knowledge
- Define the terms:
  - Wave
  - Mechanical wave
  - Electromagnetic wave
  - Energy
  - Amplitude
  - Wavelength
  - Frequency
  - Trough
  - Crest
  - Vibration
  - Longitudinal waves
  - Transverse waves
  - SONAR
Ultrasound
Explain the relationship between amplitude and energy of a wave
Explain the mathematical formula that relates amplitude to energy of a wave
Calculate the energy of a wave given its amplitude, using an appropriate mathematical formula
Interpret a graph of wave function, showing how energy is related to the amplitude of a wave
Predict how changes in amplitude will affect the energy of a wave
Learners will develop interest and curiosity about wave behavior
Learners will value the importance of mathematical modeling in interpreting wave behavior
Learners will demonstrate respect for peers during group work activities and discussions
Learners will demonstrate the utility of waves in everyday technologies.

Place lightweight objects on the drum surface e.g. grains of rice. Hold a stick vertically, one centimetre above the centre of the drum and release to tap the drum creating a wave. Observe and record the amplitude (corresponding to the height of the tap) and maximum distance the rice moves from its initial position (representing energy transferred). Repeat this exercise, holding the stick 2cm, 3cm, and 4 cm above the centre of the drum, each time observing and recording the amplitude (height of the tap) and energy (movement of the rice). Learners should then: Calculate the square of each amplitude value (4 marks), Plot amplitude squared values against energy. (4 marks), Draw a line of best fit which should indicate a linear relationship between the square of the amplitude and energy. (1 mark), Learners should discuss why the rice moves more with higher amplitude taps and how the results show that energy is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude.(6 marks) (Amplitude is the maximum distance from the rest of a wave particle. When a balloon drum is tapped from a greater height, the balloon surface stretches more, creating a higher amplitude wave. This greater movement causes the rice grains to move a further distance. The movement of the rice serves as a visual representation of energy transferred. When the amplitude squared is plotted against the movement of the rice, a linear relationship is observed, indicating that as amplitude squared increases, the energy increases proportionally)
How does a change in amplitude affect the sound energy produced by the drum? (Higher amplitude vibrations result in an increase in sound energy and therefore louder sounds)
Instead of using rice, Learners could compare the sound energy created with different amplitude taps using a decibel meter e.g. ‘sound meter’
Identifying Wave Properties (2 pts per answer) The wavelength of the wave in the diagram above is given by letter _______.
Which of the sound waves shown would be loud, and which would be quiet?

Using Sound Waves to Identify Objects: Sound waves can be used to determine the distance to an object. Have Learners do research and prepare a one-page paper response that responds to the following questions(s). 1) What does the word SONAR stand for? 2) How does sonar generate images of objects? 3) What animals use sonar for navigation? 4) What are some examples of sonar being used for a) fishing and b) treasure hunting

Research Project: Ultrasound: Learners should do research and generate a poster that responds to the following questions: 1) Use a description and picture to explain how ultrasound technology works. (5 marks) 2) Explain how ultrasound is used in the medical field to yield important information. (5 marks) 3) Proper grammar and punctuation (2 marks)

inclusive_learning_strategies:
Introductory Conversation
“Learners tell me where you have seen waves?” (ocean, lakes, ponds. puddles, skipping rope, slinky®)

In science we have categorized waves.
Mechanical Waves and Electromagnetic Waves

Mechanical waves are waves that require a medium. This means that they have to have some sort of matter to travel through. These waves travel when molecules in the medium collide with each other passing on energy. One example of a mechanical wave is sound. Sound can travel through air, water, or solids, but it can't travel through a vacuum. It needs the medium to help it travel. Other examples include water waves, seismic waves, and waves traveling through a spring.

Electromagnetic waves are waves that can travel through a vacuum (empty space). They don't need a medium or matter. They travel through electrical and magnetic fields that are generated by charged particles. Examples of electromagnetic waves include light, microwaves, radio waves, and X-rays. Retrieved verbatim from: https://www.ducksters.com/science/physics/waves.php

Drawing Game: The teacher asks student A to turn and face the wall in the classroom while he/she secretly arranges a blue skipping rope on the floor in a perfect wave: The teacher then passes a red skipping rope and a measuring stick (ruler) to student A. The teacher asks student B to the front of the class to observe the blue skipping rope and passes them a measuring stick. Student B is tasked with describing the blue skipping rope configuration using measurements and explanations such that student A (without looking) can duplicate the blue skipping rope appearance with their red skipping rope.

Class Discussion: Why might this be difficult to describe? We don't have terms to describe parts of the wave. We are not sure which measurements to make. So class, it seems that having a “language” in science, one that labels particular parts of objects, could be very useful We see different types of waves everyday in our world, with new terms we can more easily describe them.
Learners will view the following video and record definitions of the terms: wave, wavelength ‘amplitude’, frequency, crest, trough, energy: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pXG8gmupZm8 (2.31 min)

Wave: A ripple/vibration that moves through a medium or space. It carries energy from one place to another. Wave amplitude: This is how high or low the wave goes from its resting position. It is the distance from the middle of the wave to the top (crest) or bottom (trough). Crest: The highest point on a transverse wave. Trough: The lowest point on a transverse wave. Frequency: How many waves go past a point in one second? Measured in hertz (Hz). Energy: The capacity to do work.

Teacher will use the following diagram to explain the concept of characteristics of a wave. Retrieved from: https://ilp-media.wgbh.org/filer_public/e9/62/e962c2fb-1cdd-4fc3-881c-8c1fd2af5b91/buac20-img-wavecharacteristics.png
Frequency: Another Property of Waves. How are the two waves below different?

Retrieved from: https://byjus.com/physics/frequency-and-wavelength/ (One wave with the shorter wavelength repeats itself more often in the same time) We would say that the shorter wavelength wave has a higher frequency because it completes a cycle of crest to trough more often in the same timeframe. Calculating Frequency of a Wave [Image of wave diagram with frequency calculation]

Retrieved from: https://www.nagwa.com/en/videos/498143526360/ Types of Mechanical Waves: Teacher demo Hello Learners I want to show you my new “slinky®” Let me show you two ways I can move the slinky®.

Retrieved from: https://faculty.wcas.northwestern.edu/infocom/Ideas/waves.html You can see there are two types of waves and they have very specific characteristics and names. Longitudinal waves are a type of mechanical wave that moves parallel (same direction) to the medium they travel through, like sound waves in air or spring waves in metal. Transversal waves are waves that oscillate perpendicular to the direction of a wave's advance. This means that the particles of the medium vibrate up and down or side to side, while the wave travels forward. Examples of transverse waves include surface ripples on water and electromagnetic waves such as radio and light. Reinforcing Wave Types with Technology Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7cDAYFTXq3E (0:24 mins)

Simulation: https://phet.colorado.edu/sims/html/wave-on-a-string/latest/wave-on-a-string_all.html select >oscillate< & >fixed end< Learners should observe what happens when they move the sliders for >amplitude< & >frequency<

Guided discussion: What causes vibrations that produce waves? (energy) What did you notice when you adjusted the amplitude of the waves in the simulation? (With increased amplitude the maximum distance from the rest point increases.) What do you think would happen to the amplitude if more energy is used to generate the wave? (The amplitude would increase) Wave Observations at the Beach Waves at the beach must originate from energy also. Questions: 1) If there is a breeze, we often see the waves get larger. It is fun to play in the waves and feel them crash against our bodies. As the waves get larger from more wind, do we feel more force of the waves on our body or less? (more- If we feel more force, then there must be more energy that is creating the wave)

Making Waves with a Rope: Linking Energy & Amplitude: Activity: At the front of the class have two children hold opposite ends of a skipping rope. Ask one child to create a small wave using only a small amount of energy. Now ask the same child to create a bigger wave by vibrating the rope. Ask children, which of these two situations required more energy. (the second larger amplitude wave) 2) When a large wave is created in the ocean from an earthquake (called a tsunami) it crashes into the shoreline. It disrupts the shoreline and destroys buildings. We say that a wave has a very large amplitude. Ask Learners how the energy of that wave compares to a typical day at the beach. (much larger amplitude created with much more energy)
Waves and Energy: Re http://The amplitude of a wave is related to the energy which it transports.

Mathematicians have determined that there is a relationship between the amplitude of the wave and the energy it carries. The amount of energy carried by a wave is related to the amplitude of the wave. A high-energy wave is characterized by a high amplitude; a low-energy wave is characterized by a low amplitude. The energy of the wave is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude. (E ∝ A²)

How Does Sound Travel?: Have one student knock on the side of a wooden desk. Let the second student place their ear close to the desk on the other side and observe the sound heard. Repeat but have the student place their ear directly on the desk. The vibration can travel through the solid wood desk. A high energy strike against the desk will sound the loudest. In fact, sound travels through any form of matter. > Sound is amplified the most (loudest) through solids as the particles are closest together. > When underwater objects vibrate, they create sound-pressure waves that alternately compress and decompress the water molecules as the sound wave travels through the sea. Sound waves radiate in all directions away from the source like ripples on the surface of a pond.

You may have heard someone try to speak after they have inhaled helium gas. Listen here: https://whyy-od.streamguys1.com/news/1106helium.mp3 (5:23 mins) It sounds different. Why? Usually, the sound waves your vocal cords produce travel through air in your voice box. But when they go through the helium that you've inhaled, they travel about three times faster. That's because helium is so much lighter than air. When sound waves speed up but their frequency stays the same, each wave stretches out (retrieved verbatim from: https://whyy.org/articles/why-exactly-does-helium-make-your-voice-sound-like-that/)

Water Waves and Energy: Activity: Materials: Bowl of water, assorted pebbles. Ask Learners to carry out the following experiments and draw conclusions: 1) Drop a large pebble and a small pebble from the same height. What do you notice about the waves created? (Large pebble higher waves). What does this mean about the energy of the falling pebble? (the larger pebble
has more energy and that is reflected in the amplitude of the wave on impact with the water)

2) Drop the larger pebble from two different heights (.5 meters and 1 metre). What do you notice about the waves (higher drop height causes bigger waves)? Which of the two heights likely involves more energy? (the higher pebble makes the bigger wave; I think it has more energy)
Using Waves to Transmit Sound: A Simple Phone We Can Build
Learners can design and build a simple amplifier and state how the amplitude affects the quality of the sound produced.
Materials: Two Styrofoam® cups, A piece of string, A pencil/pen, Scissors, Tape/glue

Instructions for making sound amplifier (as shown in the picture below): Use a pencil to poke a small hole in the center of the bottom of a Styrofoam® cup, Cut a string to a length of about 1 meter, Thread one end of the string through the hole in the cup. Tie a knot on the inside of the cup so that the string doesn’t fall back out through the hole.
Take a second cup and make a similar hole through the center at the bottom.
Pass the other end of the string through the hole in the second cup and tie a knot to secure it.
Hold the cups with the strings pulled taut between them
Have a student speak into one cup while the other listens.

Ask Learners to account for their observations. Why does this apparatus act as an amplifier? (When you speak into the cup, your voice creates waves that travel through the string to the second cup. The cups help the sound waves to spread out and get louder when they reach your ear.)
The first student will speak into the cup with a whisper, then a speaking voice and then a shout. A second student will note observations.

Discussion: Learners will explain what their observations tell about the relationship between amplitude and volume (Speaking louder into the first cup increases the amplitude of the sound waves produced. The louder sound heard in the second cup is due to higher energy carried by the sound waves. When the speaker speaks louder the amplitude of the sound waves increases and the energy transmitted through the string also increases)
Reviewing Connections Between Energy and Wave Amplitude with Technology (Kahoot®)
Learners can do the following Kahoot® on wave energy and amplitude https://create.kahoot.it/details/8c50e83c-2ce2-4257-9914-7be3d61d54ac

The Kahoot® includes the following questions: 1) What happens to the energy of a wave when its amplitude is increased? a) The energy decreases b) The energy remains the same c) The energy increases proportionally to the amplitude squared d) The energy increases proportionally to the amplitude Ans-c
2) If the amplitude of a wave is doubled, how does the energy of the wave change? a) The energy remains the same b) The energy is doubled c) The energy is halved d) The energy is quadrupled Ans-d

3) How is amplitude measured in a wave? a) By the distance between two consecutive waves b) By the height of the wave from the rest position c) By the speed at which the wave travels d) By the frequency of the wave Ans- b

4) Why does increasing the amplitude of a sound wave make it louder? a) It increases the frequency of the sound wave b) It decreases the speed of sound c) It increases the energy of the wave, leading to a louder sound d) It decreases the wavelength of the sound wave Ans -c

5) In a graph showing the square of the wave amplitude vs. energy, what shape should the graph show? a) A linear relationship b) A circular relationship c) No clear relationship d) A disorganized relationship Ans-a

Activity: Light reflection Learners to work in pairs Stand directly in front of a partner. Place a mirror on the ground between you and your partner. Shine a flashlight directly to your partner. Observe the image in the mirror.
Draw what you see
Write a short explanation of your findings. Explain why the image looks the way it does using the concept of light reflection

Rubric: Explanation of findings - Through explanation with correct use of scientific terms-(4 marks)
Rubric: Teamwork and collaboration-worked well with partner, showing excellent collaboration and communication- (2 marks)

In groups Learners are to create models describing how waves interact with various materials. Each group will be given a set of materials (e.g. flashlights, mirrors, gels of different colours, different fabrics, glass, water tanks, sand) and assigned a wave interaction to model (e.g. reflection, absorption, and transmission). Each group demonstrates their model to the class, explaining how it demonstrates wave behavior

Rubric: Clarity and accuracy- 5marks

Designing Your House: In groups Learners are to create a poster that shows the design of house with different types of windows (transparent, translucent and opaque) and responds to the following questions: 1) Why did you choose particular window types for different parts of the house? 2) How do does light interact with each type of window?

Everyday Uses of Waves: The Microwave Oven: Learners to observe the following video (How Do Microwave Ovens Work?) and use it to explain how the reflection, absorption, and transmission of microwaves help our food to cook. They will also explain other applications of microwaves e.g. mobile phones, Wi-Fi, medical treatment

Cooking food: Reflection-Microwaves bounce around from the walls of the microwave oven (2 marks), Transmission-Microwaves pass through containers and outer layers of food to penetrate to heat food evenly inside (2 marks), Absorption -food absorbs microwave energy and this is converted to heat which warms the food (2 marks)

Other applications: Mobile phones: Microwaves carry signals. These waves travel through the air to reach the person you’re calling or texting (2 marks), WiFi- Uses microwaves to transmit internet signals wirelessly. (2 marks), Medical treatments-Microwaves can be used to heat body tissues to treat muscle pain and some cancers. (2 marks)
To observe what happens when a wave approaches a barrier, note what happens when it reaches a boundary or passes through different substances.
Worksheet retrieved from: file:///C:/Users/Admin/Downloads/transmission-absorption-and-reflection-how-waves-interact-with-materials.pdf
(Completed worksheet)

Worksheet questions and answers: 1. Explain why sound waves are mechanical waves rather than electromagnetic waves. Sound waves are mechanical waves because they require a medium (such as air, water, or solid materials) to travel through. They are created by vibrations that cause particles in the medium to move and transfer energy. In contrast, electromagnetic waves (like light and radio waves) do not require a medium and can travel through a vacuum.

2. Cathy yells in a tunnel and hears her voice echo. Is this an example of transmission, absorption, or reflection? Explain. This is an example of reflection. The sound waves produced by Cathy's yell bounce off the walls of the tunnel and return to her ears, creating an echo.

3. When Gordon jumps into the pool, he creates waves that travel from one end of the pool to the other. Is this an example of transmission, absorption, or reflection? Explain. This is an example of transmission. The waves generated by Gordon's jump travel through the water from one end of the pool to the other. The energy of the waves is carried through the water.

4. Justin has a brother named Nick. Nick’s bedroom is directly above Justin’s bedroom. Before Nick had carpet installed in his bedroom, Justin could hear the sound of Nick singing as he practiced for chorus class each night. Once Nick’s room was carpeted, Justin could no longer hear Nick singing. Why? The carpet in Nick's room absorbs the sound waves. Before the carpet was installed, sound waves traveled easily through the floor, allowing Justin to hear Nick singing. The carpet material absorbs the sound energy, reducing the amount of sound that passes through the floor to Justin's room.

5. Justin also shares a bedroom wall with his sister Jennifer. From his bedroom, he can hear that Jennifer is playing the guitar, but the sound is muffled. Describe the interactions occurring between the sound waves and the wall. The sound waves from Jennifer's guitar are partially transmitted through the wall but are also absorbed and reflected by the wall. The wall material absorbs some of the sound energy, reducing the volume and clarity of the sound that reaches Justin's room, resulting in a muffled sound.

6. There is a giant oak tree in Justin’s backyard. On a sunny day, the leaves of the tree look green. Describe the interactions occurring between the light waves and the leaves of the tree. The green color of the leaves is due to the absorption and reflection of light waves. The leaves contain chlorophyll, which absorbs most wavelengths of light (such as red and blue) and reflects green wavelengths. This reflected green light is what makes the leaves appear green to our eyes.

7. Justin’s bedroom window is divided into four parts. In the afternoon, he notices that the sunlight creates a pattern of light and dark areas on the wall. Describe the interactions occurring between the light waves and the window. The pattern of light and dark areas is caused by transmission and shadow formation. The parts of the window that allow light to pass through create bright areas on the wall, while the frames or dividers block the light, creating shadows or dark areas. The light waves that pass through the glass are transmitted, while the parts of the window that do not allow light through create the pattern of light and dark areas on the wall.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Review of Wave Background Knowledge The teacher discusses the implications of waves and their interactions in everyday life with Learners. Ask Learners if they have heard the word wave before. Yes. What are waves? (A wave is a ripple/vibration that carries energy from one place to another. It can travel through a medium (like air and water) or through space. Particles of the medium may move back and forth but do not travel along with the wave.)
Learners are to observe the following video of sea waves: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vPhg6sc1Mk4 (1.0 min)

The teacher asks Learners: Why are these considered waves? (There is a vibration through the water that carries energy. The water particles move up and down and this movement makes the energy travel across the water.)
The teacher explains that there are many other types of waves, some that we can’t see with the naked eye. They all involve vibrations that carry energy.
What are some other types of waves? (microwaves, sound, light, seismic, radio waves)

How are some of those waves different from each other? (Some waves such as water waves, sound, and seismic waves require a medium for travel and are known as mechanical waves. Others, do not need a medium and can travel through a vacuum. These are electromagnetic waves e.g. light, radio, x-rays, microwaves)

Have we come across ways that other waves may affect us and ways that we use what we know about them to improve the quality of our lives? (Waves are all around us and wave interactions can be observed in everyday life: Sunglasses protect our eyes because they are made of a material that absorbs light waves, and some buildings reduce noise because they are made of material that absorbs sound waves. Ultrasound machines used by doctors use sound waves to create images of the inside of the body, we warm our food in a microwave oven when microwaves are absorbed by food causing molecules to vibrate and produce heat. Many other examples exist.)
Let us Review the Characteristics of a Wave: Learners to observe this video and define the terms that follow: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6h8JZ2SMmQ (2.58 min)
Wave: A ripple/vibration that moves through a medium or space. It carries energy from one place to another
Wavelength: This is the length of one wave cycle. It is the distance from one wave crest (top of the wave) to the next or one wave trough (bottom of the wave) to the next
Wave amplitude: This is how high or low the wave goes from its resting position. It is the distance from the middle of the wave to the top (crest) or bottom (trough)
Wave frequency: The number of wave cycles in a second
Period: The time for one complete wave cycle
The teacher will draw this diagram on the board for Learners and label it explaining each point in the diagram. Note: In this diagram 1 cycle is the frequency of the wave.
https://www.alanpedia.com/physics_waves/waves.html

Simulation of Waves & Energy Transfer: Learners are to do the following ‘wave on a string’ simulation: https://phet.colorado.edu/sims/html/wave-on-a-string/latest/wave-on-a-string_all.html. During the simulation, Learners should adjust the frequency and amplitude of the waves. Guided discussion: What did you notice when you adjusted the frequency and amplitude of the waves in the simulation? (With increased frequency the number of cycles per second increases. With increased amplitude the maximum distance from the rest point increases.) What has to happen for a wave to form? (A vibrating source/disturbance)

What would cause the source to vibrate? (Energy would be transferred to that source, e.g. from a person, wind) What happens because of this vibrating source? (Vibrations are passed along.) What happens because these vibrations are passed along? (Energy is transferred.) Do you always need matter/medium? (Not always: Light waves from the sun travel through space to Earth. Sound needs matter, water waves need water)

Using the Simulation to Investigate Properties of Waves: The teacher can demonstrate the properties showing the simulation on a projector or provide clear directions (a worksheet question prompt) so that Learners can investigate on their own.

Important Simulation notes for the teacher: To observe reflection, observe what happens to the wave when it reaches the barrier at the end; To change the medium that the wave is traveling through, adjust the tension button to simulate different mediums. The higher the tension, the denser the medium; Also, increased damping relates to increased absorption of the wave within a medium

Sound Waves Travel Through Air: Show Learners a drum with pieces of sand on the skin. Strike the drum. Ask Learners if they can hear it. (yes) Can you see the sand bouncing? (yes). That is because the drum sound results from the drum skin's vibration. If we could see the sound leaving the drum it would appear as a wave. But how do we hear that drum? (with our ears) But how does that happen? (not sure) Our ear also has a drum -called an eardrum. When the waves enter the ear, they vibrate the eardrum, and that signal goes to our brain. Some waves come rapidly, other waves come slowly; this allows our ear to distinguish different types of sound, for example, high pitch versus low pitch.

Mechanical Waves are Affected When they Travel Through a Medium: Does changing the medium affect wave speed? (yes, waves travel at different speeds in different media) Remind Learners of the property of refraction as light travels from air to water and seems to bend the pencil! What happens to a wave when it encounters a barrier? (When a wave encounters a barrier different phenomena can be observed, depending on the nature of the wave and the nature of the barrier: Learners, tell me what happens when sunlight encounters the following barriers: 1) A mirror? (It bounces back into our eyes so we can see our reflection.) 2) A glass window? (It passes through the window. We can see street lights through the window in our house) 3) A black car? (the car gets warmer inside because the black color absorbs all wavelengths of light and heats up) Let us formalize our definitions of these properties.

1) Reflection when the wave bounces off the barrier and moves off in the opposite direction. 2) Transmission when the wave passes through the substance and continues moving in the same direction. This occurs when the barrier allows the wave to pass without obstructing or absorbing it e.g. light waves through clear glass. 3) Absorption is when the barrier absorbs the wave's energy, reducing its (the wave's) intensity. The absorbed energy is converted into another form of energy such as heat e.g. sunlight being absorbed by a dark colored bottle. Reinforcing the Concepts: Dim the lights in the classroom and use a flashlight to shine a light on different objects e.g. a mirror, transparent plastic, and black paper. Learners will explain their observations. They will also explain how these activities demonstrate reflection, absorption, and transmission of light. Observations: 1. Mirror-Bright flashlight beam and their reflections are observed. This is because the mirror has a smooth, shiny surface which causes light to be bounced back or be reflected. 2. Transparent plastic-Objects behind the plastic become illuminated and can be seen. This is because the plastic is transparent, allowing most light to pass through or be transmitted. 3. Black paper-The paper appears dark and nothing behind it can be seen. After a while the paper may feel warm This is because the dark surface absorbs

How Can We Use the Reflection Property? Ask Learners if they have ever heard of a periscope. Periscopes are used in submarines so that you can see far above the surface of the water without the submarine being above the water line. This is in part a defense against ships that might see the submarine in wartime and attack.

Activity: How Does Light Travel? Cover a comb with black paper except for the central slit. Place the comb perpendicular to a white manilla sheet. Shine a searchlight through the opening. Adjust until the beam is seen on the paper. Place a mirror in its path and see what happens to the beam of light.

Key Points to ask Learners about as they observe: Light travels in straight lines. When light hits a mirror it bounces off. Light can be reflected (bounced off surfaces). The reflection of light allows us to see objects and images by bouncing light into our eyes.

Show Learners the diagram of the periscope and ask them to explain how it might work. (light above the water enters and reflects off one mirror at “a” to direct it downward and then reflects again at a second mirror to enter the eye of the observer at c)

Learners are to observe the following pictures and state why there seems to be a picture of the mountain in the lake: Sunlight bounces off the mountain and travels to the lake. Some of the light bounces off (reflects off) the lake, like light reflecting off a mirror. The reflected light travels to your eye
and your brain creates an upside-down image just like a mirror

How Do Water Waves Behave? The Learners are to observe the following video and indicate what happens when the water waves hit the wall: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oKsiXdcgYHs (0.20 mins) When the ocean meets the shore or encounters a barrier the “water wave” gets reflected and bounces off in another direction to which it approaches the structure. This may cause chaotic or irregular wave patterns. Next time you visit the beach look out for reflection and notice the pattern in the water at the shoreline.

How Does Reflection Change Depending on the Barrier? Completely smooth surfaces like a mirror will reflect light in a specific direction. Rougher surfaces scatter light in many different directions – this is called a diffuse reflection. If we take two tubes and position them against a wall, as shown in the figure above, upon placing a speaker or any other source of sound near one end of the tube, we receive the sound at the end of the other tube. This activity proves that the surface of wall reflects the sound wave.
Retrieved from: https://byjus.com/physics/sound-reflection/

Absorption of Sunlight Waves Ask Learners if they have ever noticed a difference in the inside temperature of cars on hot days? As an experiment, paint one 2L plastic pop bottle with white paint and one 2L bottle with black paint. After the painted bottles have dried, fill them both with water. Place the two bottles in direct sunlight for 2 hours. Use a thermometer to take the temperature of the two bottles. Ask Learners which bottle is the warmest. (They will find the black bottle is warmest) Ask them why it is the warmest. (The black color absorbs sun rays while the white bottle reflects them therefore the water in the black bottle will be warmer.) Ask Learners: In the absence of air conditioning, which color car will be the cooler car in the hot sun? (white car will reflect sunlight and stay cool; black car will get hot inside)

Transmission of waves: Transparent versus Translucent Windows Learners will observe the following window and explain the difference in what they observe between the top and bottom, and the reason for their explanations:

Many windows in a house will allow light to pass freely and therefore we can easily see through them. These are said to be transparent (The top part of the window). Glass and some plastic may be transparent. Some windows are said to be translucent because they diffuse (reflect in different directions) most of the light. Materials like frosted glass and some plastics are called translucent. They only a small amount of light pass through so that you don’t see easily through the window (the bottom part of the window). What is different with this window?

Opaque objects like the window above block light from traveling through them. Most of the light is either reflected by the object or absorbed and converted to thermal energy. Materials such as wood, stone, and metals are opaque to visible light.

What does sound need in order to be transferred as a wave? We have already seen that sound readily transfers through air (to our ears) and water ((whale talk- see: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WabT1L-nN-E (2:57 mins)). Have Learners watch the following video and explain why they can’t hear the sound? https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jMEgzsmJdmQ&feature=youtu.be (1:01 mins) (When air is removed from a system to generate a vacuum, the sound wave has no wave medium in which to travel so we don’t hear anything.)

What would you predict would happen if you pass a light through a vacuum chamber? (it would shine through). In fact, light will easily pass through a vacuum. This is because light sometimes acts like a wave but also acts as a particle and with nothing in its way it can pass through the vacuum. Think of the analogy of a spaceship as a discrete particle. Once it is given enough velocity to leave the Earth, it readily passes through space which is the biggest vacuum!
Have Learners watch the following video and then explain why sound travels faster in water compared with air.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AxNdr0Bcx20 (6:22 mins)
An explanation: the relay race
Sound travels by particles bumping into each other as they vibrate. It is a little like a relay race – each runner holds a little bit of information (the baton), and when they make contact with the next runner, they pass the information on.

In the case of sound, the runners are particles and the information (baton) they are passing along is energy of vibration. In a sound wave, a particle picks up some energy and keeps it until it bumps into a neighbouring particle. The next particle will then pick up the energy and transfer it to the next one in the chain. This happens extremely fast and is detected as a wave of pressure.

Sound won’t travel in a vacuum because there are no particles to bump together to transmit the vibration.

In this experiment, Learners will use a balloon rocket to test Newton’s third law: Materials: Balloons, Balloon pump, String (fishing line) about 15 m long (35-40 feet), Bag clip, Drinking straw, Cloth measuring tape, Scissors

Instructions: Find an area where you can stretch out a string to a length of 10-15 meters. Hook one end of the string around a solid support. A member of your team will hold the other end tight when you are ready to launch your balloon. Partially inflate the balloon and tape a short piece of string from the nozzle to the centre spot on the bottom side. Draw a straight line following the string. Find the largest circumference and mark a straight line. Cut the straw in half and pull the loose end of the string through it. Mark a starting point with a piece of tape, close to the end of the string from which you will launch your balloon rocket. Attach the balloon to the drinking straw with tape, following the guide marks you drew before. The balloon should hang below the string. When you are ready, countdown and launch your balloon. Write a sentence or two to summarize the findings of your experiment in terms of Newton’s Third Law, in your own words.

Protecting the Egg: An Engineering Challenge. In groups of 4-5, Learners will plan, design, build and test cars that can minimize the damage of collision. Materials: Wheels: round objects like bottle caps or CDs, or you can cut your wheels from foam board. Axles: straws, pencils, wooden skewers, paper rolled into tubes, etc. Chassis (body): plastic bottles, cardboard boxes, Styrofoam, cardboard, etc. Other construction supplies: scissors, tape, glue, rubber bands, paper clips, etc. You will also need materials to build a ramp to roll the cars down: Wooden board, large piece of corrugated cardboard, or foam core board, at least 1 foot wide and 3 feet long. A small box or stack of books to prop up one end of the ramp, at least 1 foot tall. Learners will create and build a toy car large enough to hold an egg. Using a wooden box and books Learners will create an inclined plane/ramp. A brick or hard object should be placed at the end of the ramp and Learners will be allowed to roll the car down the ramp and strike the wall. Using duct tape or glue, Learners can place any material in their car to form a bumper. Each group
must choose a different material to test which one is best to protect the egg from breaking. Learners should note that the bumper is used to absorb the equal and opposite force exerted by the wall.
