inclusive_learning_strategies:
Recognizing Forces That Move Us
Learners, I am sure you have all gone for a swim in a pool or the ocean before. How do you move forward? (By cupping our hands and pushing against the water). Did you realize that the reason you actually move ahead is because the water is pushing back on your hands in the opposite direction? Today we are going to introduce some ideas about motion we probably have never thought about before; mostly because we live them everyday and don’t really consider their importance! I am going to introduce to you a man named Isaac Newton. He was a scientist and mathematician who was very important for explaining gravity and laws of motion.

Newton was a scientist who developed important laws of motion based on his experiments: he suggested:
In the first law, an object will not change its motion unless a force acts on it.
In the second law, the force on an object is equal to its mass times its acceleration.
In the third law, when two objects interact, they apply forces to each other of equal magnitude and opposite direction.
Retrieved from: https://geniebook.com/tuition/secondary-3/physics/how-newtons-laws-motion-impact-real-life-exploring-fun-and-engaging-examples
Today we are going to focus on his third law.
Newton's Third Law
Forces always Come in Pairs: You Push on a Wall the Wall Pushes Back
Retrieved from: https://stickmanphysics.com/stickman-physics-home/forces/newtons-third-law-of-motion-action-reaction-pairs/

The above is called a free-body diagram, Free-body diagrams are diagrams used to show the relative magnitude and direction of all forces acting upon an object in a given situation. For Newton’s 3rd Law we can draw opposing forces “action and reaction” forces.
See also: https://www.physicsclassroom.com/class/newtlaws/Lesson-2/Drawing-Free-Body-Diagrams
Identifying the Opposing Forces

Learners I wonder if you can help me understand something that happen to my friend. He was bringing his boat into the wharf and stopped his motor and drifted. But he wasn’t quite close enough, so he stretched out his leg towards the wharf and pushed off from the boat. Much to his surprise, as soon as he pushed to reach the wharf with his foot, the boat moved backwards in the opposite direction! You know what happened next! He fell in the water.
I wonder if we can use Newton’s 3rd law to explain what happened. Maybe this diagram might help you?
(As the person pushes forward there is an equal and opposite force backward so the boat moved out from under the man)
Retrieved from: https://www.wired.com/2013/10/a-closer-look-at-newtons-third-law/
Teacher Demonstration
Place a pair of children 1 meter apart in the classroom and ask them to hold their position facing each other without moving their feet.
Pass student 1 a heavy ball. Ask them to toss the ball to the other student 2. After student 2 catches the ball ask the Learners in the class questions:

1) What did you notice about student 1’s stance as they threw the ball towards student 2? (As they threw the ball, their own body moved backwards) So they exerted a force to move the ball forward but the ball pushed them back in the opposite direction

2) What did you notice about the stance that student 2 took as they received the ball (student 2 seemed to lean towards student 1 as if they were expecting the ball to hit them with force). 3) Student 2, what did you do to catch the ball without moving your feet? (I exerted a force in the direction of student 1 so the impact of the ball would not make me move my feet) In order to keep their feet from moving, student 2 had to push forward as they received the ball because of its incoming force at point of contact. Maybe this diagram will provide a good review of this demonstration.

Another example… It is probably difficult to imagine, but when you push off the ground with your foot to propel your skateboard forward, you are putting force on the ground and Newton’s 3d Law says that the ground is pushing back with an equal force. Action & Reaction: Collisions. What happens when we apply Newton’s third law to colliding objects? When a car hits a wall or another vehicle there is an equal and opposite force pushing back! That force is often felt by the bumper and is reflected in great damage and loss of energy through friction and heat generated.

How can we use Newton’s third law to make cars safer? We need design features in cars that absorb the force of impact. Learners, identify some things that have been incorporated in cars that provide the opposing force to the impact of a collision. (foam bumpers, collapsible metal, air bags, seat belts). Here is a picture of some new car features. Establishing background knowledge. Pose this scenario to Learners: If you hold a metal rod very firmly and strike it on a rock, what will it feel like on your hands? (the metal will vibrate and hurt my hands). Then ask, “If you wear gloves or wrap the handle-end of the rod in a cloth, how will that change the experience?”
(It won’t hurt so much because the layer between my hand and the rod offers a cushion).

Explain to Learners…
In the old days, cars were designed with heavy metal bumpers to avoid damage upon a collision. What do we see now? (plastic and foam bumpers)
Learners, why do you think they have changed the bumper style? (the foam and plastic cushion the car and driver instead of absorbing all the force of the collision)
Cars are now designed entirely so that the equal and opposite force of a collision is absorbed.
Ask Learners what happens if they are the driver of a car and they abruptly collide with another car or a pole or wall. (you are thrown forward very quickly).

Then ask them “How is the car designed to help you from getting injured as the force of the wall and car coming together is distributed to you moving forward? (Airbags inflate to cushion, and a seat belt restricts your motion forward)
Explain to Learners that even more consideration is given to the design of cars.

Retrieved from:
https://www.bbc.co.uk/bitesize/guides/zgn82hv/revision/11#:~:text=When%20the%20car%20crashes%2C%20there,controlled%20deceleration%20of%20the%20person

Can you identify Opposing Forces?
(formative assessment within class)
In the following two pictures, I want you to identify the opposing forces
1) A bird stays airborne by flapping their wings

Retrieved from:
https://stock.adobe.com/search?k=%22bird+flapping+wings%22

A bird flies through the air by flapping its wings and pushing the air downwards.
According to Newton’s third law, for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. Do you think the air has an equal force acting on the bird? In what direction is this force acting?
(The size of the force on the air equals the size of the force on the bird; the direction of the force on the air (downwards) is opposite the direction of the force on the bird (upwards))
2) While driving down the road, a firefly strikes the windshield of a bus and makes an obvious mess in front of the face of the driver
Retrieved from: https://slideplayer.com/slide/15063990/

While driving down the road, a firefly strikes the windshield of a bus and makes a quite obvious mess in front of the face of the driver. This is a clear case of Newton’s third law of motion. The firefly hit the bus and the bus hit the firefly. Which of the two forces is greater: the force on the firefly or the force on the bus? (Each force is the same size.)
Newton’s 3d Law of Motion and Space
Retrieved from: https://edu.gcfglobal.org/en/newtons-laws-of-motion/newtons-third-law/1/

Discussion
Newton’s third law states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. In space there is no gravity so astronauts tend to float around in the spaceship.

1) Do you think the 3rd law applies to astronauts in space? Yes it does, astronauts struggle with the law because there is no gravity in space and thus, their every action has greater opposite reactions. For example, if an astronaut pushes on a surface they are propelled in the opposite direction. In outer space, astronauts use backpack jet packs that expel a gas. Without friction the astronaut moves easily in the opposite direction.

2) Simple tasks on earth such as moving boxes and using a screwdriver are much harder for an astronaut in space where there is no gravity. What do you think would happen to an astronaut trying to use a screwdriver in space? (due to the lack of gravity and Newton’s third law, the astronaut would find himself turning or twisting if he is not anchored to a wall)

3) Describe what you think would happen to an astronaut freely floating in space and pushing a small box away from himself. (Despite having different masses, the astronaut and the box would both apply an equal and opposite force onto each other and move in opposite directions. Since there is no gravity and no ground to push against; both objects would simply float away. The box would move much faster since it has a smaller mass). The picture below shows what is likely to happen in terms of forces.
Retrieved from: https://link.springer.com/referenceworkentry/10.1007/978-3-319-55065-7_1081

Let's visualise this space concept using a video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZkVU-bj9bDk (3:07 mins) Think about the following questions as you watch. 1. What did you observe once the astronaut applied a force to the basketball in microgravity? (due to the difference in masses, the basketball accelerated much faster than the astronaut although an equal and opposite force was applied) 2. What did you observe once the astronaut applied a force to an astronaut of a similar mass? (both astronauts accelerated in opposite directions)
Reviewing and Supplementing Concepts with Video Ask Learners to watch video and write down answers to the following questions as they watch.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-wh3fJRdjo (4:25 mins) 1. State Newton’s third law (for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction) 2. Describe the action and reaction forces of a rocket being launched (the rocket exhaust moves downwards while the rocket moves upwards) 3. Describe the action and reaction forces of a cannon being fired (the cannon's backward movement is the reaction to the action of shooting a cannonball) 4. What did Newton observe about the magnitude of action and reaction forces? (action and reaction forces are always equal) 5. What is the magnitude of the reaction force if a skater pushes off the ground with 15 Newtons of force? (the ground pushes back with a reaction force of 15N) 6. Describe the effect of friction on a moving body (frictional forces would move in the opposite direction of the moving body causing more energy to be used) 7. Describe three examples of action and reaction forces which you may encounter in your everyday life.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: ELO 2: Plan an investigation to provide evidence that the change in an object’s motion depends on the sum of the forces on the object and the mass of the object.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: force, balanced / unbalanced force, mass, Newton (N), pull/push, speed/velocity, inertia, motion, friction, gravity, momentum, acceleration, elastic collision, inelastic collision, direction/position, net force, Newton’s laws
Demonstrate that an object in motion stays in motion and an object at rest remains at rest with the same speed and same direction unless acted upon by a force.
Compare how the mass and speed of objects upon impact will determine in what direction they will move.
Explain that a bigger and heavier object has greater inertia and requires more force to move it
Distinguish elastic and inelastic collisions qualitatively (bouncy vs. sticky)
Predict the outcome of collisions.
Calculate net force for simple aligned or opposing forces.
Observe the mass of objects, the speed at which they move and direction these objects end up after a collision.
Infer what will happen if two objects of the same mass, moving at same speed in opposite directions collide.
Predict the direction an object will go with different mass, velocity and moving in the opposite direction upon collision.
Carry out experiments to develop an intuition that both the mass and the speed will impact where the joined mass will end up after they collide.
Collect data from experiments. Record, analyse and interpret the data to make predictions on balanced and unbalanced forces.
Develop curiosity and persistency for science facts in recognizing the importance of forces in our everyday life.
Work collaboratively/cooperatively with peers on classroom activities and discussions on balanced/ unbalanced forces.
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and help peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
When conducting practical and group work, display respect and practice safety precautions.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Objects at Rest and in Motion: A cyclist uses force to pedal his bicycle and increase its speed. A man uses force to move a stationary lawnmower. A footballer uses force to change the direction of motion of a ball. A baker uses force on his dough to shape it into a curry puff. Retrieved from https://www.crestolympiads.com/topic/class-5-force-and-its-types

Effects of forces on objects at rest. Learners are presented with the diagram above depicting the effects of forces on objects at rest. They will answer the following questions. 1. What did the cyclist do to get the bicycle to move? (He had to use force to pedal the bicycles.) 2. How did the lawnmower get the machine to move? (He had to push it forward.) 3. How did the boy get the stationary ball to move? (The stationary ball moved after the boy had kicked it.) 4. How did the baker get the desired shape he wanted using the dough? (he applied force by kneading it)

Forces Vocabulary: Learners can practice vocabulary by completing this drag and drop quiz at the following site: https://wordwall.net/resource/10259205/forcesvocabularyhttps://wordwall.net/resource/10259205/forces-vocabulary

Answers: The spot where an object is located. A force that can be a push or a pull depending on how the poles are located. A forces that moves an object CLOSER to something. A force (pull) that attracts a smaller object to a larger object. A force that moves an object AWAY from something. A push or pull that causes an object to change direction or position. A force (push) that slows or stops an objection in motion. when forces equal zero and result in no motion. when forces result in motion because net force is not zero. the sum of all forces in a system.

Net Force: The net force of an interaction is the overall force that is felt when forces are combined. Combine the following forces appropriately to generate a net force. 1) A car is moving East with a force of 40 N. A truck, also moving east, with a force of 70 N collides with the rear of the car. Assuming an inelastic collision, what is the net force? (110 Newtons in the easterly direction) 2) A car is moving East with a force of 20 N when it collides head-on with a truck moving West with a force of 30 N. Assuming an inelastic collision, what is the net force? (10 Newtons West)

Billiards: Ask Learners to use their simulation to explain what should happen in the following scenario. When a blue billiard ball is shot directly at a stationary red ball, what should happen to both balls? The red ball moves on impact at the same speed as the incoming blue ball and the blue ball stops. The balls are both the same mass, so the momentum of the incoming blue ball is transferred to the red ball and it travels in the same direction.

Cricket: Cricket Bats typically weigh from 2 lb 7 oz to 3 lb (1.2 to 1.4 kg) though there is no standard. Ask Learners which bat would be able to hit the ball further if they were both swung at the same speed by the same person? They can use their experience and simulations to answer the question. (The heavier bat will transfer more momentum to the ball so it should go further).

Interpreting an Accident: The following is a report of an accident between a motorcycle and a bus in St. Kitts. Ask Learners to read it, and, in a one-page explanation (including a picture) explain why the motorcycle and driver are most likely to sustain the most injury in such a collision? The mass of the bus is much larger than the motorcycle, so even if the bus is traveling slower than the motorcycle, the momentum it has will cause great damage to the motorcycle and driver. Learners can check this with the online simulation. Rubric: explanation 5 marks/ drawing 5 marks/ grammar and punctuation 5 marks. The teacher may choose to engage a discussion of the dangers of riding motorcycles in the company of bigger vehicles on the road.

Newtons Laws in Space: An Essay: The lack of gravity in the space station orbiting earth provides for opportunities for astronauts to test Newtons Laws of Motion. Learners should review the following websites and write a one-page

review of Newton’s Laws in space. The questions to be answered in their essay: A) What are Newton’s Laws B) What aspect of the nature of life on the space station make it different from applying the laws on earth? C) What precautions do astronauts need to take while living in this environment? See: https://artsandculture.google.com/story/newton%E2%80%99s-laws-of-motion-in-space-nasa/LQVBeOIYpBmaBQ?hl=en https://www.esa.int/Science_Exploration/Human_and_Robotic_Exploration/Astronauts/Learning_to_live_with_the_laws_of_motion. In order for a space rocket to leave the earth’s orbit it must expend vast amounts of fuel to escape earth’s force of gravity (25000 miles per hour). This is called the escape velocity. Once in space beyond the strongest effects of earth’s gravitational force, the apparent lack of particles within the vacuum of space would suggest that a rocket in motion will remain in motion forever with no loss of speed. There is however, small amounts of hydrogen in space that potentially could minimally change rocket speed and direction.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Introductory Activity: Good morning class, you have learned about forces before. They can be a push or a pull. Today we will look at how forces can interact. Let us start with a familiar game, tug of war.

Tug of War and Forces: Learners, have you ever chosen teams for a tug of war game with a rope? If you want to choose a winning team, what characteristics would you look for in your team? (strong pullers, many pullers and pullers with large mass that might be hard to move). Since we are trying to move the rope to our team’s side, we are looking to have the greater force pulling. As far as the rope is concerned, we would call this an unbalanced force on the rope overall. Another example would be the unbalanced force in the seesaw below. The seesaw is not parallel with the ground; instead one person is lower because gravity is acting on their greater mass to apply a greater force.
Retrieved from: https://www.archiexpo.com/prod/kompan/product-51344-421183.html

If I were to ask you how you might balance the teams so the rope didn’t actually move one side to the other but stayed more or less in the center…how would you choose teams? (equal number of people, equal ability to pull, equal mass of people). That would be difficult to arrange but… we would say that this configuration should result in a balanced force on the rope.
What would a balanced force look like on a seesaw? (The two people would be directly across from each other and the seesaw would be parallel with the ground.)
As a review, let me show a picture of what we have been talking about. Balanced force top picture-unbalanced bottom picture.
Retrieved from: https://theory.labster.com/balanced-and-unbalanced-forces/ Let us pick out some other unbalanced forces in the following picture.

Retrieved from: https://blogs.scarsdaleschools.org/cboyer/2019/10/10/balanced-and-unbalanced-forces/ Teacher Note: If you have access to technology, you may reinforce the concepts above using the following video for review: http://youtube.com/watch?v=q6NRmrknPh0&feature=shared (5:17 mins) Effects of Forces 1. Moving a stationary object: When a force is applied to an object that is not moving, it can make the object start moving. For instance, pushing a car that is not moving can make it start rolling.
2. Stopping a moving object: Force can also be used to stop an object that is already in motion. Imagine applying brakes to a bicycle or a car to slow it down and bring it to a stop.

3. Changing the speed of a moving object: When a force is applied to an object that is already moving, it can either make it go faster or slow it down. For example, pushing a swing can make it move faster while using the brakes on a bicycle can make it slow down.

4. Changing the direction of a moving object: If a force is applied to an object at an angle to its current path, it can cause the object to change its direction. Think of kicking a soccer ball to make it go in a different direction.
5. Changing the shape and size of an object: Forces can also cause objects to change their shape or size. For instance, if you squeeze a soft ball, it will temporarily change its shape.

Newton was a scientist who suggested that motion that occurred as a result of a force followed a particular equation. Newton said that Force = Mass x Acceleration (F=MA) We see from the equation that forces have a strong and direct relationship to mass of the object. From earlier studies we know that acceleration is the change in speed. Supporting resource: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EJkPF21tORE (5:44 mins)

In simple language, Newton said an object will accelerate in the direction that a net force is acting on it. This is no surprise to us because when we push or pull, we expect the object to move in the desired direction! Collisions are a Unique Example of Forces Interacting Ask Learners to give you words that might be used to describe a collision. (Crash, impact, loud, smash, thud, creak, bend etc.). Then, ask them for a definition that includes any objects not just vehicles. In creating that definition, ask them to reflect on past lessons, the idea that objects don’t move unless a force is applied to them. A collision is the event in which two or more bodies exert forces on each other in about a relatively short time. Ask Learners if they have ever seen a collision between cars or trucks. Looking for background knowledge, ask them a few focusing questions: How large were the vehicles? Did they move after they collided? Where did they end up relative to the collision? How fast do you think they were going? Were they going the same speed? Was it a head-on collision or from the side?

Retrieved from: https://orlandoautobody.com/car-accidents/statistics-and-costs-of-a-car-accident/ Explain that the car engine provides the force for the car to move. When the car collides with another car with its own force that the motion that happens on impact depends on the initial forces of the cars. It is important to note that there are two types of collisions: An elastic, or 'bouncy' collision is one where kinetic energy is conserved, KE is the same before and after a collision- the objects bounce off each other like billiard balls colliding By contrast, in an inelastic, or 'sticky' collision, kinetic energy is NOT conserved; instead some energy is absorbed by the surroundings (example bending metal, heat). In a perfectly inelastic collision both objects stick together and move forward as one. Retrieved, in part from: https://study.com/learn/lesson/elastic-vs-inelastic-collision-theories-examples.html NOTE: There are examples of collisions where the colliding objects don’t stick together but are still considered inelastic. Simulating an Inelastic Collision in the Classroom After they have shared their prior knowledge, ask them to think about what factors might affect where the vehicles might end up after a head on collision (i.e. relative to where they impacted). (speed of vehicles, mass of vehicles, direction of vehicles)

Explain that when we talk about an object of mass M moving at a velocity V we say it has momentum. If a heavy ball is thrown to you to catch, it has momentum which you feel when you catch it as a result of the velocity it is traveling and the mass that it has. The teacher will clear off a large smooth desk at the front of the classroom. The teacher will choose in advance a heavy textbook and 2 noticeably lighter textbooks. For best results, the teacher should place two-sided tape or Velcro® on the spine of the books. Pairs of Learners will come to the front of the class and stand at opposite ends of the table each taking one of the books. The teacher will instruct the Learners that each of these books represents a vehicle. They will then each give a single push of the books across the table directly at each other with the book spines pointed at each other, so they stick together on contact. Learners in the class will observe the results. The key focus question is where do joined books end up relative to the impact location and why? This simulation will be used to help Learners answer questions about the following scenario. NOTE: possible variations to classroom sim 1) teachers may also have access to toy cars that can accomplish the same simulation 2) the following web-based electronic simulation will allow Learners to manage variables(http://www.mrmont.com/games/carcollision.html) 3) https://www.physicsclassroom.com/Physics-Interactives/Momentum-and-

Collisions/Collision-Carts/Collision-Carts-Interactive NOTE: Assume inelastic collisions are selected in these electronic simulations. Some suggestions: Change masses of vehicles; Set bumpers to clay so that cars stick together on impact; Set velocities as positive and negative such that cars will collide. Use one of these simulations above to have Learners discuss what will happen to the two vehicles under the following inelastic conditions: Learners should predict the result before simulating the scenario. travelling same speed opposite directions (to the right of the impact site) b) Heavy white truck traveling slow and light red truck traveling fast in opposite direction (perhaps close to the impact site) c) Light white truck traveling fast and light red truck traveling slow in opposite direction (to the right of the impact site) d) Light white truck traveling slow and light red truck traveling slow in opposite direction (perhaps close to the impact site)

The idea is that Learners develop an intuition that both the mass and the speed will impact where the joined mass will end up after they collide. Teacher Note: In future grades they will calculate resultant position and velocity using the conservation of momentum concept but for now an intuition about the collision is most important. In other words, they come to know that mass and velocity are the controlling predictors of what will happen. Elastic Collisions You have perhaps seen Newton’s cradle. Retrieved from: https://science.howstuffworks.com/innovation/inventions/newtons-cradle.htm The energy of the collision is passed on to the next ball as they swing downward. However, the loss of energy to heat (friction) eventually brings the balls to a stop so this is not a purely elastic collision.
See one in motion here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_cradle

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: ELO 3: Ask questions about data to determine the factors that affect the strength of electric and magnetic forces

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: magnetic forces, electromagnet, attraction, repulsion, electric current, magnetic poles, magnetic field, compass
Differentiate/Compare permanent magnets and electromagnets.
Explain how electromagnets work.
Identify uses of electromagnets.
Determine the factors that affect the strength of an electromagnet.
Design or engineer an electromagnet prototype.
Create a data table to show how the addition of turns of wire on a nail affects the magnetic field's strength.
Observe and describe ways in which the strength of an electromagnet can be altered.
Collect data from experiments. They will record, analyse and interpret the data to make predictions on the strength of electromagnets.
Plot graphs of electromagnetic strength
Interpret & extrapolate an electromagnetic strength graph
Research and communicate an example of an advanced application of electromagnets.
Appreciate the wide-spread importance of electromagnetism applications to solve real human problems of adaptation.
Work collaboratively/cooperatively with peers on classroom activities.
Learners will develop interest and curiosity about electromagnetism by asking questions and conducting experiments.
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and help peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
When conducting practical and group work, display respect and practice safety precautions.
Participate actively in classroom discussions.
Consider careers that build upon ideas developed in study of electromagnets.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Comprehension: An electromagnet is different form a regular magnet. In a one-page paper, explain, with a diagram, how an electromagnet works.

True or False?: 1. An electromagnet can generate a stronger magnetic field than a regular magnet. (T), 2. I would expect the electromagnet’s strength to depend on the size (voltage) of the battery because of the current flow generated. (T), 3. The number of windings on the nail had no impact on the magnetic attraction of the paper clips. (F), 4. Electromagnets at junk yards require low voltages to lift heavy items like cars. (F), 5. Electromagnets are very useful because they can be turned on and off. (T)
Major Project: Applications of Electromagnetism: Learners can choose to do research on one of two applications of electromagnets.
1. Electric motors: Primary source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CWulQ1ZSE3c (10:03 mins)
2. Maglev trains: Primary Source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8os4A4li674 (5:39 mins)
A poster should be created that includes a diagram and explanation of the application of electromagnets to make a useful technology.

Rubric: Clear labelled diagram (5 marks), Logical and clear explanation of how electromagnetism is applied. (5 marks), Timeline of technological development (3 marks), Utility of the technology to humans (2 marks), Grammar & punctuation (5 marks)
inclusive_learning_strategies: Invisible Force: Pupils are presented with the picture below (Floating Magnets) and are asked why do you think these magnets are floating in the air? (the poles of the magnets are aligned to repel each other and there is enough force to keep them suspended in air)
Retrieved from: https://www.sciencebuddies.org/Y5App22-p0GkaMmptLk_xcR9EfE=/569x520/-/https/www.sciencebuddies.org/cdn/Files/16779/4/magnetism-stem-floating-magnet.png
Now show the Learners this picture of a crane lifting a car engine. Learners how do you think this crane is able to lift this heavy engine?
Retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2EooyeAvPOM (1:34 mins)
Perhaps you thought it was a clamp or wire but here is another example of crane lifting a car. The end of the crane seems to be flat on top.
Retrieved from: https://mammothmemory.net/images/user/base/Physics/Electromagnetism/uses-of-electromagnetism-62.9252f33.jpg
Well in both cases the engine and the car are made of steel so you might have guessed it was a magnet attracting the metal.
Learners, can you give me some examples of magnets around you that attract metal? (fridge magnet, horse shoe magnet, cupboard door magnet, flashlight magnet)

Teacher Demonstration: if we put two magnets together does it increase the strength of attraction and repulsion? Let us try with two identical magnets together and an iron nail. Yes, the attraction of the nail is greater with multiple magnets. There are also very strong magnets called rare earth magnets made from special elements. These are common for embedding in cupboard doors to complete their closing

Learners, do you think regular magnets and rare earth magnets are strong enough to lift large objects like engines and cars? (No, probably not). No, they are not. The crane magnets are special magnets where the magnetic field is created by electric current flow. That is why they are called electromagnets. Because the magnetic attraction is generated by electricity, the magnet can be turned on and off- that is how the crane drops the metal when it has moved it to a new location.

How Can we Generate a Magnetic Attraction? Teacher demonstration: Materials: Compass, Nail, Wire, Batteries (1.5 V and 9V), Switch (optional). The teacher can make an electromagnet in advance and show how the magnetic field of the
electromagnet deflects a compass needle by connecting and disconnecting the wire.
This is an electromagnet, and it creates what we call a magnetic field around the nail when we add electric current to wrapped wire using a battery.
Teacher note: You can place a piece of paper with iron filings above the nail and show the magnetic field!

Testing The Electromagnet
Each student group will make an electromagnet with the available materials.
Learners are expected to create a formal report from the findings of their electromagnet experiment. It should include a diagram (5 marks, experimental data (5 marks), graph (5 marks) and answers to experimental questions (5 marks) (see below)
NOTE: To begin, the teacher can assist them to create an electromagnet with 10 turns of wire on their nail.
Ask the Learners to configure their electromagnet so the number of turns of wire around the nail corresponds to the table below. For each new electromagnet they make, have them record how many paper clips the magnet can lift at one time. They should add these results to the table
Ask them what they can conclude about the number of windings around the nail, the number of paper clips lifted and the strength of the magnetic foiled? (more windings lift more clips, therefore a stronger electromagnet)

This is called a controlled experiment because all variables (nail, battery, wire type, paper clips) are staying the same except the number of turns of wire. In that way, you can attribute the differences to only the wire turns.
a. Ask Learners to plot a graph of paper clips (y axis) vs. # turns (x axis)
b. From the graph ask them to extrapolate by extending the trend line in the graph to predict how many clips might 100 turns lift?
Ask them to predict what might happen if they use a bigger battery and explain why?
Note: (If the teacher has access to a bigger battery or more batteries in series- try it and see!) (more voltage from the battery(s) will increase current flow and therefore the electromagnet will be stronger)

Testing Design:
If the battery is hooked up to the coil in the opposite direction, does it affect the strength of the magnet? Have Learners test this with their electromagnet and paper clips. (the current is reversed but the amount of current doesn't change-should be the same magnetic strength)

Possible experimental extension
If the nail is changed from steel to galvanized steel to copper does it affect the magnet strength? Have Learners test this and report back which is the strongest magnet.

A typical junkyard magnet operates on 250 volts. Ask Learners how many 1.5 volt batteries is that equivalent to? (250/1.5 = 167 batteries) How many 9 volt batteries is that equivalent to? (250/9= 28 batteries)

Uses of electromagnets: Learners, as you watch the following video, I want you to identify at least 5 uses of electromagnets. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4fGSmpoV9CE (1:00 mins) - Generators, motors, and transformers. - Electric buzzers and bells. - Headphones and loudspeakers - Relays and valves - Data storage devices like VCRs, tape recorders, hard discs, etc. - Used in transportation (movement of trains) - MRI scanning devices. - It is used in the hospitals to scan inside the body of humans and detect most fractures and other issues

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: ELO 1: Analyse and interpret data to provide evidence for the effects of resource availability on organisms and populations of organisms in an ecosystem

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: species, population, over-population, under-population, death rate, birth rate, ecosystem, cause and effect, scarce, abundant
Identify animals competing for food in a food web.
Explain competition among living organisms in an environment.
Identify and describe the impact of under- and over-population of organisms in an environment.
Demonstrate they understand the concept of cause and effect relationships in ecosystems.
Identify the impact of abundant and scarce resources on the growth of individual organisms and the numbers of organisms in ecosystems.
List some factors that can affect population growth.
Estimate the population number of a given organism in a small habitat.
Identify factors that may disrupt the balance of nature.
State the consequences of disrupting the balance of nature.
Infer that food webs help to keep nature in balance.
Communicate effectively with peers
Hypothesize the effect on an ecosystem if one or more organisms are removed from the food web.
Hypothesize the effect of resource scarcity on the ecosystem due to natural or man-made disasters.
construct graphs depicting the number of organisms in an ecosystems
Apply knowledge of cause and effect relationships to predict the impact of resource availability on ecosystems.
Analyze real-life examples of cause and effect relationships in ecosystems.
Show respect for living things by making an effort to conserve resources.
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges

Hodder Education Page 70 Let us study the food web of a garden ecosystem. There is a feeding relationship among living things in an ecosystem. This relationship can be represented using a food web. A food web not only shows the feeding relationship, it also shows animals that compete with each other for food. Let us examine the food web below. Notes for the teacher Use the food web to show Learners the feeding relationship among organisms and competition.

1. Which animals are competing to eat the grasshopper? (bird, owl) These animals both eat the grasshopper. Therefore, they are competing for the same source of food. 2. What do you notice about the arrows when animals are competing in a food web? (Competition is shown in a food web when the arrows point from a food source to more than one consumer.) Let us further discuss competition in ecosystems. Do you have a brother or sister? Do you both try to get attention from your parents or grandparents? If so, you

compete for attention. In this instance, both of you are working against each other to try to get what you want. Just like humans, animals also compete in nature for resources. What resources do you think animals compete for? (Food, water, oxygen, mates). How does competition occur in nature? In any ecosystem, organisms and populations with similar requirements for food, water, oxygen, or other resources may compete with each other for limited resources, which consequently limits their growth and reproduction. All living things need natural resources, but the problem is that there is not enough for everyone. This means that individuals have to fight for them in order to survive. Retrieved from https://slideplayer.com/slide/15234209/ In nature, there are many instances of competition. Sometimes competition occurs between members of the same species. What happens when animals compete for the same resources? (the stronger animal survives, survival of the fittest) For example, both sharks and dolphins eat fish. Both need food to survive. If one eats enough fish to survive but there

is not enough fish for the other, the one that does not get its necessary food may die. Other sea organisms may eat the fish as well, making competition even fiercer. Who competes? Competition occurs between members of different species. This is called interspecific competition. Competition also occurs between members of the same species. This is called intraspecific competition. Retrieved from: https://slideplayer.com/slide/15234209/ Some organisms are better able to survive because they have paired up with another organism and they help each other to survive. Here the egret below essentially helps the cow by removing pesky insects, while at the same time getting food. This type of interaction represents mutualism. In such cases both organisms benefit. Retrieved from:

https://www.istockphoto.com/photo/cattle-egret-and-a-cow-gm92193729-7800055 What happens when animals do not have little or no food to eat? (They can starve to death and even die). What happens when there is an abundance of food? (The animals have lots to eat and are healthy). Let us examine some situations and discuss. What do you notice about the animal in pictures (a) and (b) below? Picture (a) Retrieved from: https://www.shutterstock.com/video/clip-802744-cow-eats-grass Picture (b)

Learners should make the link between abundance/scarcity of grass and the appearance of the cattle. When they do, ask questions to bring out the cause-and-effect relationship between the abundance/ scarcity of grass and the appearance of the cattle. The cow in picture a) eats more grass therefore it is fatter and looks healthier than the cow in picture b) which has less grass to each and hence is not as fat or healthy looking. Cause: An abundance of grass. Effect: Cow eats more and gets fat and looks healthy. Cause: Very little grass. Effect: Cow eats less and is thin and looks unhealthy. The cause-and-effect relationship is a relationship in which one event (the cause) makes another event (the effect) happen. Cause is the reason for a situation or change; why something happens. The effect is the result or the consequent; the outcome or conclusion- what happened. The cause happens before the effect.

There is an abundance of grass in picture (a) so the cow eats more grass and gets fat. There is a scarcity of grass in picture (b) so the cow eats less grass and is skinny. Ask questions during the discussion to elicit the meaning of scarcity and abundance. Note the following terms: 1. Scarcity: not enough of something. 2. Abundance: having more than enough of something. What do animals compete for? There are four resources for which animals compete. What are they? food, water, mates, land (territory). Which resource is not relevant for interspecific competition? Members of different species will not compete for mates. Retrieved from: https://slideplayer.com/slide/15234209/. Resource availability affects growth, competition, and rate of reproduction. How resources become available in an ecosystem directly affects several factors within and among populations of organisms. Let us have a look.

Population changes: Changes in the amount and availability of a given resource (e.g.: less food) may result in changes or limits in the population growth of an organism (e.g.: less food results in fewer organisms).

Individual organism changes: Changes in the amount or availability of a resource (e.g.: more food) may result in changes in the growth of individual organisms (e.g.: more food results in faster growth).
Competition: Resource availability drives competition among organisms, both within a population and between populations.
Rate of reproduction: Resource availability also affects a population’s rate of reproduction

3. Relationship where one organism hunting, killing and eating another organism is called _________.
4. A relationship between two organisms in which both organisms benefit is called _________.
5. When an animal is hunted and killed for food it is called the _________.
6. When an animal is the hunter and kills for food it is called the _________.
1. Parasitism 2. Symbiosis 3. Predator/Prey 4. Mutualism 5. Prey 6. Predator

In nature, organisms interact with each other in a variety of ways. Some are predators, some are prey, and some just compete for food against each other. Some of these relationships last a long time while others do not. In nature a relationship between two organisms that last a long time is called symbiosis. There are different kinds of symbiosis. There are times when both organisms in the relationship benefit from each other. There are times when only one will benefit while harming the other. Then there are times when only one benefits, but no harm is done to the other.

When a relationship between two kinds of organisms benefits both organisms, it is called mutualism. For example, in The Mojave Desert there is a plant that exists called the Yucca tree. When this tree's creamy flowers are in bloom there appears to be shadows that jump around on the flowers. However, a more careful look reveals that these shadows are actually moths. They are called yucca moths. The yucca moths cannot survive without the yucca tree. The yucca trees would also quickly become extinct if it were not for the yucca moth. The yucca moth visits the yucca flower at night and picks up pollen and rolls it up into a ball. The moth then flutters over to another flower and implants that pollen into that flower's ovary. The moth is able to pollinate the flower which in turn sprouts new seeds that become new yucca plants. The yucca tree and the yucca moth depend on each other for survival and they both benefit and share a relationship of mutualism.

When two organisms form a relationship where one organism lives on or in another organism and may harm the other in the process it is called parasitism. The organism that lives on or in the other is called a parasite. The organisms they feed on both are called hosts. The parasites benefits from the relationship. The hosts are harmed by the relationship. For example, fleas are parasites of dogs. The fleas feed off the blood of the dog and give nothing back to the dog in return. The host is harmed while the parasite benefits. Another example of parasitism is mistletoe. It is an evergreen that grows in the branches of a tree such as a poplar, fir, or apple. The mistletoe feeds off the tree while offering nothing to benefit the tree. The relationship between these two organisms is called parasitism.

The relationship between two organisms where one benefits from the other while causing no harm in the process is called commensalism. An example of this would be when a tropical fish lives unharmed among the poisonous tentacles of a sea creature. The sea creature provides safety for the fish and the fish neither harms nor helps the creature. The fish becomes protected from other predators by the poison, yet provides nothing to the sea creature. Another example is when orchids attach itself to the trunk of a tree on the rain forest floor. The orchid only attaches itself to benefit from the position of the tree in order to get sunlight. It causes no harm to the tree. This relationship is called commensalism.

There are different kinds of symbiosis. Each defines the relationship between organisms in nature. While some organisms are predators, other becomes the prey. Still other organisms can live together without any harm being caused. Whether these relationships are mutualism, parasitism, or commensalism, it is clear that organisms interact with one another in a variety of different ways. The nature of these relationships and how they interact differently together is called symbiosis.
1) In nature a relationship between two organisms that last a long time is called: A: Commensalism B: Parasitism C: Symbiosis D: Mutualism
2) When a relationship between two kinds of organisms benefits both organisms, it is called: A: Symbiosis B: Mutualism C: Commensalism D: Parasitism

3) The relationship between two organisms where one benefits from the other while causing no harm in the process is called which of the following? A: Commensalism B: Symbiosis C: Parasitism D: Mutualism

4) When two organisms form a relationship where one organism lives on or in another organism and may harm the other in the process is called which of the following? A: Mutualism B: Commensalism C: Symbiosis D: Parasitism
5) Mistletoe that attaches itself to the branch of a tree and feeds off the nutrients is an example of: A: Parasitism B: Mutualism C: Symbiosis D: Commensalism
6) What is the yucca moth able to do to the yucca flowers that sprouts new seeds that become new yucca plants? A: Implants B: Pollinates C: Plants D: Imbeds

3. Tapeworms are a particular species of worm that resides in the small intestine of mammals (including humans). The tapeworm latches on to the walls of the small intestine, and siphons (steals) nutrients from the mammal’s digestive tract. This can cause severe nutrient deficiency, weakness, and extreme weight-loss for the mammal.

4. Cattle Egrets are a type of bird commonly found on farms. As cattle, horses, and other livestock graze (eat grass) on the field, they cause movements that stir up various insects. As the insects are stirred up, the cattle egrets following the livestock catch and feed upon them.

5. Oxpeckers are a small bird commonly found on rhinoceroses. The oxpecker feeds on the parasites that cover the rhinoceroses’ skin. The bird gets a meal and the rhinoceros is relieved of the harmful parasites.

The teacher will explain to the Learners: Interaction is a necessity for any living being to survive. The teacher will then inform Learners that they will be investigating to find out about the different relationships that exist within ecosystems.
The teacher introduces symbiotic relationships using a video or pictures: A symbiotic relationship is a relationship between two or more organisms living together closely.

Guiding question: What are the three types of symbiotic relationships? What are examples of each? https://youtu.be/QHSHrunOvMo?si=gUM6HI25mCrS90df (5:42 mins) [Recommendation: pause after each type of symbiotic relationship to generate discussion]

Mutualism: The teacher refers to the graphic organizer to identify mutualistic relationships in the Learners' everyday lives and then the examples seen in the video Mutualism: aphids and ants, red-billed oxpeckers and herd animals, anemones and clownfish, lichen (fungi and algae).

Mutualism: Here, the living beings benefit from each other's company. This is a positive, friendly relationship. A mutualistic interaction comprises two species of organisms that work together, each benefiting from the relationship. A classic example of Mutualism is pollination, as the hungry bee gets its nectar from the flower, and in return, the flower is pollinated by the bee so it can reproduce.
Retrieved from: https://widget-club.com/i/a32d4cf8848d4d91a17a
Learners add examples of mutualisms to their graphic organizer. Learners may give other examples that they know from previous experiences.

Commensalism: The teacher refers to the graphic organizer to identify commensalism relationships in the Learners' everyday life and then the examples seen in the video (algae and manatee, bromeliad plants and large trees).

Commensalism: It is a one-sided beneficial relationship between two living organisms, where one organism reaps benefits like food or shelter from the other organism without benefiting or harming it. The partners that profit from such a kind of association are commensal, while the latter organism is the host species. Example: Cattle egrets eat the insects stirred up by cattle when they are grazing. The cattle are unaffected, while the birds gain food.
Retrieved from: https://rushingoutdoors.net/2021/06/16/cattle-egrets-the-not-bad-not-natives/
Learners add examples of commensalism to their graphic organizer. Learners may give other examples that they know from previous experiences.

Parasitism: The teacher refers to the graphic organizer to identify parasitic relationships in the Learners' everyday life and then the examples seen in the video (ticks and animal fur, cuckoo birds and other birds' nests, cordyceps and insects, parasites: tapeworms, fleas)

Parasitism: It is an interaction in which one organism, the parasite, benefits or profits while the other organism, the host, is harmed. In such an association, the parasite is found to live within or upon the host's body. Depending on the type of parasite involved, the parasitism may be termed ecto-parasitism or endo-parasitism. Example: Ticks present on the fur of dogs. Harmful ticks reside on the dog's fur and obtain shelter from the fur. The ticks harm the dog by lavishly feeding on its blood and causing it to itch. In some cases, these ticks are a source of the carrier of diseases, infecting the animals they live on.
Retrieved from: https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Tick-infestation-in-a-dog-before-the-application-of-collar-Day-0-Tick-abundance-was_fig1_250918823

Predation and Competition: The teacher will set up workstations around the classroom where Learners will view videos, pictures, or simulations of various ecosystems to identify some of the relationships that exist within these ecosystems. Relationships that should emerge from the viewing of videos or pictures and classroom discussion: Predator-prey; Competition for food
Symbiotic: mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism

The Learners will prepare a food web for each type of ecosystem. A key and markers or crayons can be used to identify the types of relationships that exist in the ecosystem from the living things identified in the food web.

Predation: It is an ecological interaction in which one organism kills and feeds on the other. The hunter organism in this interaction is called the predator, and the unfortunate organism that serves as the food is called the prey. Example: wild animals like tigers, lions attacking the zebra, deer, cattle, etc.

Competition: When two different species fight or compete with each other for the same resource, this form of interaction is termed competition. The animals could compete for resources like food, support, shelter, space, etc. Organisms are compelled to follow this type of ecological interaction for their survival or existence. Such ecological interaction could either lead to the extinction of one of the parties or the extinction of both parties. Therefore, this ecological interaction allows only the best competitor to survive and pass this trait to their offspring, thus, maintaining the community structure. For example, competition for a berry will be fierce if only two species of birds from different species eat it.
To wrap up this section, we will now watch a short video.

Class Activity: Based on the information we learnt in this lesson, we will now engage in a guided discussion, where a scenario will be given and you will determine the type of relationship (mutualism, parasitism, commensalism, predation or competition) being described, and briefly explain your reasoning. a) A bee feeds off the nectar of a sunflower. While feeding, pollen from the sunflower clings to the legs and body of the bee. When the bee lands on a different sunflower, the pollen is left behind and new pollen picked up. b) A cat is carrying a dead mouse as a hawk swoops down to grab it. They both pull on the mouse but the flapping wings of the hawk against the face of the cat are strong enough to make the cat drop the mouse. c) Polar bears wander the ice looking for signs of seals. Once they find a seal they will attack and eat it.
Follow-Up Activity: The Learners in groups can write a blog on the types of relationships in one of the ecosystems explored in the lesson and their importance.

Factors that cause changes in an Ecosystem: We might think of ecosystems as something constant but in reality, ecosystems are subject to change, just like everything else on this planet. Let's discuss the different changes to ecosystems, and the natural and human causes behind those changes. If you've ever seen a fallen tree lying in the road the morning after a thunderstorm, you probably already have some idea of how natural events can cause changes in ecosystems. A natural disaster is a weather-related event that causes widespread damage to an area. Natural disasters are not caused by humans. • Wildfire/forest fire • Flooding
Drought
Earthquake
Volcanic eruption
Tornado
Tsunami
Cyclone
Disease

Natural disasters can fundamentally change an ecosystem. Entire forests can be burned down by a wildfire or uprooted by an earthquake, leading to deforestation. An area can be completely flooded, drowning all of the plants. A disease like rabies can spread through an area, killing large numbers of animals.

Many natural disasters only cause temporary changes to ecosystems. Once the event has passed, the area slowly recovers: trees grow back, animals return, and the original ecosystem is largely restored. However, natural causes of changes to ecosystems can be permanent. This usually has to do with long-term changes to climate or physical geography. For example, if an area faces drought for too long, it may become more desert-like. Or, if an area remains permanently flooded after a hurricane or tsunami, it may become an aquatic ecosystem. In both cases, the original wildlife will likely never return, and the ecosystem will be forever altered.

Human causes of changes to ecosystems are almost always permanent because human activity often results in land-use change. This means that we humans will repurpose land that was once part of a wild ecosystem. We may cut down trees to make way for farmland; we may pave over part of a grassland to create a road. These activities change the way wildlife interact with each other and their environment, as it introduces new, artificial elements to a natural ecosystem. For example, animals that attempt to cross busy roads in search of more food will be at risk of being hit by a car.
If an area becomes urbanized enough, the original natural ecosystem may functionally cease to exist, and any animals and plants that remain in an area will be forced to adapt to human infrastructure.

Besides land-use change, human management can play a role in ecosystems. You can think of human management of ecosystems as intentionally or unintentionally 'tinkering' with the natural function of an ecosystem. Human management includes: Pollution from agriculture or industry, Manipulating pre-existing physical geography, Hunting, fishing, or poaching, Introducing new animals to an area (more on this below)

Dams and wind turbines, which we depend on for renewable, sustainable energy, can disrupt the natural swim patterns of fish or flight patterns of birds, respectively. Pesticides or fertilizers from agriculture can wind up in rivers and streams, altering the acidity of water, and in the most egregious cases, causing bizarre mutations or death.

Activity: Learners will research and present the factors that affected the specific desert, marine, pond, and coral ecosystems/habitats e.g. Amazon rainforest, and the Sahara desert. Their research presentation can include: names of plants and animals that live in the specific habitat and the food they typically eat in the environment, Food web, Living things that compete with each other for food and living space, The human and non-human factors that have and are currently affecting the ecosystem. What changes have occurred because of these factors?
The Learners can create a video, blog, or paper that they will present to the class.

Core Ideas for meeting specific curriculum outcomes. Matter of any type can be subdivided into particles that are too small to see, but even then, the matter still exists and can be detected by other means (e.g., by weighing or by its effects on other objects). For example, a model showing that gases are made from matter particles that are too small to see and are moving freely around in space can explain many observations, including the inflation and shape of a balloon; the effects of air on larger particles or objects (e.g., leaves in wind, dust suspended in air); and the appearance of visible scale water droplets in condensation, fog, and, by extension, also in clouds or the contrails of a jet.

Opportunities for Subject Integration: Science: Discuss the chemical processes involved in turning natural resources into synthetic materials. You can use simple examples like rubber, plastics, and medications. Social Studies: Explore how synthetic materials have impacted Caribbean economies, like the role of oil refineries or agriculture and the use of fertilizers. Mathematics: Have Learners calculate how much of a natural resource is needed to produce a certain quantity of synthetic material or explore the cost/benefit analysis of using synthetic vs. natural resources. Environmental Studies: Discuss the environmental effects of synthetic materials like plastic waste and the impact on marine life in the Caribbean.

Elements from Local Culture: Vincentian farmers, especially in the rural area make coalpits to create charcoal which is used for fuel in outdoor cooking. Also in the rural areas, coconuts are green gold as they are used to make a variety of products i.e. coconut oil, coconut sugar cakes, bottled coconut water. Some traditional natural remedies have been synthesised into modern medicines (e.g., the use of plants like neem or noni, aloe). Some of the traditional materials used in the home like crocus/plant based bags, gourd bowls, bamboo spoons, coconut fibres scrubbers are now being replaced with products made from synthesised materials.
Environment: Air masses and air pressure systems greatly influence the Caribbean weather. We have a known hurricane season because of this.
Technology: How is technology used to make synthetic products. (How Its Made tv series. See: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x7ehv8dSHYQ&list=PL4gMXdLO5DEecaT1pOliQ-Oc75kWuUxwp (5:24 mins))

TVET and Technology: Incorporate local industries that rely on synthetic materials, such as manufacturing or oil refining. TVET programs that focus on these fields can offer insight into local job markets and skills development.
Agriculture: Synthetic fertilisers have impacted local Caribbean agriculture. Discuss natural vs. synthetic fertilizers and their effects on soil health and crop yields.

QUESTIONS: Kinetic Energy
Circle the correct answer.
1. Kinetic energy is the energy of:
A. potential
B. objects at rest
C. objects in motion
D. accelerating objects

2. Kinetic energy _____________ speed.
A. decreases with
B. increases with
C. is the same as its
D. is unrelated to

3. An object’s kinetic energy remains constant as long as:
A. an object remains the same size and continues to move at the same speed
B. an object remains the same size
C. an object continues to move at the same speed
D. an object doesn’t move

4. Potential energy becomes kinetic energy when:
A. an object gets bigger
B. an object gets smaller
C. an object starts to move
D. an object stops moving

5. Kinetic energy can:
A. become potential energy during a collision
B. decrease during a collision
C. increase during a collision
D. be transferred between objects during a collision

Answer Key
1. C
2. B
3. A
4. C
5. D

1. Kinetic energy and mass (Ans: B)
2. Kinetic energy and velocity (Ans: A)
inclusive_learning_strategies: Introductory Activity: A Classroom Demonstration/Group Activity
Materials needed: Marble, dense rubber ball (or any two balls of different masses but the same or similar in volume), table top, bucket half filled with water.
Activity: Place the two balls of different masses on the top of the table top.
Figure 1: Drawing of two balls of masses placed on table just above bucket of water.

Learners, I am going to roll the balls off the edge of the table so that they fall into the bucket. What will happen when the balls hit the water (make a splash)? Why do the balls even fall downward? (Gravity pulls them to the ground.) So objects get pulled to the ground by a gravitational force. If I hold a heavy rock above a wooden stake placed in the ground and release the rock. What Is likely to happen to the stake? (it will be driven into the ground) So the rock does work as it pushes the stake into the ground. We would say that the rock has the potential to do work and therefore holding the rock above the ground gives the rock potential energy. If you wanted to drive the stake as deep as possible into the ground by releasing a rock, would you choose a bigger rock or a smaller rock? (bigger rock)

Let’s make a conclusion statement here: So Learners, you are telling me the rock with the bigger mass will have more potential energy to do work on the stake. Potential energy must depend on the mass of the object falling.

Back to the balls and the water bucket...We can see another measure of the potential energy. When I roll the balls off the edge of the table and into the water bucket...Learners, which do you think will make the biggest splash? (The larger ball). Why? (The larger ball has more mass)

Let’s make a conclusion statement here: So Learners, the balls as they fall, hit the water and do work on the surface of the water. They must have potential energy just like the rock. The size of the splash shows us how much energy was being transferred to the water. Let us try the experiment and see if the bigger ball really does make a bigger splash!

We also know that as the ball falls, the potential energy gets converted to energy of motion called kinetic energy. When do you think the kinetic energy is the highest? (Near the bottom just as the ball hits the water.)
Where does the energy go after that? (water get energy of motion as it moves)
Do you think all of the energy goes into moving the water? (maybe yes)

Actually, any time we have a collision of two objects (in this case, a ball and water) some energy is lost in the form of heat given up to the water. Try clapping your hands rapidly. After a few moments of those collisions, you will feel your hands get warmer as they absorb some of the kinetic energy from that collision!

Demonstration: What About the Speed of the Ball? I want John to come to front of the class. Now let us have him turn around so he is facing away from me. I have a ping pong ball that I am going to toss at John’s back. John did you feel the ball striking your back? (yes but only gently) So John, you could feel the energy as it impacted your back but it was very small, correct? (Yes teacher). John, what will it feel like if I throw the ping pong ball at your back as hard as I can? (It wouldn’t hurt because it so small but the impact would be much greater than when you tossed the ping pong ball. You wouldn’t want me to throw a cricket ball at your back would you John? (no teacher) Why? John? (Because that ball would be heavier and it would certainly hurt- I would feel much more energy!)

John has helped us learned two important things about energy today and I want you to remember this demonstration, so these findings make sense to you! 1: the energy transferred from the balls depends on the mass of balls 2: the energy transferred from the balls depends on the speed of the balls.

Concluding statements from Activities:
1. Kinetic energy is the energy of objects that are in motion.
2. Potential energy is stored energy, ready to be converted into another form
3. As the mass of an object increases, the kinetic energy of the object increases.
4. As the speed of an object increases, the kinetic energy of the object increases.
5. Mass and kinetic energy have a direct or proportional relationship.

One More Example: A Scenario
Which is heavier (has more mass) a cricket ball or a basketball? (basketball)
When someone throws a cricket ball to you and you catch it, it takes very little force from you in the opposite direction to keep you from falling over. If someone then throws you a basketball, it requires more force from you in the opposite direction to maintain your stance.

What if someone then threw a concrete block to you?
Describe how it would feel? (I would steady myself for the impact of the block and exert significant force in the opposite direction. I may not have enough force, and may fall over.)
So, the kinetic energy that something possesses as it moves, depends on two things: the mass of the object and the speed with which it is moving toward you. In both cases the kinetic energy is said to be directly related to the mass and directly related to the speed.

Equation for Kinetic Energy:
Kinetic Energy can be found by using the formula: KE = 1/2 mv²
Where KE represents the Kinetic Energy; m represents the mass of the object possessing kinetic energy; v represents the speed or velocity of the object and needs to be squared (multiplied by itself) in the formula.
Example:
Consider a boy/girl rolling down a hill on his bicycle. Together, they have a mass of 50 kg. Near the bottom of the hill, the boy/girl has a speed or velocity of 10 m/s. To calculate the Kinetic energy at the bottom of the hill,
1. multiply his mass (m) by 1/2: 50 × 1/2 = 25
2. square the speed/velocity (v): 10² = 100
3. Multiply the answers in 1 and 2 to get the Kinetic Energy of the boy/girl on a bicycle going down the hill: KE = 25 × 100 = 2500 Joules [Joule (J) is the Standard Unit of Energy]

Kinetic Energy and Speed
What if the velocity of the boy/girl on the bicycle was doubled (20 m/s) What do you think happens to Kinetic Energy? Possible responses: Nothing, doubled, quadrupled. Allow Learners to calculate it.

What is the mathematical relationship between speed and Kinetic energy? (Kinetic energy is related to the speed of an object. More advanced: Kinetic energy is directly proportional to the square of its speed.)
Interpreting Kinetic Energy and Graphs
Provide Learners with a graph sheet or grid paper with an already drawn scale.

For Table 1, Learners should plot a graph of Kinetic Energy against mass. After plotting the points Learners should connect the points with a straight line (Learners should use a ruler to draw the line).
Graph drawn with Microsoft Excel
Alternatively, the teacher can display the graph from a projector or on a white board. Learners will then describe the shape of the graph. (A straight line)

We will now discuss the trend. When the mass increases, the kinetic energy also increases by the same factor - a constant that is multiplied by the quantity to cause the increase or decrease. This type of relationship is called direct proportion. When one quantity increases, the other quantity increases. Conversely, when one quantity decreases, the other quantity also decreases.

Ask Learners to determine the amount of kinetic energy possessed by objects of specific masses, travelling at the same speed, using the graph. For instance, what is the kinetic energy of an object of mass 60 kg? (Learners should draw a line from 60 kg up to the trendline and then read the horizontal axis (kinetic energy) See picture below :)
Ask Learners to predict what happens when the mass is doubled (120 kg), tripled (180 J). They will then use the graph to verify.

Ask Learners for the kinetic energy of a mass that is not on the graph. Example: What is the kinetic energy possessed by an object of mass 300 J? Ans: 15 000J. Learners may determine the kinetic energy of 150 J and multiply by 2, OR others may recognize that 300 kg is 5 times 60 kg, hence the kinetic energy will be 5 times 3000 J which is 15 000 J.

For Table 2, Learners should be guided to plot the graph of kinetic energy against speed. After plotting as Learners whether a straight line connects the points. No. Instead the graph forms a curve. Learners should then try to connect the points by forming a smooth curve. Teacher may demonstrate.

Ask Learners to compare the relationship between kinetic energy and speed with the relationship between kinetic energy and mass with respect to the graphs. Kinetic energy increases at a faster rate than the speed increases. However, kinetic energy increases at the same rate as the mass of the object increases. The shape is different because when the speed increases by multiplying by a constant, the kinetic energy increases by multiplying the previous value by the square of that constant! The same applies for division.

A similar exercise can be done with Learners on this graph. Ask Learners to predict values and verify on the graph. Then extend to values that are related to the trend of the graph, but are not points plotted on the graph.

Comprehension: Learning from Reading Passage. Potential Energy. Potential energy is the energy that exists in an object when it is not moving. Like its opposite, kinetic energy, potential energy is measured in joules (J). Potential energy is inversely related to kinetic energy. This means that the more potential energy there is in an object, the less kinetic energy there is, and vice versa. For example, if you hold an object up in the air on your flat, open palm, that object has potential energy because it is at rest. When the object falls, that potential energy becomes kinetic energy. Moving objects have potential energy, too. A classic example of this is the case of the roller coaster. As a car moves up the coaster its potential energy increases. At the peak of the incline it has achieved its peak of potential energy. On its descent, as it gets faster and faster, its kinetic energy increases in direct proportion to its speed. You could say that potential energy is kinetic energy that hasn't happened yet. There are different types of potential energy. Gravitational potential energy (GPE) comes from Earth's gravity. GPE is the potential energy that is stored in an object based on its mass and how high it is off the ground. The larger an object is and the higher it is off the ground, the more potential energy it has. Elastic potential energy is the amount of energy that is stored when materials like springs, rubber bands, and slingshots are stretched or compressed. Electric potential energy is the capacity to do work based on an object's electric charge. Nuclear potential energy is the potential energy of the particles inside an atom. Chemical potential energy is the energy stored in substances as a result of their chemical bonds, i.e., the amount of energy that will be released if the bond is broken.

QUESTIONS: Potential Energy
1. What is potential energy?
2. What unit of measurement is used to express potential energy?
3. What is the relationship between potential and kinetic energy?
4. Give an example of a moving object having potential energy.
5. What is gravitational potential energy?
6. What is nuclear potential energy?
7. What is elastic potential energy?
8. What is chemical potential energy?
9. What is electric potential energy?

ANSWERS
1. the energy that exists in an object when it is not moving
2. joules (J)
3. they have an inverse relationship
4. as a roller coaster car moves up the coaster its potential energy increases. At the peak of the incline it has achieved its peak of potential energy.
5. GPE is the potential energy that is stored in an object based on its mass and how high it is off the ground. the larger an object is and the higher it is off the ground, the more potential energy it has.
6. Nuclear potential energy is the potential energy of the particles inside an atom.
7. Elastic potential energy is the amount of energy that is stored when materials like springs, rubber bands, and slingshots are stretched or compressed.
8. Chemical potential energy is the energy stored in substances as a result of their chemical bonds, i.e., the amount of energy that will be released if the bond is broken.
9. Electric potential energy is the capacity to do work based on an object's electric charge.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Introductory Activity: Increasing Potential Energy. Set up an inclined plane with books and a piece of cardboard or wood. Place a marble or ball at the top of the ramp. Before letting the ball go engage a discussion. Retrieved from https://tinybop.com/blog/experiment-with-simple-machines-make-an-inclined-plane and modified. Learners, when I release the ball, what will happen? (It will roll down the ramp). What causes it to roll down? (We’ve learned already about gravity as a force pulling things down). We have seen in previous lessons that objects at a height above the ground have the potential to do work as they fall. You will remember we called this potential energy. As the ball rolls down the ramp what will happen to its speed? (It will probably go faster for awhile). We know from our prior lessons that the potential energy gets converted to energy of motion called kinetic energy.

Types of Potential Energy. When we talk about the potential to do work, there are actually a few types of potential energy we should clarify. Gravitational potential energy is the energy stored in an object as the result of its vertical position or height (like our ball/marble). The energy is stored as the result of the gravitational attraction of the Earth for the object. Elastic potential energy is the energy stored in elastic materials as the result of their stretching or compressing. Elastic potential energy can be stored in rubber bands, bungee chords, trampolines, springs, an arrow drawn into a bow, etc.

How Does the Height of the Ramp Make a Difference in How Far the Car Travels? Learners, we are going to set up three ramps with books. The ramps will be increasing heights. If I roll the same toy car down the ramps, in which case do you think the car will travel the furthest as it reaches the

Which ramp will have the car travel the fastest as it goes down the ramp? (the highest ramp) Let us try it and see what happens. It is important that, when we do experimental trials that we design a controlled experiment. That means we maintain all the variables in the experiment the same except the variable we want to measure. What do you think the controls will be for this experiment? Let me give you a hint...we will use the same car, the same ramp length & materials- what do you think other controls might be? (same books, measure distance from the same point, same release point)
Materials: 6 identical books, three pieces of hard cardboard or wood to create a plank the same size, three identical toy cars, a measuring stick, tape or ruler, chalk

Approach: Have Learners release the cars from the same point at the top of the ramps. Ask them which ramp appeared to have given the car the greatest speed? (The tallest ramp). Since speed is a measure of distance-the ramp length-per unit time...how could they measure that speed of those cars? (By timing the car over the distance from the top of the ramp to the bottom.)

Ask Learners to use chalk to mark the distance from the bottom of the ramp to the car's resting place. They can enter the distance in cm in the table below. Based on those measures, which ramp had the car traveling the furthest from the bottom of the ramp? (the tallest ramp)
What variables are changing in our experiment? (height of the ramp) What are we measuring? (distance travelled)
Sample Observation Table: Number of Books, Height of the plane (cm), Distance car travelled (cm) with rows for 2, 4, 6 books.
