Instructions: 1. Divide the class into teams of three or four Learners each. 2. Show the Learners how to use a straw like a water dropper: Stick the straw in a glass of water. Place a finger on the top of the straw. Pull the straw out of the glass, while still holding your finger over the top. Hold the straw over a piece of paper and remove your finger.
3. Ask the Learners how they can all be sure to use the same amount of water for each trial. (Answer: By marking a line on the straw and always refilling it to that point.)

4. Discuss with Learners’ methods for measuring the diameter of a splash. Have the Learners agree on a standard class method for determining the diameter of a splash. Have Learners use a pencil or pen to outline the splash, and label it with their name, the drop height, and the splash diameter.

5. Have student teams describe on the worksheet what they predict will happen to the splash as it is dropped from increasing heights. Do they expect the size of the splash to increase or decrease with higher drop heights?

6. Have the class agree on four heights from which to drop the water, such as 30 cm, 60 cm, 90 cm and 1 m (note this on the worksheet). Teams measure the diameter of the water splash for all four heights. Each team member drops the water once from each height. Thus, teams with three members have 12 data points and teams with four members have 16 data points.
7. Learners take turns doing each part of the activity. One student holds the meter stick. One student drops the water using the straw. A third student measures the splash diameter.

8. Hold the bottom of the straw at the height by first placing it next to the correct height on the meter stick and then moving it several centimeters away, being careful to keep the straw at the same height. On the paper, write the height, diameter and team member name, and record the data on the worksheet.
9. After one person drops the water, the team members switch places and repeat the process. The student measuring the diameter of the splash moves the paper so the water falls on a dry piece.
10. After teams have finished their drops, have them complete the worksheet by calculating averages and creating a graph of their average data.
11. Conclude with a class-wide comparison of each team's results (table data and graphs) and a concluding discussion, as described in the Assessment section.

Areas of Focus in activity: 1. As the distance from the surface increases, the gravitational potential energy of the liquid increases. 2. This increase in potential energy is directly related to the distance the particles move when they hit the surface (splash diameter). 3. The potential energy is converted to kinetic energy according to the principle of conservation of energy.

How Roller Coasters Use Energy. The following video can be shown to Learners to explain energy changes that occur on a rollercoaster ride, due to the initial height of the roller coaster. https://youtu.be/jSyz_U79iQ0?si=HamqneyGIFF8ZaUA (8:03 mins) Alternatively, the teacher can use the following simulation https://contrib.pbslearningmedia.org/WGBH/conv16/conv16-int-rollercoaster/index.html

A roller coaster ride is a thrilling experience which involves a wealth of science. The ride often begins as a chain and motor (or other mechanical device) exerts a force on the train of cars to lift the train to the top of a very tall hill building the potential energy as the height increases. The steeper the gradient the faster the roller coaster moves. The roller coaster track is designed so that the potential energy from a high height is converted to kinetic energy of motion as the car descends but as it rises again it begins to gain potential energy once again.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Help Me Explain This Problem! Good morning Learners, I want to tell you a story and see if you can explain what might have happened. My friend was making a delicious soup and the phone rang. She left the kitchen to take the call and forgot to remove the metal stirring spoon from the soup pot. When the call was finished, she returned to the kitchen and reached for the spoon to continue stirring the soup. When she grabbed the spoon, she screamed in pain. That is right, she burned her hand on the metal spoon! Why do you think that happened? (The heat from the soup pot was heated the spoon too). How did the heat get to the spoon from the soup pot though? Well, we know that particles that make up matter move very quickly when they get heated up and that energy can be passed along each and every particle. When the heat is transferred like that we call it conduction. Sometimes conduction is useful if we want the heat to be transferred (like throughout a pot) but other times like the spoon it can be harmful. What do we often do with kitchen tools that we don't want to conduct heat? Let me give you a hint, what do you often see on the lid of pot? (a wooden, plastic or rubber handle). We call those materials that reduce heat transfer insulators.

Demonstration of Conduction: Figure of apparatus for experiment. Retrieved from https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9yjApqj-jdA. Materials Needed: Metal rod or metal spoon, Candle or heat source (like a hot plate), Butter or wax, Paper clips or small metal objects, Stopwatch or timer, Tongs or heat-resistant gloves, Safety goggles. Procedure: Cut small pieces of butter or wax and stick them at regular intervals along the length of the metal rod or spoon. Attach a paper clip or small metal object to each piece of butter or wax. This will help visualize the melting process.

Ask Learners to predict what will happen to the butter or wax when only one end of the metal rod is heated. (Record predictions). Hold the metal rod or spoon with tongs or heat-resistant gloves. Place one end of the metal rod or spoon into the flame of the candle or on the hot plate. Start the stopwatch as soon as the metal rod or spoon is placed in the heat source. Observe the butter or wax pieces and note the time it takes for each piece to start melting and the paper clips to fall off. Record the time for each interval. Have Learners record their observations and measurements in a table.

Discussion Questions: "What did you observe when the end of the metal rod was heated?" (After some time the paper clips fell off the rod, one at a time.) "Why did the butter or wax melt, and the paper clips fall off?" (Heat was conducted (flow through) the metal rid and melted the wax.) Learners, this demonstration is meant to show the results of the process of conduction. Teachers with access to technology: Experiment can be seen in the following video:
the butter or wax is not in contact with the water.
Place each spoon (metal, wooden, plastic) into a separate cup or beaker.
Start the stopwatch as soon as the spoons are placed in the hot water.
Observe the butter or wax and note the time it takes for each piece to start melting on each spoon.
Record the time for each spoon.
Have Learners record their observations and measurements in a table, noting which spoon's butter or wax melts first.

Discussion Questions: "What did you observe when the spoons were placed in the hot water?" (The butter stayed on the spoons initially) "Which spoon's butter or wax melted first? Why do you think this happened?" (The metal spoon) "How does this demonstrate the process of conduction?" (The butter or wax on the metal spoon melted first since the metal was a good conductor of heat whereas the others were insulators.)

Applying What You Know: A wooden spoon and a metal spoon are placed on a counter. They are both at room temperature. Why does the metal spoon feel colder than the wooden spoon when you touch it? Let me give you a hint...your sense of touch would detect a change in temperature of your finger. Why would your finger seem cooler?

1. Convection: Allow Learners to observe a pan of boiling water and how the water circulates. Question: 'Why does the water in a pan boil from the bottom and then circulate to the top?' Explain the process of convection in fluids.

Demonstrating Convection: Conduct a demo or show a video experiment (Convection Current Experiment by Lynda Williams) using a clear container, hot/cold water, and food coloring to visualize convection currents.
Record predictions, observe the experiment, and describe the circular movement of fluid. Explanation: Hot water is less dense and rises, cold water sinks, creating convection currents.
Examples of Convection currents: Water boiling on a stove (illustrated with a diagram showing warm molecules rising and cool molecules sinking).

Retrieved from https://letstalkscience.ca/educational-resources/backgrounders/introduction-heat-transfer
Convection Currents: Land and Sea Breezes:
Sea breezes occur on summer days because land heats up faster than the sea. Warm air rises as it is less dense than cooler air. As the warm air rises, cooler air from over the ocean moves over land to take its place. It is this movement of warm and cool air that creates a sea breeze!
The opposite happens at night when the surface of land cools faster than the surface of water. The warmer air over the ocean rises and cooler land air flows out to sea to replace it.

LAND VS SEA BREEZE
Retrieved from https://www.science-sparks.com/what-is-a-sea-breeze/
Other examples: ocean currents, wind patterns, and the movement of magma inside the Earth
2. Radiation
“Why does wearing dark clothing make you feel warmer in the sun than light-colored clothing?” (By experiment, scientists know that black objects absorb all wavelengths of light and convert them to heat. Conversely,

white objects reflect all wavelengths and therefore here is no conversion to heat)
Learners may develop a hypothesis and use the following experiment to test it.
Retrieved from https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DI fzIXmGth0 (2:00 mins)
Experiment: Black coloured objects absorbs and loose more radiant heat than a white object
- Black colour object absorbs and loses more radiant heat than a white object.
- Take two metal containers A and B.
- Paint container A with black colour and container B with white.
- Fill both the container with equal amount of water
- To measure the temperature, fix two thermometers in the container
- Note the thermometer reading
- Place the two container in the sun for about 15 to 20 minutes

Again note the thermometer readings. You will find that the temperature of water placed in the black container (A) rises more than that of the water in container white (B). This happens because black object absorbs more radiant heat than a white object. Now place both the container A and B in the shade. You will observe that the temperature of water in black colour container cools down faster. Therefore we conclude that black coloured objects absorb and radiate heat more as compared to white objects in the same time period. Application of this is real life: A black car sitting in the sun at the same time with a white car, will feel hotter inside because the black colour absorbs heat more than the white. When standing close to these two vehicles outside in the sun, you will feel more heat from the black car than the white. Shiny bright surfaces are good reflectors of heat but poor absorbers. Examples of this property for reflection of heat can be seen in the shiny reflective surfaces of cars, so that heat from the sun is reflected instead of absorbed into the car, shiny galvanized roofs in the Caribbean. Objects that are good absorbers of radiant heat are also good emitters of heat. They give off heat energy through radiation.

Light colored shiny objects are poor absorbers and therefore good reflectors of radiant heat. Radiation is a method of heat transfer that does not require particles to carry heat energy. Heat is transferred in infrared waves (a type of electromagnetic wave or radiation) from hot objects in all directions. Unlike conduction and convection, particles are not required for heat transfer through radiation. Learners can view the following video about heat transfer: radiation. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OYW7lR-XuJA (1:46 mins) Greenhouse Effect: A Problem of Heat Transfer Learners, what is a greenhouse? (A place to grow vegetables). Why is it a useful technology? (We can grow things in a warm environment). Have you ever walked in a greenhouse? What does it feel like?

(Steamy, war, wet). How does it stay so warm; are they pumping warm air in? (No, hot air isn't added, the sun shines through the glass or plastic and the heat gets trapped inside). The greenhouse effect on earth is very similar. When we burn hydrocarbon fuels like wood, diesel, oil etc. one of the products is carbon dioxide. There are other gases produced by industrial processes that contain nitrogen and sulfur. If we burn too much the carbon dioxide as well as other industrial byproducts makes a blanket over the earth. The sun shines through and warms the earth but the reflected heat can't escape because the gaseous blanket holds the heat in. We therefore have elevated temperatures at the surface; just like a greenhouse! This has many detrimental effects!
Retrieved from: https://www.shalom-education.com/courses/gcsechemistry/lessons/earths-and-atmospheric-science/topic/the-greenhouse-effect/

Supplemental Video Resource: Greenhouse Effect As Learners watch the following video (https://youtu.be/SN5-DnOHQmE (2:29 mins)) they should make notes that allow them to answer the following questions: 
- Is the greenhouse effect useful or harmful? Why? 
- What would happen if there were too many greenhouse gases in the atmosphere? 
- What would happen if there were no greenhouse gases? 
- What ways can we reduce the number of greenhouse gases in our atmosphere?

Heat Transfer Summary Learners may be shown the following video which summarizes all three methods of heat transfer, with real life applications. Retrieved from https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HpCvWuvCUoA (3:16 mins)

Minimizing energy transfer: The Vacuum Flask A vacuum flask is designed to keep food or beverages warm or cold. It does this by minimizing the transfer of thermal energy from outside to inside the flask and vice - versa. Ask Learners: What is a vacuum? (A vacuum is any space that has no matter. This means that there are no particles.) Show Learners a diagram of the parts of a vacuum flask and discuss how each element of the design minimizes the transfer of thermal energy. Discuss the specific method of heat transfer being minimized. Retrieved from: https://feed.theskepticsguide.org/images/podcast_images/Thermos.jpg In this diagram, the flask is keeping the tea inside hot. What is the role of each of the labelled parts in keeping this tea hot? Leading questions: Can you identify the insulators? How do you think heat transfer by radiation is reduced? Why do you think there is a vacuum?

Answers: Plastic Stoppers and corks serve as insulators to avoid heat transfer by conduction. A vacuum has no particles, hence heat cannot be transferred by conduction and convection. The silvered surface is shiny and therefore reflects heat back into the flask instead of outward. The walls are thin hence limiting heat transfer through conduction.

Thermos Engineering Project: Now that Learners know the three methods of heat transfer, they will now explore methods of minimizing heat transfer and use it to construct a device. In this activity Learners will apply scientific principles to design, construct and test a device that minimizes energy transfer. Look at the following video for instructions: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kfBcqhsaVJ0 (2:08 mins)

Learners are expected to test different materials and come up with the best insulation to put around their cup as well as the lid. Two plastic cups are required: a smaller cup that fits inside a larger one. It is intended for the insulating material to fit in between the two cups. Consideration for the lid design is also important. Other materials: thermometer, beaker, Petri dish, hot plate, water. Examples of insulating materials Learners may use include cotton, aluminum foil, cardboard, cloth. Learners will test each materials’ insulation property by placing it between a beaker of water and a hot plate and heating the water for a specific amount of time. The time for heating should be the same for each material. Also ensure that the water never reaches boiling.

Retrieved from https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kfBcqhsaVJ0. Learners should make note of the temperature of the water at the end of the heating period (5 minutes) for each object in the table: [Table with columns: Material, Initial temperature of water (°C), Final temperature of water (°C), Change in Temperature (°C)]. A bar graph can be constructed to determine the best insulating material.
Learners will create their insulation around their cup from each material to determine which one best minimizes heat transfer
They will place some water in the cup and note the temperature after heating, with each design,
Learners should record results in a table and may construct graphs to display their findings. These results should help determine the best design.

Additional Useful Content Knowledge for the Teacher: An object can store energy as the result of its position. For example, the heavy ball of a demolition machine stores energy when it is held at an elevated position. This stored energy of position is referred to as potential energy. Similarly, a drawn bow can store energy as a result of its position. When assuming its usual position (i.e., when not drawn), there is no energy stored in the bow. Yet when its position is altered from its usual equilibrium position, the bow can store energy due to its position. This stored energy of position is referred to as potential energy. Potential energy is the stored energy of position possessed by an object. Retrieved from https://eschooltoday.com/learn/the-inclined-plane/

Calculating Circumference of Planets. The formula for circumference of a circle is C =2πr where π=3.14. If the earth has a radius of 6371 km, how many times bigger is the circumference of Jupiter? Earth circumference = 2πr = 2(3.14)(6371km)= 40,010 km. Living on Mars – two-page report. Mars seems to be the most habitable planet after earth of course! Why Do We Thrive on Earth- Quiz

Multiple-Choice Questions 1. What is one reason Earth can support life? a. Earth is the largest planet in the solar system. b. Earth has water in all three forms (solid, liquid, gas). c. Earth is closest to the Sun. d. Earth has the shortest day/night cycle.

Answer: c

Short Answer Questions 1. Explain why liquid water is necessary for life on Earth. (Water is essential because all living organisms need it for survival. It helps with biological processes such as transporting nutrients and removing waste.)

2. How does Earth's distance from the Sun contribute to supporting life? (Earth’s distance from the Sun places it in the "habitable zone," where temperatures are just right for liquid water to exist. It’s not too hot or too cold for life.)

inclusive_learning_strategies: How Big is the Sun Compared to the Earth? Learners are presented with the following items; basketball, golf ball, tennis ball, marble and 2mm bead or steel ball bearing.

Learners, if the earth was represented as the small bead (or ball bearing), which of these objects do you think best represents the relative size of the sun. The word relative means by comparing two things. For example, you are probably shorter relative to the height of your parent. Learners write down their choices. You may be surprised but the relative size of the sun is actually bigger than the basketball! Have you ever seen an exercise ball? (show an actual ball or a picture- see below)
Retrieved from: https://www.fitball.com.au/product/opti-ball. If the earth is represented as a ball bearing, the sun should be as a large as an exercise ball.

Learners, you may have seen an earth-sun model similar to the one shown here but I think you will agree, this is not an accurate representation of the relative size of the earth and the sun. Retrieved from: https://www.eiscolabs.com/products/bd0075

How Far Apart are the Bodies in Our Solar System? Learners can be taken out in the school yard to map out the relative distance between the sun and all the planets. 1. Cut 10 strings each 3 meters in length. These strings each represent 500 x 10^6 km of distance (that is 500,000,000 km). Give a string to each group for their respective planet. 2. Refer to the following table. It shows the distances in our solar system. Share this with Learners and explain that ten to the power of six essentially means 6 zeros added to the number.
Celestial Body Distance from sun (x 10^6km) Mercury 57.9 Venus 108.2 Earth 149.6 Mars 227.9 Jupiter 778.3 Saturn 1427 Uranus 2873 Neptune 4498 Pluto 5914 Our Moon Distance from earth 0.382

3. With the teacher representing the sun, each celestial body group will use their string to measure how far their celestial body will be from the sun. Keep in mind the distance may be a fraction of the string or a multiple of the string length.

Teacher Examples to explain to Learners. ➤ The relative distance of Pluto from the sun is ~12 string lengths because 500 x 10^6km x 11 is nearly equivalent to 5914 x 10^6km. The student group representing Pluto would fold the string end for end 12 times and stand this distance from the sun. ➤ Similarly, Mercury is about 1/10 of a string length because 57.9/500 is about a tenth. Learners would estimate a tenth of their string length and stand that distance from the sun.

4. Were Learners surprised, the planets are not evenly spaced, some are very close in a group and others are very far away. Remind them that Pluto has recently been removed from the designation of planet. The outer planets are very cold because they are so distant from the sun. See distance activity video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wwz4Ff68Vt4 (1:45 mins)

Distances in Space Distances between the sun and planets in space are very large. They are so large that we would be placing many zeros after the number. For that reason, astronomers (those who investigate the stars) use an easier unit. Light from your flashlight (torch) travels very fast. If you turn on that light while in the dark and cast the beam at an object even across the street, it seems to instantly appear. In fact light travels at 300,000,000 metres per second; scientists use a notation that accounts for all of those zeros as a multiple of 10. 300,000,000 =3 x 10 x10 x 10 x 10 x 10 x 10 x 10 =3 x 10^8 m/s (note the exponent number above the ten which accounts for 8 multiples of 10 and the zeros) This short hand (3 x 10^8 m/s) is called scientific notation. The scientists then express distance as how far light travels in one year and call that a light year. If you calculate the number of seconds in a year and then multiply by the speed of light in (m/s) you will get the distance in metres. Let us do the math for the distance light travels in one year! 60 s/min x 60 mins/hr x 24 hr/day x 365 days/yr = 31,536,000 seconds/yr Teacher note: take this opportunity to show Learners how the units when multiplied and divided give the proper unit result. Avoid using the word cancel and instead say mins/mins = 1 > anything multiplied by one is itself! 3 x 10^8 m/s x 3.1536 x 10^7 seconds/yr = 9.5 x 10^15m/yr Point out that the exponents of 10 are additive here.

Interesting Facts: Video Resources Learners are given the following questions to consider as they watch the two videos. Which is the biggest planet in the solar system? (Jupiter) 2. Which is the smallest planet? (Mercury) 3. What is in the center of the solar system? (sun) 4. What is the main source of light in the solar system? (sun) 5. Which planet supports life? (Earth) 6. Which of the planets has the most moons around it? (Saturn) 7. How Do We Observe the Solar System? Learners, have you ever wondered how scientists are able to view the planets in outer space? One of the first tools for space exploration was the telescope. It gave scientists their first look at what exists beyond Earth’s atmosphere. Telescopes use lenses and mirrors to observe outer space.

The telescope is no longer an Earth-bound instrument. In 1990, the Hubble Space Telescope was sent into outer space. It’s now been there for over 30 years. It has sent back pictures of our universe that couldn’t have been taken in any other way.

Thanks to the Kepler telescope, launched in 2009, scientists now know that there are possibly over 3,000 planets orbiting more than 2,000 stars in the Milky Way galaxy.(see: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milky_Way)
Probes, such as NASA’s Cassini probe, have been sent to explore other planets.

Atmospheres of Planets Learners will have done the outdoor planetary distance activity but as a review the teacher can show this picture of the order of the planetary distances even if the relative distances are inaccurate. mole whereas oxygen (O2) is 32! Applying the same force, a cricket ball will go further on a humid day compared to a dry day because the atmosphere is less dense! inclusive_assessment_strategies: Multiple Choice Quiz: 1. What type of force is gravity? A) A force that pushes objects away from each other B) A force that pulls objects toward each other C) A force that only affects objects on Earth. Correct Answer: B) A force that pulls objects toward each other.

Multiple Choice Quiz: 2. Which statement best explains how gravity exerted by a body affects two objects with different masses? A) Gravity pulls equally on both objects, regardless of their masses. B) The object with more mass experiences a stronger gravitational pull. C) Gravity pushes objects with less mass away from the larger object. Correct Answer: B) The object with more mass experiences a stronger gravitational pull.

Multiple Choice Quiz: 3. Why do objects fall toward Earth when dropped? A) Earth's gravity pulls the objects toward its center. B) The objects are being pushed by air. C) The objects are too heavy to stay in the air. Correct Answer: A) Earth's gravity pulls the objects toward its center.

Multiple Choice Quiz: 4. Which of the following best supports the claim that the strength of gravity depends on the mass of the objects? A) A small rock and a large boulder both fall at the same speed. B) The Moon orbits around Earth because of Earth’s gravity. C) A person weighs more on Earth than on the Moon because Earth is more massive. Correct Answer: C) A person weighs more on Earth than on the Moon because Earth is more massive.

Multiple Choice Quiz: 5. What would happen if Earth’s mass decreased by half? A) The gravitational pull on objects would increase. B) The gravitational pull on objects would stay the same. C) The gravitational pull on objects would decrease. Correct Answer: C) The gravitational pull on objects would decrease.

Multiple Choice Quiz: 6. Which of the following is an example of gravitational interaction? A) The Moon orbiting around Earth. B) A person pushing a car. C) A balloon floating in the air. Correct Answer: A) The Moon orbiting around Earth.

7. Which of the following factors affects the strength of gravitational force between two objects? A) The distance between the objects B) The temperature of the objects C) The speed of the objects Correct Answer: A) The distance between the objects

8. Which of the following explains why astronauts float in space? A) There is no gravity in space. B) Gravity in space pushes them upward. C) They are in free-fall, but Earth’s gravity is still pulling them. Correct Answer: C) They are in free-fall, but Earth’s gravity is still pulling them.

9. What would happen to the gravitational force between two objects if the distance between them increased? A) The gravitational force would stay the same. B) The gravitational force would increase. C) The gravitational force would decrease. Correct Answer: C) The gravitational force would decrease.

10. Why is the Sun able to keep the planets in orbit around it? A) The Sun’s mass creates a strong gravitational force. B) The Sun’s heat pulls the planets toward it. C) The Sun’s light attracts the planets toward it. Correct Answer: A) The Sun’s mass creates a strong gravitational force.

Explaining the Tides on Earth Learners should be tasked with explaining why we have high tides on opposite sides of the earth. They can do internet research but their submission to the teacher should include a clear diagram with logical explanation. ***Learners should be encouraged to use a magnet model where strength of attraction and distance are the controlling variables. Learners may use the following website as a primary source for their assignment: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3RdkXs8BibE (5:10 mins)

Why Do Planets Orbit the Sun The sun is pulling on the orbiting planet with its gravity and you would expect the planet to fall to the sun’s surface just the way a ball tossed in the air on earth will certainly fall to the earth’s surface. But that doesn’t happen because the planet has momentum away from the surface of the sun. Think of a ball at the end of string that you swing around in a circle. As long as you hold the string, the ball will continue in a circle. The string represents gravity and the swinging motion that keeps the ball on its path represents the momentum the planet has trying to leave the sun. If we drop the string (i.e. remove sun’s gravity), the ball would continue on a straight path away from us. Learners should revisit the alternate explanation shown here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wwYegvdEJHg

One-page Response Paper Learners are expected to review the following internet sources and respond to the following questions. Resources: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DVF3rK6ML_8 (2:54 mins) https://www.nesdis.noaa.gov/news/why-dont-satellites-fall-out-of-the-sky Questions: 1. When communication satellites are launched into space- why don’t they simply fall back to earth? (Communication satellites stay in orbit because they balance the Earth’s gravitational pull with their forward motion, ensuring they continually “fall” around the Earth rather than back to its surface. The Earth’s gravitational pull, which constantly attracts satellites towards itself, acts as a tether pulling the satellite inward while the forward motion of the satellite at high speeds creates a force that counteracts gravity) 2. How do you get satellites to orbit at higher altitudes? (you give the satellite more initial velocity to get it further away from the earth before it begins to respond to the earth’s gravity and pull it into an orbit) 3. What is a geostationary satellite orbit and why might it help certain regions of the earth benefit from consistent communication? (If the satellite is rocketed to 36,000 kms, it will actually orbit at the same rate as the earth is rotating. This means the satellite will reside over the same region of the earth permanently- called a geostationary orbit) 4. What causes satellites to eventually fall to the earth’s surface? (Satellites may encounter increase density of atmosphere that causes drag on the angular velocity of the satellite. Because the ability of the

satellite to stay in orbit is so closely adjusted to its angular velocity at a horizontal to the earth, any change in that velocity can allow for gravity to take over the balance and pull the satellite to the ground)

Apollo 13- A Successful Failure
After an unexpected explosion on their spacecraft enroute to the moon, the crew onboard and at NASA devised a way to use the moon’s gravity to slingshot them back to earth. You can read the story here https://www.planetary.org/space-missions/apollo-13
Write a one-page newspaper article that responds to the following questions.
• What actually happened on the spacecraft?
• What impact did it have on the intended mission?
• What was the primary concern of the astronauts and NASA?
• How do you think the astronauts felt about their possible demise?
• What was the clever solution to get the astronauts back home safely?
• How does this experience demonstrate the ingenuity of humans to solve problems?
• Why is problem solving crucial in our education system?
inclusive_learning_strategies: Conversation/Discussion: Begin the class by taking Learners outside and giving each one a small toy. Ask them to throw their toy as far up into the air as possible on the count of three. Ask Learners these questions to facilitate discussion: 1. What happened to the toys? (they fell to the ground) 2. Why did the toys fall to the ground when released? Some will say it is because of something called gravity. 3. Is it a force that we can see? (no it is invisible) 4. Do things ever fall on an angle or only straight down? (straight down). The teacher can then restate that “gravity” is an invisible attractive force that pulls things downward towards the centre of the earth. Ask Learners to jump a few times. Then ask why they always fall back instead of floating off. (When you jump, you always come back down to the ground. This happens because Earth’s gravity pulls you back).

Ask Learners if they know of any other objects that exert gravity? (Expected responses: sun, moon, other planets). Ask the Learners if they think the moon has gravity of its own. Ask them to give evidence. (it pulls spaceships to the surface; it pulls on the water on the earth and affects our tides). Restate that it seems that planets and moons have their own gravity.

What Determines the Strength of Gravitational Pull? Ask Learners to look at the tables below and see if they can find any patterns that relate the mass of the planet and its gravity. (higher mass leads to higher gravity).

Surface Gravity of the Planets and the Sun Rank Name Surface Gravity (meter pr. square second) 1 Sun 274 2 Jupiter 24.92 3 Neptune 11.15 4 Saturn 10.44 5 Earth 9.798 6 Uranus 8.87 7 Venus 8.87 8 Mars 3.71 9 Mercury 3.7 10 Moon 1.62 11 Pluto 0.58 Source: NASA If we look at the sun or the planets with the greatest mass, they also have the greatest gravitational force. So then strength of gravity depends directly on mass of the object exerting the attracting force.

Video Review Have Learners write down the following questions before they watch the video below so you promote active listening. https://youtu.be/suQDwZcnJdg (3:01 mins) 1. What keeps us from floating off into space? (gravity) 2. Who discovered gravity? (Sir Isaac Newton) 3. What does the size of the gravitational pull depend on? (the mass of the object) 4. What happens because of the gravitational pull of the moon? (the tides)

Reinforcing the Variable Mass Using a Magnet Analogy Have Learners use magnets to investigate the effect of mass and distance on gravitational pull. https://arcscrap.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/shutterstock_1630046611.jpg Magnets exert a similar pull to that of gravity Place Learners in groups and provide each group with two magnets, one large and one small (respective magnets should be of the same size and strength) and magnetic objects of the same mass. Learners will investigate magnetic objects of the same mass placed at different distances away from the magnet. They experiment using the small magnet first then repeat using the large one. Learners record observations on a record sheet.

Investigating magnetic pull What do you think will happen to the object as it is moved further away from the magnet? Hypothesis: Distance Observation 2 cm 10 cm 20 cm 30 cm Conclusion As the Learners share their results, the teacher should attempt to inductively pull out two important features: 1. (The force of attraction is greatest, the closer the object is to the magnet and the weakest when the object is furthest away. 2. The force of attraction is greatest, the larger the magnet is.

The Sun’s Gravity The Sun has the largest mass in our planetary system and therefore possesses a very large gravitational force. This force is enough that the planets are held in orbits, the completion of which, take varying amounts of time. We

saw the period of planetary orbits in the last lesson (earth is 365 days).
Teacher note: The nature of orbits and the role of gravity are explained here in these video resources.
https://howthingsfly.si.edu/flight-dynamics/gravity-orbit
https://youtu.be/VfdBgUwn9RU (2:49 mins)
https://youtu.be/ffGDxGQpHb4 0:00 (2:05 mins)

The Moon’s Gravity
Ask the Learners what they know about the moon’s gravity. Ask them to give evidence. (it pulls spaceships to the surface; it pulls on the water on the earth and affects our tides)
Restate that planets and moons have their own gravity.
https://youtu.be/vwfbdPyzgDo (3:54 mins)
Learners look at this video of an astronaut walking on the moon. How does walking look different on the moon compared to the earth? (the astronaut appears to be bouncing while walking on the moon)
https://youtu.be/NHeOpJh5Q-M (0:30 mins)
Why does the astronaut seem to bounce on the moon’s surface?
When Learners look at the force of gravity on the moon compared to earth (1.62 versus 9.8 m/s² or 9.8 Newtons/Kg) they should be able to explain why astronauts tend to bounce across the moon’s surface compared to a walk on the earth. (the gravity on the moon is a fraction less than 1/5th of the earth’s gravity- the force you normally use to lift your foot when walking on earth would be 5 x what you need on the moon so your body would overcompensate)

The Moon and Tides
Earth has one moon which is kept in orbit by its gravitational pull. This moon is a natural satellite of Earth. Also, when the sun and moon line up around the earth, their combined gravity causes higher tides as the water is pulled away from the earth’s surface.
Retrieved from: https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-c75266afb398b22afc6531f0f12b05b6-lq
© Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.

Retrieved from: https://cdn.britannica.com/81/94581-050-531CAC1F/Effects-gravity-Earth-Moon.jpg
Teacher note: If Learners have difficulty with the orbit explanation above, return to the following analogy to help them with the concept of the moon’s orbit and also satellites that are placed in orbit around the earth.
See: https://howthingsfly.si.edu/flight-dynamics/gravity-orbit
How are mass and weight different ideas?
Our body has the same mass no matter where we are in the universe. However, because planets and moons have different gravitational pull, our weight is different depending on which of these planets we visited. When standing on a scale, a person’s weight is a measure of Earth’s gravitational pull on their mass. On planets with stronger or weaker gravity than Earth, a person’s weight would change even though their mass stays the same.
How Much Would We Weigh on Different Planets?
Gravity on Earth is 9.8 m/s²
The table below shows gravity on other planets.

Share the table with Learners. Walk through an example of how to calculate a person’s weight on another planet. Sample Calculations On Jupiter On Earth, a person weighs 100lbs or 45.4 kg How much will they weigh on Jupiter? Gravity on Jupiter is 24.9 m/s² This is a multiple of the gravity on Earth. How much greater is the gravity on Jupiter? 24.9/9.8 =2.54 times greater Therefore a 100 lb person x 2.54 = 254 lbs (~115 kg) On Mercury On Earth, a person weighs 100lbs or 45.4 kg How much will they weigh on Mercury? Gravity on Mercury is 3.7 m/s² This is a fraction of the gravity on Earth. How much less is the gravity on Mercury? 3.7/9.8 =0.378 times (a little over a third the weight) Therefore a 100 lb person x 0.378 = 37.8 lbs (~17 kg)

Based on these sample calculations, Learners are expected to complete the following table for 100 lb person. Planet Weight of 100 lb person Mars Neptune Venus Saturn Uranus Compare their results with the following: [Image: If you weigh 100 lb on Earth, you’d weigh... 17 lb on the moon, 38 lb on Mercury, 91 lb on Venus, 36 lb on Mars, 253 lb on Jupiter, 107 lb on Saturn, 91 lb on Uranus, 114 lb on Neptune] Retrieved from: https://spaceplace.nasa.gov/what-is-gravity/en/

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: ELO 1: Analyse and interpret data on the distribution of fossils and rocks, continental shapes, and seafloor structures to provide evidence of the past plate motions

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: complimentary, Pangaea, continental drift, core, mantle, crust, tectonic plates, convection currents, plate margins, ocean ridges, lithosphere, constructive or divergent shift, destructive or convergent shift, transform or conservative shift, subduction/subduction zones, fault line, tsunamis, volcanoes
Demonstrate they understand how fossil records support the idea of a single continent (Pangaea)
Compare different types of tectonic movement processes
Give examples of fossil records that support Pangaea’s existence.
Analyse media that demonstrate the separation of Pangaea into smaller continents.
Show with diagrams the three types of tectonic plate interactions.
Account for the formation of ocean ridges.
Account for the formation of folded mountains.
Account for the formation of volcanoes
Communicate your understanding of how earthquakes occur.
Construct models of tectonic plate interactions.
Calculate continental drift based on observed trends.
Investigate qualitative and quantitative ($) impacts of natural disasters such as earthquakes and tsunamis.
Practice the use of new vocabulary in project work.
Appreciate that continental drift happens over a very long period of time.
Appreciate that estimates of fossil age are based on scientific data but remain estimates.
Interest/Curiosity in how landforms are created especially in our own region.
Respect for Evidence of fossils
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
Participate actively in classroom discussions.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Student Response Paper: Have Learners on a single page, present the argument that there is scientific evidence that Pangaea existed and that the continents have since drifted apart. (fossils confirming the same living things lived on the boundaries of the past continent)

New Words Used Everywhere: Converge – means to come together. Sentence: The cars converged at the intersection. Diverge- means to move apart or depart from the normal. Sentence: The girl was a divergent thinker; she came up with innovative ideas outside of the box!
How are Volcanoes Formed?
Based on the models they have observed in class, have Learners explain the type of tectonic process they believe generates volcanoes.

Tsunamis: In a poster, student groups should include the following: What is a tsunami? An example of a tsunami that has occurred on earth. Show a map of where the tsunami occurred. Use a diagram to show the tectonic process that led to the tsunami. Describe the damage to the community that experienced the tsunami. How can the impact of tsunamis be reduced or prepared for?

Earthquakes: In a poster, student groups should include the following: What is an earthquake? An example of an earthquake that has occurred on earth. Show a map of where the earthquake occurred. Use a diagram to show the tectonic process that led to the earthquake. Describe the damage to the community that experienced the earthquake. How can the impact of earthquakes be reduced or prepared for?

Continental Drift: In class conversion problem for Learners. If the rate of increased continental separation of North America and Africa is estimated at 2.5 cm/yr, how long will it take for the separation to grow by 1 meter? 1 meter = 100 cm. 100cm /2.5 cm per yr= 40 years

Did You Know? The Mariana Trench in the Pacific Ocean is deeper that Mount Everest is tall! On 10 November 2020, the Chinese submersible Fendouzhe reached the bottom of the Mariana Trench at a depth of 10,909 m (35,791 ft; 5,965 fathoms).
Have Learners research the internet to discover how the trench was likely formed. Primary source: https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/news/planet-postcard-mariana-trench
inclusive_learning_strategies: Pangaea: The Forgotten Continent: Learners, let us look at a globe or map of the world. Have you ever noticed the shapes of the continents seem to be somehow complementary- that is, they could fit together like a jigsaw puzzle? Well, we wouldn’t be the first ones to notice that and for scientist many years ago it certainly made them curious about the history of the earth! Show learners a diagram of Pangaea, the supercontinent, and explain that it existed around 250 million years ago.

Explain that over millions of years, the tectonic plates beneath Earth’s surface slowly moved, causing Pangaea to break apart into the continents we see today. This process is called continental drift, proposed by Alfred Wegener in 1912. It is a widely held theory that can be accounted for using fossils records. Explain that Alfred Wegener gave the German name Urkontinent to a single land mass on the face of the earth in early geologic time. More often referred to now as Pangaea. Video resource: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QhldiOaFqpE (0:55 mins)
Proposed Timeline of Continental Drift
Retrieved from: https://geography.name/continental-drift/
Video Resource: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Wq9kLzm36h0 (1:21 mins)

How Did These Land Masses Move Apart? Pose the following questions to Learners. While they watch the video below, they should write down the answers to these questions they find in the video Teacher note: questions prior to a video promotes active listening/viewing! Video resource: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vxmoxDznXe4 (6:47 mins) Questions: 1. What are the three parts that make up the earth? (core, mantle, crust) 2. Which is the thinnest layer? (the crust) 3. What is the combination of the upper mantle and crust called? (lithosphere) 4. What are the pieces called that make up the divisions in the lithosphere? (tectonic plates) 5. What is the movement of plates over time called? (plate tectonics or continental drift)
How do we know that the continents were once joined? (Duplicate fossils have ben found on each side of the boundaries where the continents were once joined.)
What causes the plates to move? (convection currents in the mantle)
What are plate margins? (places where the tectonic plates meet)
What are three types of processes at the plate margins? (constructive-divergent, destructive-convergent &transform-conservative)
In what direction do plates move in a divergent tectonic shift? (plates move apart)
What do divergent processes create? (mid-Atlantic ridge)
One plate is forced up, while one is forced under (deep trenches)
Plates collide and are both forced upward. (fold mountains)
Describe the plate movement in a conservative tectonic shift? (plates slide along each other either opposed in direction or alongside each other at different speeds)
Transform or conservative tectonic shifts.
What types of earth events occur as a result of conservative tectonic shifts? Give an example. (Earthquakes along boundaries fault lines- San Andreas fault California)

San Andreas fault California; site of a conservative tectonic shift When tectonic plates from around the world interact in such a way that the oceanic plate (of higher density) is pushed underneath a continental plate (of lower density) this process is called subduction. The denser oceanic plate is forced down into the mantle where it is heated and essentially recycled. The region where this occurs is called a subduction zone.
Student Activity: Modelling Plate Movements
Station 1: Divergent Boundary Simulation Materials: Modelling clay, wax paper
Roll two long strips of clay (each representing a tectonic plate) and place them side by side on the wax paper.

Slowly move the clay strips apart to simulate a divergent boundary. As the plates pull apart, a gap forms between them. Explain that this process is similar to how new ocean floor is created at mid-ocean ridges.
Explanation: When plates move apart at divergent boundaries, magma rises from below to form new crust. Over time, this creates features like mid-ocean ridges.
Station 2: Convergent Boundary Simulation Materials: Two foam blocks or sponges
Place the two blocks at the edge of the wax paper.

Slowly push the blocks toward each other to simulate a convergent boundary. When they collide, one block should "subduct" (sink) under the other, demonstrating the subduction of one tectonic plate beneath another.

Explanation: At convergent boundaries, one plate is often forced beneath another, leading to the formation of deep ocean trenches and volcanic arcs. Alternatively, if two continental plates collide (without subduction), they form mountains, such as the Himalayas.
Station 3: Conservative or Transform Boundary Simulation Materials: Two cardboard strips
Cut two strips of cardboard and place them side by side on the wax paper. These strips represent tectonic plates at a transform boundary.
Gently slide the strips past each other in opposite directions to simulate a transform fault (like the San Andreas Fault).
Explanation: At transform boundaries, plates slide past each other, which causes intense friction. The release of built-up tension results in earthquakes.
Natural Disasters of Evidence of Plate Movements Show Learners videos of natural disasters that show evidence of plate tectonic movements.
Video on volcanoes Retrieved from https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=R_pDKyg5YKY
Video on earthquakes Retrieved from https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RqqqSnaTfQo
Video on tsunamis Retrieved from https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Wx9vPv-T51I&t=65s

Fossil Evidence for Continental Drift Distribute fossil record evidence diagrams: Provide diagrams showing where certain fossils of ancient plants and animals have been found. For example: Mesosaurus (a freshwater reptile) fossils found in South America and Africa.
Retrieved from https://www.busaccagallery.com/catalog.php?itemid=5231
Glossopteris (an extinct plant) fossils found in South America, Africa, Antarctica, and India.
Retrieved from https://www.alamy.com/stock-photo/glossopteris-fossil-plant.html?sortBy=relevant
Lystrosaurus (a land reptile) fossils found in Antarctica, Africa, and India.
Retrieved from https://carnivora.net/lystrosaurus-spp-t990.html#google_vignette

Discuss how fossils provide evidence of continental drifts. Explain that these organisms couldn't have crossed oceans. The fact that similar fossils are found on continents now separated by vast oceans suggests that these landmasses were once connected, supporting the idea of Pangaea and continental drift.
