essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: ELO 1: Develop a model to describe the cycling of water through Earth’s systems driven by energy from the sun and the force of gravity

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: water cycle, hydrologic cycle, water vapor, evaporation, precipitation, transpiration, sublimation, glaciers
Demonstrate they understand how heat energy is responsible for the changes of state of water.
Show how the energy impacts water particle motion.
Show how the energy impacts water particle spacing.
Account for how global warming could influence water resources by melting glaciers.
Explain how gravity brings water down from higher altitudes to sustain life at lower altitudes.
Observe the process of evaporation and account for the result.
Infer from experimentation that water molecules are not broken during boiling but instead converted to water vapor.
Conduct an experiment which demonstrates transpiration.
Communicate the importance of glaciers through a poster project.
Collect, tabulate and graph rainfall data.
Share rainfall data with a cooperating school.
Express the change of state of water using your hands to model the impact of temperature on water molecules.
Create a model of the water cycle.
Appreciate that our water supply on earth must be protected so as to serve living things.
Teachers should foster interest/curiosity in the water state change processes using dramatic analogies with ones hands.
Inquiry can be stimulated by working with other schools on rainfall data collection projects.
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
Participate actively in classroom discussions.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Recall Quiz
Multiple Choice Questions: 1. What is the process by which water turns into vapor and rises into the atmosphere? a) Precipitation b) Condensation c) Evaporation d) Runoff
2. What happens to water vapor when it cools down and turns back into liquid? a) Evaporation b) Condensation c) Transpiration d) Precipitation
3. What do we call the water that falls from clouds as rain, snow, sleet, or hail?
4. What part of the water cycle involves water soaking into the ground? a) Collection b) Infiltration c) Condensation d) Evaporation
5. Which process involves plants releasing water into the air? a) Evaporation b) Precipitation c) Transpiration d) Runoff
True or False: 6. Water can never be destroyed or created; it just changes form in the water cycle. a) True b) False
7. Condensation is the process of water turning into vapor. a) True b) False
Fill in the Blanks: 8. Water from lakes, rivers, and oceans turns into water vapor through the process of _______________.
The water cycle is a continuous movement of water between the Earth's surface and the ____________.
Precipitation can fall as rain, ____________, snow, or hail.
Short Answer Questions: 11. What is the difference between evaporation and condensation? 12. Why is the water cycle important for life on Earth?
Bonus Question: 13. Draw and label the four main stages of the water cycle. (Learners should include: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and collection.)
Answers: 1. c) Evaporation 2. b) Condensation 3. b) Precipitation 4. b) Infiltration 5. c) Transpiration 6. a) True 7. b) False 8. Evaporation 9. Atmosphere 10. Sleet
11. Evaporation is the process of water turning into vapor, while condensation is the process of vapor cooling and turning back into liquid.
12. The water cycle ensures that water is continuously renewed and distributed, providing fresh water for drinking, agriculture, and maintaining ecosystems.

One-page Response Paper: Learners should answer the following two questions on a single page. An explanation and drawn picture should be included. 1. What role does the sun play in the water cycle? (energy provided for water molecules to change state solid>liquid>gas/ transpiration in plants) 2. What role does gravity play in the water cycle? (water, ice and snow high in the bills is brought to lower elevations in rivers and streams)

What Impact Do Glaciers Have on the Water Cycle? Using the following website as a primary source, Learners in groups will create a poster that outlines the influence of the polar ice caps (glaciers) on the overall water cycle. Primary source: https://www.usgs.gov/special-topics/water-science-school/science/ice-snow-and-glaciers-and-water-cycle Rubric
Comprehensive coverage of the influence (5 marks)
Links to global warming and the sun’s energy that converts ice to liquid water. (5 marks)
Graphics/pictures (5 marks)
Spelling & punctuation (5 marks)
inclusive_learning_strategies: Disappearing water, what is the trick? The teacher sets a shallow pie pan of water out on the warm windowsill through the day and overnight. In the morning, the water has disappeared. Ask Learners, where did the water go? (up in the air). Do our ponds and lakes lose water sometimes? (Yes, they can dry up). Do they stay that way or is the water replaced? (Rainwater sometimes replaces the water that was lost from drying up). We call that a water cycle or hydrologic cycle of water loss and water gain. Learners, let us investigate this further.

Simulating Water Molecules: Ask Learners, why did the water molecules behave this way? Have the Learners stand up and pretend their fists are water molecules. Have them simulate cold liquid water molecules by moving their fists slowly back and forth and relatively close together. Then tell them they are adding energy and heating up the water - their fists move more quickly, a little further apart. Now tell them it is getting very warm, so they move their fists more quickly and much further apart. Explain that the water molecules now have enough energy to jump out of the liquid and become a gas we call water vapor. The water vapor is still H2O but is now in the gas form. Can we prove that the water has just changed state and not structure?

Teacher Demos: Cover a bowl of water with cellophane secured with an elastic band, string or tape. Place a coin on top of the cellophane over the middle of the bowl. Place the bowl assembly on a windowsill in the sun. What do we see after a few hours? (We see droplets on the underside of the cellophane). What are the droplets? (Liquid water molecules are slowing down as they cool and converting back from gaseous vapor to liquid water). So, we get back the water that once evaporated, it only changed state and not form, it is still H2O We call this conversion process from gas to liquid condensation. (see extensions of this experiment at: https://www.yumpu.com/en/document/view/11276437/experiment-1-mini-water-cycle-get-2-know-h2o-learning-zone)

Why does it rain? This is an example of the experiment above. When the water molecules get cold enough, they return from the vapor state to the liquid state. (Have Learners slow the motion of their fists and bring them closer together) These liquid water droplets then gather in clouds. When liquid water droplets form in cold clouds they begin to cling together, get heavy and fall to the ground. We call this rainfall or precipitation.

Review: As a review, have Learners watch the following video and relate it back to the experiments in class. The Water Cycle- How rain is formed-Lesson for kids Retrieved from: https://youtu.be/s0bS-SBAgJI (2:43 mins)

Other Processes That Cycle Water: Two other processes happen that make up the water cycle. Plants take in water from the ground through their root systems and they release water vapor into the air through their leaves via evaporation. The overall process is called transpiration.

Transpiration: Proof of Water Movement- A Teacher Demonstration: Fill a vase or jar with water and add a little food colouring. Place the flowers in the jar and leave for a few hours. Usually, you will see the flowers change colour within a few hours. Retrieved from: https://www.science-sparks.com/changing-colour-flowers-with-transpiration/

Another process can happen when water, in the form of ice, is heated but directly changes to water vapor and does not ever pass through the liquid state. This process is called sublimation, and it is common in the high altitudes or cold winter climates.

The teacher should use the following diagram to summarize the entire water cycle. Learners can draw their own version and label it as a review of the lesson. Retrieved from: https://www.khanacademy.org/science/biology/ecology/biogeochemical-cycles/a/the-water-cycle

Review Questions Related to Diagram: Ask Learners the following questions in a closure session making comprehensive reference to the above diagram 1. What are the water sources for the water cycle? (Rivers, lakes, wells, ponds, runoff from mountains.) 2. Where does the water get the energy to change from liquid to gas state (evaporation)? (sun’s energy) 3. Can water evaporate without the sun? (Yes but it is slow, it gathers heat from the surroundings to get enough energy to change state from liquid to gas) 4. Why does cooling in the atmosphere change the vapor back to liquid water? (the particles have less energy, slowdown their motion and get closer together-once they are water particles they cling together, get heavy and fall)
As a review, have Learners listen to the Water cycle song and pick out the familiar words associated with the process. Water Cycle Song Retrieved from: https://youtu.be/TWb4KlM2vts (3:41 mins)

How much water is available for users on earth? Have Learners set up a rain gauge at the school, collect weekly rainfall volumes and plot rainfall volume in ml versus the month in a histogram. Have the Learners linked with another school in a neighboring country or region (through relatives or a teacher connection) using social media to share rainfall data form another school. Do comparisons of the monthly data and account for differences in patterns (e.g. rainy seasons, prevailing winds, ocean currents etc.)
Creating a 3D model of the water cycle can be a fun and educational project for Learners to understand how water moves through Earth’s spheres (atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere,
and biosphere). Below is a step-by-step guide to create a simple yet effective 3D model using materials commonly found at home or in the classroom.
Materials Needed:
Large Styrofoam® or cardboard sphere (as the base)
Modeling clay or playdough
Blue cellophane or colored paper
Small plastic trees, plants, or figurines
Cotton balls or polyester fiberfill
Craft glue, tape, scissors
Markers or colored pencils
Optional: Toothpicks, thin wire, or string for creating connections
Steps to Create the 3D Water Cycle Model:
1. Prepare the Base Sphere: Use a large Styrofoam® or cardboard sphere as the base for your model. This represents the Earth.

2. Represent the Geosphere: Landforms: Use modeling clay or play-dough to create mountains, hills, valleys, and other landforms on the surface of the sphere. Labeling: Use markers or colored pencils to label these landforms directly on the sphere as "Geosphere."
3. Depict the Biosphere: Plants and Trees: Place small plastic trees, plants, or figurines on the landforms you created with clay. Labeling: Label these plants and trees as "Biosphere."
4. Create the Hydrosphere: Water Bodies: Use blue cellophane or colored paper to create rivers, lakes, and oceans on the surface of the sphere. Labeling: Label these water bodies as "Hydrosphere."
5. Represent the Atmosphere: Clouds and Sky: Use cotton balls or polyester fiberfill to create clouds around the sphere, representing the atmosphere. Labeling: Label this area as "Atmosphere."

6. Show Interactions: Arrows or Connections: Use toothpicks, thin wire, or string to create arrows between the different components (geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere) to show interactions. For example: Arrows from the atmosphere (clouds) to the biosphere (plants) to represent rain. Arrows from the hydrosphere (rivers) to the geosphere (soil) to show water infiltration.

7. Add Labels and Explanations: Use small pieces of paper or cardstock to write labels for each sphere (geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere). Write brief explanations of the interactions and attach them near the corresponding arrows or spheres.
Rubric for Assessment:

1. Model Accuracy and Detail: Excellent (4): Model accurately depicts the interactions between spheres with detailed and realistic features. Good (3): Model accurately depicts the interactions with some detail. Fair (2): Model depicts interactions with minimal detail and some inaccuracies. Needs Improvement (1): Model lacks accuracy and detail.

2. Comparison Analysis: Excellent (4): Comparison is thorough, well-explained, and supported by detailed examples. Good (3): Comparison is clear and supported by examples. Fair (2): Comparison is mentioned but lacks depth and supporting examples.
Needs Improvement (1): Comparison is unclear or missing.

3. Creativity and Presentation: Excellent (4): Model is highly creative, well-organized, and effectively presented. Good (3): Model is creative, organized, and clearly presented. Fair (2): Model shows some creativity and organization but is somewhat unclear. Needs Improvement (1): Model lacks creativity, organization, and clarity.

4. Collaboration and Participation (if group work): Excellent (4): All group members actively participated and collaborated effectively. Good (3): Most group members participated and collaborated effectively. Fair (2): Some group members participated and collaborated. Needs Improvement (1): Few group members participated or collaborated.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: ELO 2: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how the uneven distributions of Earth’s mineral, energy, and groundwater resources are the result of past and current geoscience processes.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Learners are expected to: Knowledge Define the terms: natural resources, energy, non-renewable, renewable resources, petroleum, fossil fuel, fracking, oil sands, minerals, coal, global warming, greenhouse effect, water conservation, ground water, surface water, hydropower dam, solar power, geothermal power, human rights & mining
Demonstrate they understand the difference between renewable and non-renewable resources.
Compare ways that electricity can be produced.
Develop awareness of the control oil-rich countries have over energy production worldwide.
Give examples of how alternate energies are more sensitive to protecting the environment.
Give examples of different ways to extract oil for energy purposes.
Use a picture to describe how fracking is used to extract oil.
Become aware of the environmental impacts of strip-mining practices.
Identify some dangers of underground mining.
Awareness of the scale coal use in China and the inherent impacts on global warming.
Explain potential environmental impacts of building large scale hydropower dams using the Three Gorges Dam in China as an example.
Establish which countries have the greatest mineral resources.
Filter internet information for objective facts and data on renewable and non-renewable resources in the world.
Infer from a graph, the extrapolated consumption of coal in China.
Classify resources as renewable or non-renewable.
Research information on oil extraction.
Research the process of geothermal energy production.
Debate who should take responsibility for safe mining practices.
Extract mining dangers from the lyrics of a song.
Research methods of water conservation.
Calculate/estimate short & long term costs of solar power for family homes.
Appreciate that fossil fuel resources are both limited and costly.
Appreciate the burning of fossil fuels impacts the environment.
Awareness of limited water supply.
Be inventive around conservation of water supply.
Be sensitive to the economics (salary) that balance the danger in a mining job or any risky occupation.
Possess interest/curiosity as to how geology of the Caribbean has impacted landforms and access to minerals and clean water.
Inventiveness around developing potential alternate energy sources.
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
Participate actively in classroom discussions.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Water Conservation is Important to the Caribbean The following verbatim excerpt outlines the status of water access in the Caribbean. Surface Water Caribbean Surface Water is a mixture of North Atlantic surface waters, Amazon River water, and local freshwater runoff from South America. Low presence of plankton is what makes Caribbean waters so famously crystal clear. Many of the islands predominantly use their surface water for their water supplies. The urbanisation of islands has affected higher peak flows, downstream flooding, and overall reduction in base stream flows. The effects of catchment conversion can be mostly seen in Haiti and its devastating floods. Such decrease in surface water quantity impacts water production.

Groundwater: The average annual rainfall affects the ability to of groundwater to recharge. Some islands make use of groundwater for public water supply, meanwhile the groundwater heavily depend on weather conditions to sustain itself. Recharge usually occurs in the peak wet season months, and it is key that the monthly rainfall is above the threshold of 195 mm monthly. Another important issue is to keep the groundwater from contamination with the proper management of sewage and waste systems to prevent high levels of nitrates flowing inland. (Retrieved from: https://irglobal.com/article/article-freshwater-situation-in-the-caribbean/)

After Learners have read this account of how the geology of the region impacts water access, task them with developing a poster that outlines how their region could preserve and recycle the water resource.

Primary Sources that Learners may find useful in the preparation of their posters: https://www.newterra.com/article/innovations-in-island-water-management-in-the-caribbean/, https://www.cardi.org/wp-content/uploads/2011/12/Sustainable-management-water-for-agri-in-Cbbean-P-Titus-2011-Brussels-Oct-Final-version.pdf, https://davidsuzuki.org/living-green/make-every-drop-count-water-conservation-tips/

Is Solar Power a Viable Alternative?- debate: Learners have studied some of the negative impacts of non-renewable resources such as fossil fuels. In many island nations, solar power seems to be gaining popularity. Learners should be tasked with investigating the cost of solar power; both initial and long-term financial implications for a typical home. They may choose to contact a local solar installation company to get itemized costs to support their study.

Primary sources: https://ases.org/caribbeanelectricalbills/, https://www.caribsurf.net/information/photovoltaic_systems.html, https://offgridinstaller.com/off-grid-solar-power-in-grenada-and-the-grenadines/, https://www.greentechmedia.com/articles/read/four-facts-you-should-know-about-the-caribbean-solar-market, https://www.ctc-n.org/sites/www.ctc-n.org/files/resources/solar-pv-in-the-caribbean.pdf

With an educated background, Learners should be placed in groups of 4 where two persons debate and argue the promise of solar power whereas the remaining two support the economics of continued use of fossil fuels for electricity production.

Geothermal Has Potential-one page response paper: Because of the volcanic activity in the Caribbean region, geothermal energy for electricity production is gaining popularity. Learners should conduct internet research and respond to the following questions. Using a picture and text, explain how geothermal energy is accessed for electricity production. Why is geothermal energy an attractive option for some Caribbean islands? Geothermal energy can be used for applications other than electricity production.

Primary Sources: https://www.energy.gov/eere/geothermal/electricity-generation, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mCRDf7QxjDk&t=11s (3:47 mins), https://oecs.int/en/geothermal-energy-in-the-eastern-caribbean

Mining Disasters: In years gone by and even present day, the conditions in underground coal mines can be dangerous. When humans make tunnels in the earth, it is very easy for those tunnels to disturb the natural rock strength and tunnels can collapse. Within the rock there is also pockets of methane gas which can easily ignite and cause an underground explosion.

The Springhill mining disaster in Canada resulted in a famous song. Learners can learn much about the culture of coal mining from the lyrics below. What do they learn from the song? Learners should be tasked with submitting answers to the following questions next class. a) What are the conditions experienced in a mine? b) What are the indicators of a problem in a mine? c) Why is it difficult to rescue miners? d) If you were an employed miner with a family and knew the risks what factors would you need to weigh in order to decide whether to continue with your job? Rubric: 5 marks for each question 5 marks overall for grammar & punctuation 5 marks for well-balanced rationale in question (d) Lyrics In the town of Springhill, Nova Scotia Down in the dark of the Cumberland Mine There’s blood on the coal and the miners lie in the roads that never saw sun nor sky Roads that never saw sun nor sky In the town of Springhill, you don’t sleep easy. Often the earth will tremble and roll.

When the earth is restless, miners die; Bone and blood is the price of coal. In the town of Springhill, Nova Scotia, Late in the year of fifty-eight, Day still comes and the sun still shines, But it’s dark as the grave in the Cumberland Mine. Down at the coal face, miners working, Rattle of the belt and the cutter’s blade, Rumble of the rock and the walls closed round The living and the dead men two miles down. Twelve men lay two miles from the pit shaft, Twelve men lay in the dark and sang Long hot days in the miners’ tomb It was three feet high and a hundred long. Three days passed and the lamps gave out And Caleb Rushton he up and said, “There’s no more water nor light nor bread So we’ll live on songs and hope instead.” Listen for the shouts of the barefaced miners, Listen thru the rubble for a rescue team, Six hundred feet of coal and slag; Hope imprisoned in a three-foot seam. Eight days passed and some were rescued, Leaving the dead to lie alone. Thru all their lives they dug their grave Two miles of earth for a marking stone. In the town of Springhill, you don’t sleep easy Often the earth will tremble and roll. When the earth is restless, miners die Bone and blood is the price of coal.

They can hear the song sung here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ebJFulmE TMA (3:13 mins) Mining Practices In many parts of the world, mining occurs deep underground in conditions that can be dangerous. Nonetheless, miners in need of a salary to support their families, will take potentially take a daily risk and return to the mines. Learners should debate (in teams) where the responsibility lies for protecting miners? Is it the mining companies, the government regulators or do the miners themselves make the choice to visit the mine in less than perfect conditions? Primary sources: https://www.csis.org/analysis/why- responsible-mining-human-rights-imperative https://www.bsr.org/en/primers/10- human-rights-priorities-for-the-extractives- sector
inclusive_learning_strategies: What are Natural Resources? Ask Learners what they think the term “natural resources” means. From the word natural they should come to suggest that it comes from nature and resource is something we have access to help us accomplish something. Provide them with a formal definition. Natural Resources: are things that are supplied or produced by nature.

Teacher’s Notes: Humans depend on Earth’s land, ocean, atmosphere, and biosphere for many different resources. Minerals, fresh water, and biosphere resources are limited, and many are not renewable or replaceable over human lifetimes. These resources are distributed unevenly around the planet as a result of past geologic processes. All human activity draws on natural resources and has both short- and long-term consequences, positive as well as negative, for the health of people and the natural environment. Resources are typically limited and renewable due to factors such as long amounts of time required for some resources to form or the environment in which resources were created forming once or only rarely in the Earth’s history.

Let’s Talk About Energy: Learners, many of you would have electricity delivered to your home to operate lights and appliances. You simply flick a switch or plug in an appliance but how is that energy produced? Maybe you have never thought of that before? (generators fueled by oil) If your country used generators, what happens when the oil runs out? Many countries buy oil from other countries and the price and access is controlled by that oil-producing country. That leaves your country very vulnerable from being cut-off from oil supply and therefore electricity! We would say that oil is non-renewable; when it runs out we can’t just find more, there is a limited quantity world-wide.

We do have other ways of creating electricity and our generation has become particularly innovative. I want you to write down three ways that you have read about that electricity can be generated. Solar power, Wind power, Hydropower, Geothermal power, Tidal power

What do you notice about these examples? Will the sun ever stop shining? (no), Will water ever stop flowing down hill due to gravity? (no), Will the wind ever stop blowing? (no), Will the earth ever stop having heat in its core? (no), Will the tides ever stop changing? (no)
Because we can be sure these resources will always be present, we would call these renewable resources.

These resources have certain characteristics that I would like to review with you in this table of comparison: Renewable: 1. Can be replaced by natural process in a short amount of time or can be recycled. 2. Can be reused or recycled and used multiple times. 3. Some of the examples are: wind energy, solar power, hydroelectricity, geo thermal. 4. No harm done to the environment because of its use. Non Renewable: 1. These are natural resource that either cannot be replaced or may take millions of years to replace by natural process like coal and oil. 2. Cannot be reused or recycled. 3. Some of the examples are: Petrol, coal, Natural gas, nuclear energy, fossil fuels. 4. Huge harm done to the environment because of the harmful emissions.
Retrieved from: https://www.etutorworld.com/7th-grade-science-worksheets/renewable-and-non-renewable-energy-resources.html
Since oil is a common source of fuel for electricity worldwide, it is worth spending more time investigating it.

Oil Industry: Petroleum is referred to as a fossil fuel because it is formed when large quantities of dead organisms, (primarily zooplankton and algae), are buried beneath sedimentary rock and subjected to both intense heat and pressure. The oil deposits are spread across the world in different locations but the 5 top producers are: United States, Saudi Arabia, Russia, Canada and China (see: https://www.investopedia.com/investing/worlds-top-oil-producers/)

Humankind are large consumers of oil for energy and therefore they are constantly looking for new sources and ways to access more oil. With the best of intentions, humans in interacting with natural resources can often cause problems.

Learners should be asked to research and respond to 3 questions in an essay format regarding oil consumption. 1. Humans have accessed oil under the sea. Give an example of how accessing that source has caused environmental problems. (teacher source: https://www.britannica.com/event/Deepwater-Horizon-oil-spill), 2. “Fracking” is a process for accessing oil that has been considered in North America. What is fracking and how does this impact the environment? (teacher source: https://serc.carleton.edu/NAGTWorkshops/health/case_studies/hydrofracking_w.html), 3. Canada has sought to access crude oil from what are called “oil sands”. What aspects of this process pose dangers to our environment? Learners should consider how transportation of petroleum is a factor here. (teacher sources: https://www.capp.ca/oil/what-are-the-oil-sands/, https://www.watershedcouncil.org/pipeline-risks.html, https://www.history.com/topics/1980s/exxon-valdez-oil-spill)
Rubric: Comprehensive review of questions (5 marks)

Unbiased reporting of facts (2 marks) Clear & logical arguments (5 marks) Spelling and grammar (5 marks) Accessing Mineral Resources Sources of energy like coal and valuable minerals for (e.g. gold & silver) have caused humans to dig in areas around the world affecting the geology of the natural landscape. Show Learners the picture below of open-pit mining and ask them what the impact might be on the ecosystem (plants, animals, humans). Retrieved from: 10 deepest open mines in the world here: https://www.mining-technology.com/features/feature-top-ten- deepest-open-pit-mines-world/ The Impact of Coal Mining on the Environment Unfortunately, coal burning for energy produces high levels of emissions especially in heavily industrialized countries like China. In 2018, coal made up 59 percent of China's energy use. Since 2011, China has consumed more coal than the rest of the world

combined. China's industrial sector is by far the largest consumer of coal. Have Learners review the graph below and determine The increase in Chinas’ consumption between 2005 and 2010 (~ 1billion tons) Retrieved from: https://www.worldometers.info/coal/china- coal/ North of Shanghai, the temperatures are such in the winter that coal burning for heat is common in households. This contributes to high levels of emissions in cities like Beijing. This contributes to the problem of global warming. Global warming occurs when carbon dioxide (CO2) and other air pollutants and greenhouse gases collect in the atmosphere and absorb sunlight and solar radiation that have bounced off the earth’s surface. Normally, this radiation would escape into space—but these pollutants, which can last for years to centuries in the atmosphere, trap the heat and cause the planet to get hotter. Commonly known as the greenhouse effect.

Excerpt from: https://www.nrdc.org/stories/global-warming- 101 Coal Burning Replaced by Hydropower? The Chinese have tried to curb their need for coal as power source by creating the largest hydropower dam in the world. This is another example of how humans have sought to capitalize on natural resources with some unwanted results. Have Learners research this major Chinese project (the Three Gorges Dam- https://www.britannica.com/topic/Three-Gorges-Dam) and report back to class the potential impact on local habitat. (diversion of water sources-agricultural impacts, displacement of peoples from indigenous lands, displacement of natural species from water ways and land) Gold Mining in Guyana In the interior of Guyana in South America, gold mining is undertaken by locals using huge water pumps to flush sand in pools and filter for gold. It is also common for these junior miners to use mercury as a way of binding out the gold. Ask Learners to research the dangers of using mercury in the mining process. (they should look at the impact of mercury on water sources)
Photograph Taken Above Region Near Mahdia, Guyana

Comprehension Activity Learners should be asked to read the following passage and summarize the negative effects of mining. "While there have been improvements to mining practices in recent years, significant environmental risks remain. Negative impacts can vary from the sedimentation caused by poorly built roads during exploration through to the sediment, and disturbance of water during mine construction. Water pollution from mine waste rock and tailings may need to be managed for decades, if not centuries, after closure. These impacts depend on a variety of factors, such as the sensitivity of local terrain, the composition of minerals being mined, the type of technology employed, the skill, knowledge and environmental commitment of the company, and finally, our ability to monitor and enforce compliance with environmental regulations. One of the problems is that mining has become more mechanized and therefore able to handle more rock and ore material than ever before. Therefore, mine waste has multiplied enormously. As mine technologies are developed to make it more profitable to mine low-grade ore, even more waste will be generated in the future." Quoted directly from: https://www.safewater.org/fact-sheets-1/2017/1/23/miningandwaterpollution

Distribution of Mineral Resources The Caribbean region has been shaped by a number of tectonic plate interactions (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z72DaFPMGwo) that has contributed to the formation of volcanic and coral islands. This geology has inherently led to a limited number of mineral and fossil fuel resources many of which have been exhausted. Wood, coal and oil fuels traditionally used for heating and cooking tends to be located in other parts of the world. This means the Caribbean is heavily reliant on South America for some of these necessities. The interaction of the Caribbean tectonic plate with neighboring plates has impacted the mineral deposits in the region. "In the distant past, as North and South American plates rifted apart, metal deposits began to accumulate in the rift basins, which were later exposed, as a result of subduction on the eastern edge of the Caribbean Plate. Within the Caribbean, some of the most important deposits of bauxite and lateritic nickel, as well as significant deposits of gold and silver, copper, zinc, manganese, cobalt, and chromium, are hosted in the Greater Antilles. Massive sulphide deposits formed in fore-arc basins as well as back-arc basins due to volcanic activity, also giving rise to porphyry copper and precious metal deposits in the more mature island arc. The various tectonic features listed above all formed as a result of rifting and subsequent subduction and collision in the Caribbean region. This provides a perfect setting for initial deposition followed by uplift along with weathering to yield a variety of deposits." (Retrieved verbatim from: https://caribbeantectonics.weebly.com/resources-in-the-caribbean.html primary source: Nelson, C. E., Proenza, J. A., Lewis, J. F., & López-Kramer, J. (2011). The metallogenic evolution of the Greater Antilles. Geological Act: an international earth science journal, 9(3-4), 229-264.

Largest Mineral Deposits in the World The majority of mineral deposits seem to reside on large continental plates rather than the subduction zones that populate the Caribbean region. Country Deposits Australia Largest producer bauxite, iron ore and lithium/ a top global producer of coal, aluminum, copper, gold, manganese, nickel, silver, uranium, and zinc. Chile copper, lithium China Rare earth elements, salt, cement, phosphate, nitrogen and potash. Coal, gold, antimony, magnesium, tin, zinc, manganese and tungsten. It leads in barite, fluorspar, graphite, molybdenum, phosphate rock, strontium, and lead reserves as well. Russia Gold, iron ore, aluminum, nickel, copper. It is among the top three nickel, platinum and palladium producers as well. Canada It ranks among the top five worldwide producers of potash, uranium, cobalt, aluminum, tungsten, platinum group metals, salt, titanium concentrate, and diamonds. Canada’s immense geology hosts abundant deposits of gold, silver, nickel, copper, cobalt, graphite, lithium, rare earths Congo 70% of global cobalt reserves, boasts rich diamond deposits and has bountiful reserves of copper, tin, tantalum, tungsten and gold. Brazil World’s largest niobium, third-largest bauxite and third-highest iron ore reserves globally. It ranks as a top producer of gold, tin, lithium, nickel, United States Leading producer of copper, gold, molybdenum, phosphate rock, rare earths, salt, soda ash, zeolites and zinc
Data retrieved from: https://www.cruxinvestor.com/posts/top-mining-countries-in-the-world-2024
